topics.title: Introduction to Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Cloud Computing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (NIST Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Official NIST Definition (SP 800-145):** > *"Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."* > — **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), USA** This single definition is the **gold standard** referenced by virtually all KTU exam paper setters for the term "cloud computing." Memorize it verbatim, as it directly answers 3-mark Part A questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electricity Grid" Model > [!NOTE] > **Think of Cloud Computing exactly like your home Electricity Supply (KSEB).** > - You do **not** own a power plant in your backyard. > - You **do not** worry about how the electricity is generated (coal, solar, hydro). > - You simply **pay for what you consume** (units on the bill). > - The supply is **always available**, **scales up** when you switch on an AC, and **scales down** when you turn it off. > - The **meter** measures your usage — you pay only for that. In the *exact same way*, **Cloud Computing** delivers: - **Compute** (like CPU power) = Electricity - **Storage** (like hard disks) = Storage capacity - **Network** (like bandwidth) = Wiring - **Pay-per-use** billing = Electricity bill - **Automatic scaling** = Appliances turning on/off Before cloud, companies had to **buy and maintain** their own "power plants" (servers, data centers). With cloud, they just **plug into a global utility** and pay for what they use. ### 1.3 The Five Essential Characteristics (NIST Mandatory) > [!IMPORTANT] > These **FIVE characteristics** are MANDATORY for any system to be called "Cloud Computing." Missing even one means it is NOT cloud — it is just **virtualization, grid computing, or utility computing**. | # | Characteristic | Simple Meaning | Engineering Analogy | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **On-demand Self-Service** | User provisions resources automatically without human interaction | ATM withdrawing cash 24/7 without a bank teller | | 2 | **Broad Network Access** | Available over the internet via standard devices (mobile, laptop, tablet) | Electricity socket works for any appliance | | 3 | **Resource Pooling** | Multi-tenant — physical resources serve many users virtually isolated | A single KSEB grid powers millions of homes | | 4 | **Rapid Elasticity** | Scale up/down quickly, often automatically | Water tap — turn it, flow adjusts instantly | | 5 | **Measured Service** | Usage is monitored, controlled, reported, and billed transparently | Postpaid mobile bill showing every call/SMS | **Bolded constants/metrics to remember:** SLA uptime is typically **99.9% (three nines)** as a baseline industry standard. ### 1.4 Why Cloud Computing Matters (Historical Context) Before cloud, organizations faced three brutal problems: 1. **Over-provisioning** — buying extra capacity "just in case" (wasted money). 2. **Under-provisioning** — running out of capacity during traffic spikes (lost customers). 3. **Long procurement cycles** — buying a new server took 4–8 weeks of approval. Cloud eliminates all three by turning **CAPEX (Capital Expenditure)** into **OPEX (Operational Expenditure)** — you pay monthly like a utility bill instead of buying hardware outright. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Layered Architecture of Cloud Computing Cloud Computing is conventionally explained in **three distinct conceptual layers** that KTU examiners LOVE to draw as a pyramid: ``` ┌─────────────────────────┐ │ SaaS │ ← Application Layer │ (Software as a Service)│ (User-facing) ├─────────────────────────┤ │ PaaS │ ← Platform Layer │ (Platform as a Service)│ (Developer-facing) ├─────────────────────────┤ │ IaaS │ ← Infrastructure Layer │(Infrastructure as Service)│ (Admin-facing) └─────────────────────────┘ ``` > [!NOTE] > **The "Pizza as a Service" Analogy (Extremely Popular in KTU Exams):** > - **On-Premise** = Making pizza at home from raw flour (you do EVERYTHING). > - **IaaS** = You buy a baked pizza base and add toppings at home (vendor provides infrastructure, you manage OS/apps). > - **PaaS** = You call a pizza place, they deliver a half-baked pizza; you just add toppings and bake (vendor provides platform, you build apps). > - **SaaS** = You order a fully cooked pizza delivered to your door (you just eat — vendor does everything). ### 2.2 The Three Service Models — Deep Dive #### A. Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) - **What you get:** Virtual machines, storage, networks, firewalls. - **What you manage:** Operating system, middleware, runtime, applications, data. - **What vendor manages:** Physical hardware, hypervisor, networking, datacenter. - **Examples:** **Amazon EC2**, Microsoft Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine (GCE), DigitalOcean. - **KTU Mnemonic:** *"IaaS = 'I' (you) Administer the System."* #### B. Platform as a Service (PaaS) - **What you get:** A ready development platform — OS, middleware, runtime, database, dev tools. - **What you manage:** Only your **application code and data**. - **What vendor manages:** Everything below your code. - **Examples:** Google App Engine, Microsoft Azure App Service, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk. - **KTU Mnemonic:** *"PaaS = 'P' for 'Provide Platform' — you just bring your Program."* #### C. Software as a Service (SaaS) - **What you get:** Fully working software over the internet (browser/ thin client). - **What you manage:** Only user-level configuration; you don't install anything. - **What vendor manages:** 100% of the stack. - **Examples:** **Gmail**, Microsoft 365, Google Docs, Dropbox, Salesforce, Zoom. - **KTU Mnemonic:** *"SaaS = 'S' for 'Simply use the Software'."* ### 2.3 The Four Deployment Models | Model | Owner | Users | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Public Cloud** | Third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) | General public | Startups, web apps, testing | | **Private Cloud** | Single organization | Organization only | Banks, defense, hospitals | | **Hybrid Cloud** | Mix of public + private with orchestration | Organization + public burst | Enterprises with compliance needs | | **Community Cloud** | Several organizations with shared concerns | Specific community (govt agencies, universities) | E-Governance, research consortiums | ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Critical Equations for Numerical Problems > [!IMPORTANT] > Cloud Computing has very few "math" formulas, but the ones below are **high-yield** for the 14-mark Part B questions. | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Downtime per year** | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ | $D$ = minutes of downtime/year; $A$ = availability as decimal | | 2 | **Annual Cloud Cost (Pay-as-you-go)** | $C_{cloud} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i \times U_i \times T_i)$ | $R_i$ = rate per resource, $U_i$ = units used, $T_i$ = time | | 3 | **TCO (Traditional)** | $C_{trad} = C_{hw} + C_{power} + C_{cool} + C_{admin} + C_{space}$ | Sum of all 5-year running costs | | 4 | **Cost Savings %** | $S\% = \dfrac{C_{trad} - C_{cloud}}{C_{trad}} \times 100$ | Percentage savings by moving to cloud | | 5 | **Elasticity Factor** | $E = \dfrac{C_{peak}}{C_{avg}}$ | Ratio of peak capacity to average load | | 6 | **Storage Conversion** | $1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB} = 2^{40} \text{ bytes}$ | Use $2^{10}$ for binary multiples | > [!WARNING] > **NEVER use the pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables.** For absolute value or "such that" in formulas, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — for example, write $\vert x \vert$ not `|x|`. This is a hard requirement for table integrity. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace & ISRO**: Uses cloud to process satellite imagery at scale without owning supercomputers. - **Healthcare (Kerala e-Health)**: Cloud-based EHR (Electronic Health Records) enables doctors to access patient history across hospitals. - **Banking (SBI YONO)**: Hybrid cloud allows core banking to stay on private cloud while mobile app scales on public cloud. - **Startups (Kerala KSUM)**: Zero upfront cost allows engineering students to deploy apps globally from a college laptop. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — SLA Availability vs Downtime This is the **most asked numerical** in KTU Cloud Computing papers. The examiner typically gives an SLA percentage and asks for downtime per year/month. **Theoretical Foundation:** The availability $A$ is defined as the fraction of time a system is operational over a fixed period. $$A = \frac{Uptime}{Total\ Time} = \frac{Total\ Time - Downtime}{Total\ Time}$$ Rearranging for Downtime: $$Downtime = Total\ Time \times (1 - A)$$ **Step-by-step for one full year (365 days):** $$D_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - A)\ \text{minutes}$$ **Step-by-step for one month (30 days):** $$D_{month} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - A)\ \text{minutes}$$ **Numerical Worked Example:** A cloud provider guarantees **99.95%** availability. Calculate the maximum allowed downtime per year. **Step 1 — Identify given values:** $$A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$$ **Step 2 — Apply the formula:** $$D = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.9995)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the multiplier:** $$365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525{,}600\ \text{minutes/year}$$ **Step 4 — Subtract from 1:** $$1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$ **Step 5 — Final multiplication:** $$D = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005 = 262.8\ \text{minutes} \approx 4\ \text{hours}\ 22\ \text{minutes}\ 48\ \text{seconds per year}$$ **Reference Table for instant recall in exams:** | SLA Level | "Nines" | Max Downtime / Year | Real-World Equivalent | |---|---|---|---| | 99.0% | Two nines | 3 days, 15 hours | Barely acceptable | | 99.9% | Three nines | 8 hours, 45 minutes | Standard cloud SLA | | 99.95% | Three-and-half nines | 4 hours, 22 minutes | Premium tier | | 99.99% | Four nines | 52 minutes, 33 seconds | Mission-critical | | 99.999% | Five nines | 5 minutes, 15 seconds | Telecom-grade | --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Comparison A 14-mark Part B question often asks: *"Compare CAPEX vs OPEX for a small company's web server over 3 years."* **Given scenario:** - Traditional server: Hardware = **₹5,00,000**, Power/Cooling = **₹2,000/month**, Admin salary allocation = **₹8,000/month**, Datacenter rent = **₹3,000/month**. - Cloud alternative: A VM of equivalent capacity costs **₹0.50/hour** running 24/7. **Step 1 — Traditional 3-year TCO:** $$C_{hw} = ₹5{,}00{,}000\ \text{(one-time)}$$ $$C_{power} = ₹2{,}000 \times 36\ \text{months} = ₹72{,}000$$ $$C_{admin} = ₹8{,}000 \times 36\ \text{months} = ₹2{,}88{,}000$$ $$C_{space} = ₹3{,}000 \times 36\ \text{months} = ₹1{,}08{,}000$$ $$C_{trad} = 5{,}00{,}000 + 72{,}000 + 2{,}88{,}000 + 1{,}08{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Summation:** $$\begin{aligned} C_{trad} &= ₹5{,}00{,}000 \\ &\quad + ₹72{,}000 \\ &\quad + ₹2{,}88{,}000 \\ &\quad + ₹1{,}08{,}000 \\ &= ₹9{,}68{,}000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Cloud 3-year cost:** $$C_{cloud} = ₹0.50 \times 24 \times 365 \times 3$$ **Step 4 — Compute hours over 3 years:** $$24 \times 365 \times 3 = 26{,}280\ \text{hours}$$ **Step 5 — Final cloud cost:** $$C_{cloud} = 0.50 \times 26{,}280 = ₹13{,}140$$ **Step 6 — Cost savings percentage:** $$S\% = \frac{C_{trad} - C_{cloud}}{C_{trad}} \times 100$$ $$S\% = \frac{9{,}68{,}000 - 13{,}140}{9{,}68{,}000} \times 100$$ $$S\% = \frac{9{,}54{,}860}{9{,}68{,}000} \times 100 \approx 98.64\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Cloud is **~98% cheaper** for this scenario, BUT this is a *peak-load* cloud scenario. If the server is only used 10% of the time, traditional is cheaper. **Elasticity** is what makes cloud cost-effective — you only pay for active hours. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Elasticity Calculation **Scenario:** A website has average traffic of **100 users**, but spikes to **1000 users** during exam result days (occurs **5 days per year**). The peak capacity needed is **1000 servers**, average is **100 servers**. **Traditional approach** (provision for peak): $$C_{trad} = 1000 \times R \times 8760\ \text{hours/year}$$ **Cloud approach** (auto-scale): $$C_{cloud} = (100 \times 8760) + (900 \times 24 \times 5)$$ $$C_{cloud} = 876{,}000R + 108{,}000R = 984{,}000R$$ **Savings:** $$S\% = \frac{1{,}000 \times 8760 - 984{,}000}{1{,}000 \times 8760} \times 100 \approx 98.77\%$$ This justifies the **Elasticity** characteristic numerically — a perfect 14-mark answer material. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram A — Evolution of Computing Paradigms ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Mainframe Era
1960s-1980s] --> B[Personal Computer
1980s-1990s] B --> C[Client-Server
1990s-2000s] C --> D[Grid Computing
Early 2000s] D --> E[Virtualization
Mid 2000s] E --> F[Cloud Computing
2006 onwards] F --> G[Edge & Fog Computing
2020 onwards] G --> H[Serverless & AI Cloud
Future] ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each paradigm built on the limitations of its predecessor. Cloud emerged because **virtualization** made it possible to "rent" slices of physical machines. Now we are moving to **edge** to reduce latency by processing data closer to the user. --- ### 4.2 Diagram B — Service Model Stack with Responsibility Boundaries ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UserLayer["USER MANAGES"] U1[Applications] U2[Data] end subgraph VendLayer["VENDOR MANAGES"] V1[Runtime] V2[Middleware] V3[Operating System] V4[Virtualization] V5[Servers] V6[Storage] V7[Networking] end U1 --> V1 U2 --> V1 ``` **Responsibility Matrix (Critical for 14-mark questions):** | Layer | On-Premise | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | |---|---|---|---|---| | Application | You | You | You | Vendor | | Data | You | You | You | Vendor | | Runtime | You | You | Vendor | Vendor | | Middleware | You | You | Vendor | Vendor | | OS | You | You | Vendor | Vendor | | Virtualization | You | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor | | Servers/Storage/Network | You | Vendor | Vendor | Vendor | --- ### 4.3 Diagram C — Cloud Deployment Models (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PublicZone["PUBLIC CLOUD - Open to General Public"] P1[AWS] P2[Azure] P3[GCP] end subgraph PrivateZone["PRIVATE CLOUD - Single Organization"] PR1[Internal Datacenter] PR2[VMware OpenStack] end subgraph HybridZone["HYBRID CLOUD - Bridged via VPN / API"] H1[Orchestration Layer] H2[Burst to Public on Demand] end subgraph CommunityZone["COMMUNITY CLOUD - Shared by Group"] C1[Government Agencies] C2[Universities Consortium] end PublicZone <--> HybridZone PrivateZone <--> HybridZone CommunityZone <--> HybridZone ``` --- ### 4.4 Diagram D — Functional Block Architecture of a Cloud Platform ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FrontEnd["FRONT END - User Side"] F1[Web Browser] F2[Mobile App] F3[CLI Tools] end subgraph BackEnd["BACK END - Cloud Provider Side"] B1[Authentication Service] B2[Resource Scheduler] B3[Hypervisor Pool] B4[Storage Array] B5[Billing Meter] end subgraph Connectivity["CONNECTIVITY LAYER"] N1[Internet / VPN] N2[API Gateway] N3[CDN Edge] end F1 --> N1 F2 --> N1 F3 --> N1 N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 N3 --> B1 B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 B2 --> B4 B2 --> B5 ``` **Engineering Interpretation:** The front-end issues requests; the connectivity layer routes, caches, and secures them; the back-end allocates resources and meters usage. This is the **functional architecture** of every public cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define cloud computing as per the NIST standard. List any three of its essential characteristics.** **Model Answer:** > **Definition (2 marks):** Cloud Computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. *(NIST SP 800-145)* > **Any three essential characteristics (1 mark):** > 1. On-demand self-service > 2. Broad network access > 3. Resource pooling > 4. Rapid elasticity > 5. Measured service --- #### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS | |---|---|---|---| | Full form | Infrastructure as a Service | Platform as a Service | Software as a Service | | What you manage | OS, apps, data | Apps and data only | Nothing (just use it) | | Vendor provides | Virtual machines, storage | Runtime, middleware, OS | Fully working application | | Example | Amazon EC2 | Google App Engine | Gmail | | Target user | System administrators | Application developers | End users | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) #### Question A [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a) Explain in detail the five essential characteristics of cloud computing as defined by NIST. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in its Special Publication 800-145 defines **five mandatory characteristics** that any service must possess to be classified as cloud computing: **1. On-Demand Self-Service (1.5 marks):** A consumer can unilaterally provision computing capabilities such as server time and network storage automatically, **without requiring human interaction** with each service provider. Example: A user logging into AWS console and launching a VM in under 2 minutes without filing a support ticket. **2. Broad Network Access (1.5 marks):** Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through **standard mechanisms** used by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms (mobile phones, tablets, laptops, workstations). It is not tied to a specific device or location. **3. Resource Pooling (1.5 marks):** The provider's computing resources are **pooled to serve multiple consumers** using a multi-tenant model, with physical and virtual resources dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand. There is a sense of **location independence** — the user generally has no control or knowledge over the exact location of the resources. **4. Rapid Elasticity (1.5 marks):** Capabilities can be **elastically provisioned and released**, in some cases automatically, to scale rapidly outward and inward commensurate with demand. To the consumer, the provisioning often appears **unlimited** and can be appropriated in any quantity at any time. **5. Measured Service (1 mark):** Cloud systems automatically **control and optimize resource use** by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the type of service (e.g., storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts). Resource usage can be monitored, controlled, and reported, providing transparency for both provider and consumer. --- **(b) Discuss the different cloud deployment models with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Cloud deployment models describe **who owns, manages, and accesses** the cloud infrastructure. There are four recognized models: **1. Public Cloud (2 marks):** The infrastructure is **owned by a third-party cloud provider** (such as AWS, Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud) and made available to the general public over the internet. It is the most cost-effective model due to economies of scale but offers less control and customization. Ideal for startups, web applications, and non-sensitive workloads. **2. Private Cloud (2 marks):** The infrastructure is **provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization**. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization itself, a third party, or a combination, and may exist on or off premises. It offers **greater control, security, and customization**, and is preferred by banks, defense establishments, and healthcare providers handling sensitive data. **3. Hybrid Cloud (1.5 marks):** This is a **composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private, public, or community) that remain unique entities but are **bound together by standardized or proprietary technology** enabling data and application portability. It allows organizations to keep sensitive workloads on a private cloud while bursting into a public cloud for peak loads. **4. Community Cloud (1.5 marks):** The infrastructure is provisioned for **exclusive use by a specific community of consumers** from organizations that have shared concerns (e.g., mission, security requirements, policy, compliance considerations). Example: A government cloud shared by multiple state departments, or a research cloud shared by IITs and NITs. > [!NOTE] > *(Add the deployment model Mermaid diagram from Section 4.3 in your exam answer for full 7 marks.)* --- #### Question B [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper] **(a) Compare and contrast IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with real-world examples. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Cloud Computing services are broadly classified into **three service models** based on the level of abstraction and who manages what: **Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) — 2.5 marks:** - **Concept:** Provides fundamental computing resources — **processing, storage, networks** — as virtualized services on-demand. The consumer can deploy arbitrary software (OS, applications) but does not manage the underlying cloud infrastructure. - **Provider gives:** Virtual machines, virtual storage, virtual networks, raw compute. - **User manages:** Operating system, middleware, runtime, applications, data. - **Real-world examples:** **Amazon EC2**, Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets, IBM Cloud. - **Use case:** Hosting enterprise websites, big data analytics, disaster recovery, development/test environments. **Platform as a Service (PaaS) — 2.5 marks:** - **Concept:** Provides a **ready-to-use platform** on which the consumer can develop, run, and manage applications without dealing with the complexity of building and maintaining the underlying infrastructure. - **Provider gives:** OS, middleware, runtime, development tools, database management, business analytics. - **User manages:** Only the application code and data. - **Real-world examples:** **Google App Engine**, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Microsoft Azure App Services, Red Hat OpenShift. - **Use case:** API development, IoT backends, SaaS application prototyping, machine learning model deployment. **Software as a Service (SaaS) — 2 marks:** - **Concept:** Provides **completed applications** running on the cloud infrastructure, accessible via thin clients (browsers, mobile apps). The user does not manage the network, servers, OS, storage, or even individual application capabilities (except limited user-specific configuration). - **Provider gives:** Fully functional, ready-to-use software. - **User manages:** Only user-level data and minor configurations. - **Real-world examples:** **Gmail**, Microsoft 365 (Outlook, Word, Excel Online), Google Workspace, Salesforce CRM, Dropbox, Zoom, Slack. - **Use case:** Email, collaboration, CRM, file storage, video conferencing — everything used by end users daily. --- **(b) A cloud provider offers 99.99% availability SLA. Calculate the maximum permissible downtime per year, per month, and per week. Also list and briefly explain the challenges of cloud computing. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Numerical Part (4 marks):** Given: $A = 99.99\% = 0.9999$ **Formula:** $$D = Total\ Time \times (1 - A)$$ **Per Year (365 days):** $$D_{year} = 365 \times 24 \times 60 \times (1 - 0.9999)$$ $$D_{year} = 525{,}600 \times 0.0001 = 52.56\ \text{minutes per year}$$ **Per Month (30 days):** $$D_{month} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 \times 0.0001 = 4.32\ \text{minutes per month}$$ **Per Week (7 days):** $$D_{week} = 7 \times 24 \times 60 \times 0.0001 = 1.008\ \text{minutes per week}\ (\approx 60.5\ \text{seconds})$$ **Conclusion:** A 99.99% (four nines) SLA allows the service to be down for only **~52 minutes per year** — a stringent commitment typical of mission-critical cloud services. --- **Challenges of Cloud Computing (3 marks):** 1. **Security and Privacy (1 mark):** Data stored on third-party servers raises concerns about unauthorized access, data breaches, and compliance with regulations like the IT Act and GDPR. Encrypted data can still be subpoenaed by governments. 2. **Downtime and Availability (0.5 mark):** Despite SLAs, cloud outages (e.g., AWS S3 outage in 2017) can take down major portions of the internet. Single point of failure risk exists. 3. **Vendor Lock-in (0.5 mark):** Migrating from one provider to another is difficult due to proprietary APIs, data formats, and lack of standardization. Switching costs can be prohibitive. 4. **Data Transfer and Latency (0.5 mark):** Moving large datasets (petabytes) to/from the cloud over the internet is slow and expensive. Applications requiring real-time response suffer from network latency. 5. **Compliance and Legal Issues (0.5 mark):** Different countries have different data residency laws. Storing Indian citizen data on US servers may violate DPDP Act 2023. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Do NOT write only the NIST definition for 14 marks** — examiners expect a long-form answer with diagrams, examples, and a comparison table. A 2-line definition gets at most 2 marks. > 2. **Always label diagrams** with proper arrows and legends. An unlabeled Mermaid-style block diagram = 0 marks for that sub-part. > 3. **For numerical questions, show ALL steps** — partial credit is given for correct formula application even if the final answer is wrong. Skipping steps = losing 2-3 marks. > 4. **Don't confuse "Virtualization" with "Cloud"** — they are related but not the same. Virtualization is a *technology*; Cloud is a *service model* built on virtualization. > 5. **Distinguish carefully between "Deployment Models" (Public/Private/Hybrid/Community) and "Service Models" (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS)** — these are two different classifications and are often asked together. > 6. **Avoid writing generic answers** like "Cloud is internet-based storage" — this is **wrong**. Cloud is far more than storage; it includes compute, networking, platforms, and software. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam:** - [ ] **NIST Definition** — Memorize the exact wording; it is the most-quoted definition. - [ ] **Five Essential Characteristics** — On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service. - [ ] **Three Service Models** — IaaS (you manage OS+), PaaS (you manage apps only), SaaS (you use only). - [ ] **Four Deployment Models** — Public, Private, Hybrid, Community. - [ ] **Pizza Analogy** — Use it to answer any "explain the three service models" question for guaranteed full marks. - [ ] **SLA Formula** — $D = Total\ Time \times (1 - A)$ ; remember $525{,}600$ minutes/year and $43{,}830$ minutes/month (30-day). - [ ] **TCO Comparison** — Always show 5-year cost: Hardware + Power + Cooling + Admin + Space vs Cloud per-hour cost. - [ ] **CAPEX vs OPEX** — Cloud converts capital expenditure to operational expenditure. - [ ] **Cloud ≠ Internet** — Cloud uses the internet but is a specific service model with defined characteristics. - [ ] **Public cloud providers to remember** — AWS (Amazon), Azure (Microsoft), GCP (Google), IBM Cloud, Oracle Cloud. - [ ] **Multi-tenancy** — Key concept behind resource pooling; one physical server hosts multiple isolated virtual tenants. - [ ] **Edge Computing** — Next evolution; processes data near the user to reduce latency. - [ ] **Serverless** — Extension of cloud; developer only writes functions, vendor manages everything else (e.g., AWS Lambda). - [ ] **DPDP Act 2023** — Indian data protection law relevant for cloud data residency questions. - [ ] **Three nines (99.9%) = 8h 45m downtime/year** — Most common SLA baseline. - [ ] **Four nines (99.99%) = ~52 minutes downtime/year** — Mission-critical. - [ ] **Five nines (99.999%) = ~5 minutes downtime/year** — Telecom-grade, almost impossible to achieve. > **End of Module 1 — Introduction to Cloud Computing Notes**

topics.title: Types of Cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

In the context of **Cloud Computing (OECST722)**, the term **"Types of Cloud"** refers to the standardized **Cloud Deployment Models** — the architectural classification of cloud environments based on **ownership, accessibility, scalability, and tenancy**. As defined by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Special Publication 800-145**, cloud deployment models describe how cloud infrastructure is provisioned, managed, and consumed by the tenant organization.

The **four primary cloud deployment models** recognized by NIST are:

1. **Public Cloud** — Infrastructure provisioned for open use by the general public.
2. **Private Cloud** — Infrastructure provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization.
3. **Hybrid Cloud** — A composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private, public, or community) bound by standardized or proprietary technology.
4. **Community Cloud** — Infrastructure provisioned for exclusive use by a specific community of consumers with shared concerns.

Two **modern extensions** (not originally in NIST SP 800-145 but widely accepted in KTU 2024 syllabus updates) are:

5. **Multi-Cloud** — The strategic use of multiple public cloud providers to avoid vendor lock-in.
6. **Distributed Cloud** — A public cloud architecture that extends cloud services to geographic locations outside the provider's data centers, processing data at the edge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The syllabus explicitly lists *Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community* clouds as the core four. However, examiners frequently award bonus marks for explaining **Multi-Cloud** and **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** as advanced extensions. Always cover the four primary types in depth first.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Housing Model of Computing"

Imagine cloud computing is like **renting vs. owning a house**:

| Cloud Analogy | Real-Life Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Public Cloud** | Staying in a **hotel room** — you share the building, pool, and gym with hundreds of strangers. Cheap, flexible, no maintenance. |
| **Private Cloud** | Owning a **private villa** — exclusive to your family, fully controlled, expensive to build and maintain. |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Owning a villa but using a **hotel for holidays** — keep sensitive work at home, burst into public when guests overflow. |
| **Community Cloud** | A **co-operative housing society** — built and shared by a group of like-minded organizations (e.g., all banks sharing one secure vault). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Anchor:** Whenever a KTU question asks "Which cloud type would a bank prefer for sensitive data?" — the answer is **Private Cloud** (or Hybrid with on-premise private). This is a recurrent exam pattern.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

- **Tenancy** — The number of organizations sharing the same cloud infrastructure.
- **Elasticity** — The ability to scale resources up/down dynamically (measured in **provisioning time in seconds/minutes**).
- **Cloud Bursting** — The act of offloading overflow workload from a private to a public cloud.
- **Vendor Lock-in** — The dependency on a specific cloud provider's proprietary technologies.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** — A formal contract guaranteeing **uptime of 99.9% (three nines)** to **99.999% (five nines)**.
- **CAPEX vs OPEX** — Private clouds favor **CAPEX (Capital Expenditure)**, public clouds favor **OPEX (Operational Expenditure)**.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Frequency KTU Term:** The term **"Cloud Bursting"** is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A. Memorize its definition precisely.

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Cloud Deployment Models — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Public Cloud

**Definition:** A public cloud is owned and operated by a third-party cloud service provider (CSP) such as **Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud Platform (GCP)**. Resources (servers, storage, applications) are made available to the general public over the public internet.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
- The CSP owns the physical data centers, networking equipment, and hypervisors.
- The CSP provisions **Virtual Machines (VMs)**, **containers**, or **serverless functions** on demand.
- Users access resources via **web consoles, REST APIs, or SDKs**.
- Billing is **metered** — pay only for what you consume (per-second or per-hour billing).

**Characteristics:**
- **Multi-Tenancy** — Multiple customers share the same physical hardware via logical isolation.
- **Massive Economies of Scale** — Cost per compute unit drops dramatically.
- **High Elasticity** — Provision thousands of VMs in minutes.
- **No Capital Investment** — Pure OPEX model.

**Real-World Utility:** Startups, SaaS product companies, academic institutions, and large enterprises running **non-sensitive workloads** (web hosting, CDNs, development/test environments).

---

### 2.1.2 Private Cloud

**Definition:** A private cloud is provisioned for the **exclusive use of a single organization**. It may be owned, managed, and operated by the organization itself, a third party, or a combination of both. It can exist **on-premises (on-prem)** or in a **dedicated off-premises data center**.

**Operational Logic:**
- The organization retains **full control** over the infrastructure stack — physical servers, hypervisor (VMware vSphere, OpenStack), and orchestration layer (Kubernetes, OpenShift).
- Deployment options:
  - **On-Premise Private Cloud** — Built within the company's own data center.
  - **Hosted Private Cloud** — Built in a third-party data center but dedicated to one client.
  - **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** — A logically isolated private cloud running inside a public cloud provider's infrastructure (e.g., AWS VPC, Azure Virtual Network).

**Characteristics:**
- **Single-Tenancy** — No other organization shares the hardware.
- **Maximum Control & Customization** — Hardware, OS, and software stack can be tuned precisely.
- **High Security & Compliance** — Meets strict regulatory requirements (HIPAA, GDPR, RBI banking norms).
- **Higher CAPEX** — Requires significant upfront investment in hardware and skilled staff.

**Real-World Utility:** Banks, defense organizations, healthcare providers, government agencies, and any entity handling **sensitive PII (Personally Identifiable Information)** or **PHI (Protected Health Information)**.

---

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Cloud

**Definition:** A hybrid cloud is a composition of **two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private, public, or community) that remain unique entities but are **bound together by standardized or proprietary technology** enabling data and application portability.

**Operational Logic:**
- The organization maintains a **private cloud for core/sensitive workloads**.
- It uses a **public cloud for burst capacity, big data analytics, or CDN distribution**.
- A **Hybrid Integration Platform (HIP)** — such as **AWS Outposts, Azure Arc, Google Anthos** — connects the two environments.
- **Cloud bursting** is the canonical hybrid use case.

**Characteristics:**
- **Best of Both Worlds** — Security of private + scalability of public.
- **Workload Portability** — Move VMs, containers, or data seamlessly between environments.
- **Complex Governance** — Requires unified identity (e.g., Active Directory + Azure AD), networking (VPN, Direct Connect), and observability.

**Real-World Utility:** Enterprises running legacy ERP on private infrastructure while leveraging public cloud AI/ML services for analytics.

---

### 2.1.4 Community Cloud

**Definition:** A community cloud is provisioned for the **exclusive use of a specific community of consumers** that share common concerns (mission, security requirements, policy, compliance considerations).

**Operational Logic:**
- Several organizations with **shared regulatory or industry requirements** pool resources.
- May be managed by the community itself, a third party, or a combination.
- Examples include:
  - **Government Community Cloud** (e.g., AWS GovCloud, Azure Government).
  - **Healthcare Community Cloud** (e.g., Microsoft Cloud for Healthcare).
  - **Financial Community Cloud** (banking consortium clouds).

**Characteristics:**
- **Collaborative Cost-Sharing** — Costs are distributed among community members.
- **Shared Compliance Framework** — Pre-built to meet industry-specific regulations.
- **Limited Scalability** — Constrained to community size.

---

## 2.2 Advanced Cloud Types (KTU 2024 Bonus Topics)

### 2.2.1 Multi-Cloud

**Definition:** The deliberate use of **multiple public cloud providers** (e.g., AWS + Azure + GCP) by a single organization to **avoid vendor lock-in, optimize cost, and improve resilience**.

**Key Distinction from Hybrid:** Hybrid = Private + Public. Multi-Cloud = Public + Public.

### 2.2.2 Distributed Cloud

**Definition:** A public cloud architecture where the provider extends its services to **multiple geographic locations** (edge nodes, on-prem customer data centers, 5G edge) while the customer retains a single pane of control.

**Example:** Google Cloud's **Distributed Cloud Edge** runs GCP services on the customer's premises with centralized management.

### 2.2.3 Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)

**Definition:** A **logically isolated virtual network** provisioned within a public cloud. It bridges the gap between public cloud convenience and private cloud isolation.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 Cloud Cost Calculation Formulas

**Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** for cloud deployment:

$$TCO_{cloud} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_{compute,i} + C_{storage,i} + C_{network,i} + C_{overhead,i}) \times T_{i}$$

Where:
- $C_{compute,i}$ = Cost of compute resources in time period $i$
- $C_{storage,i}$ = Cost of storage (GB/month) in time period $i$
- $C_{network,i}$ = Cost of egress/ingress bandwidth in time period $i$
- $C_{overhead,i}$ = Cost of management, monitoring, licensing in time period $i$
- $T_{i}$ = Duration of time period $i$ (typically months)

---

**Cost-Per-Instance Pricing Model** (Public Cloud — On-Demand):

$$C_{instance} = P_{hourly} \times H_{usage} \times M_{multiplier}$$

Where:
- $P_{hourly}$ = Hourly price of the VM instance
- $H_{usage}$ = Total hours of usage
- $M_{multiplier}$ = Regional pricing multiplier (e.g., **1.0** for US-East, **1.2** for EU-West)

---

**Reserved Instance Savings Formula** (Public Cloud — 1-Year vs 3-Year Reserved):

$$S_{reserved} = \left(1 - \frac{P_{reserved}}{P_{ondemand}}\right) \times 100\%$$

Typical values: **20%–40%** for 1-year, **40%–60%** for 3-year reserved.

---

**Cloud Bursting Capacity Formula** (Hybrid Cloud):

$$C_{total} = C_{private} + C_{burst}$$

$$C_{burst} = \max(0, \, L_{peak} - C_{private})$$

Where:
- $C_{total}$ = Total compute capacity needed
- $C_{private}$ = Capacity of private cloud (fixed)
- $L_{peak}$ = Peak workload demand
- $C_{burst}$ = Additional capacity to be provisioned in public cloud

---

**SLA Uptime-to-Downtime Conversion:**

$$D_{allowed} = T_{period} \times (1 - U_{SLA})$$

| SLA Uptime | Allowed Downtime per Month (30 days) | Allowed Downtime per Year (365 days) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 99% (Two Nines) | 7 hours 18 minutes | 3 days 15 hours 36 minutes |
| 99.9% (Three Nines) | 43 minutes 50 seconds | 8 hours 45 minutes 36 seconds |
| 99.99% (Four Nines) | 4 minutes 23 seconds | 52 minutes 33 seconds |
| 99.999% (Five Nines) | 26.3 seconds | 5 minutes 15 seconds |

---

### 2.3.2 Comparison Matrix — Cloud Deployment Models

| Attribute | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant | Single-tenant | Mixed | Multi-tenant (specific group) |
| **Owner** | Third-party CSP | Single organization | Organization + CSP | Community / Third-party |
| **Location** | Off-premise (provider's DC) | On-premise or hosted | Both | On/Off-premise |
| **Cost Model** | OPEX | CAPEX + OPEX | Mixed | Shared CAPEX |
| **Security Level** | Medium-High | Very High | High | High |
| **Scalability** | Virtually Unlimited | Limited by hardware | High | Moderate |
| **Customization** | Low | Very High | High | Moderate |
| **Compliance Fit** | Public workloads | Strict (HIPAA, GDPR) | Mixed | Industry-specific |
| **Example Providers** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack, VMware, AWS Outposts | Azure Arc, AWS Outposts, Anthos | AWS GovCloud, Azure Gov |
| **Best For** | Startups, web apps | Banks, defense, healthcare | Enterprises with mixed workloads | Govt, healthcare, banking consortiums |

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When a question provides a scenario (e.g., "A hospital needs to share patient records securely with 5 other hospitals"), the answer is **Community Cloud** — not Hybrid. The "shared concern" keyword is the giveaway.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Workload: Choosing the Optimal Cloud Type

This section walks through a **decision-making framework** that KTU examiners frequently test in 14-mark questions. We will derive a **weighted scoring model** to select the optimal cloud deployment model for a given business scenario.

---

### 3.1.1 The Weighted Decision Matrix Formula

For each cloud type $j$ (where $j \in \{Public, Private, Hybrid, Community\}$), we compute a **Total Weighted Score (TWS)**:

$$TWS_{j} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i} \times S_{ij}$$

Where:
- $TWS_{j}$ = Total Weighted Score for cloud type $j$
- $W_{i}$ = Weight of evaluation criterion $i$ (where $\sum_{i=1}^{n} W_{i} = 1$)
- $S_{ij}$ = Score (0 to 10) of cloud type $j$ on criterion $i$
- $n$ = Number of evaluation criteria

The optimal cloud type is the one with the **maximum TWS**:

$$j_{optimal} = \arg\max_{j} TWS_{j}$$

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Banking Application

**Scenario:** A mid-sized bank must host its core transaction processing system and customer-facing mobile banking app.

**Evaluation Criteria & Weights:**

| Criterion $i$ | Weight $W_{i}$ |
| :--- | :---: |
| $C_1$: Security & Compliance | 0.35 |
| $C_2$: Scalability | 0.20 |
| $C_3$: Cost Efficiency | 0.15 |
| $C_4$: Performance | 0.20 |
| $C_5$: Control & Customization | 0.10 |
| **Total** | **1.00** |

**Scoring Matrix $S_{ij}$ (0–10):**

| Criterion | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $C_1$: Security | 6 | 10 | 9 | 9 |
| $C_2$: Scalability | 10 | 4 | 9 | 5 |
| $C_3$: Cost | 9 | 4 | 7 | 6 |
| $C_4$: Performance | 8 | 8 | 9 | 7 |
| $C_5$: Control | 4 | 10 | 7 | 6 |

---

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step TWS Calculation

**Step 1: Compute TWS for Public Cloud**

We expand the summation term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{public} &= W_1 \cdot S_{1,public} + W_2 \cdot S_{2,public} + W_3 \cdot S_{3,public} + W_4 \cdot S_{4,public} + W_5 \cdot S_{5,public}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{public} &= (0.35 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 10) + (0.15 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 4) \\
&= 2.10 + 2.00 + 1.35 + 1.60 + 0.40 \\
&= 7.45
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2: Compute TWS for Private Cloud**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{private} &= (0.35 \times 10) + (0.20 \times 4) + (0.15 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 8) + (0.10 \times 10) \\
&= 3.50 + 0.80 + 0.60 + 1.60 + 1.00 \\
&= 7.50
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3: Compute TWS for Hybrid Cloud**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{hybrid} &= (0.35 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.15 \times 7) + (0.20 \times 9) + (0.10 \times 7) \\
&= 3.15 + 1.80 + 1.05 + 1.80 + 0.70 \\
&= 8.50
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: Compute TWS for Community Cloud**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TWS_{community} &= (0.35 \times 9) + (0.20 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 6) + (0.20 \times 7) + (0.10 \times 6) \\
&= 3.15 + 1.00 + 0.90 + 1.40 + 0.60 \\
&= 7.05
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Select the Optimal Cloud Type**

$$
\begin{aligned}
j_{optimal} &= \arg\max_{j} TWS_{j} \\
&= \arg\max(7.45, \; 7.50, \; 8.50, \; 7.05) \\
&= \text{Hybrid Cloud}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** With a TWS of **8.50**, **Hybrid Cloud** is the optimal deployment model for the banking application. This is a real-world accurate outcome — banks keep core transactions on private infrastructure while bursting analytics and mobile app backend to the public cloud.

---

## 3.2 Capacity Planning Derivation — Cloud Bursting Trigger

A common KTU 14-mark question asks: *"Explain cloud bursting with a numerical example."* Below is the complete derivation.

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A company has a **private cloud with 100 vCPUs of capacity**. During business hours (9 AM – 6 PM), the workload peaks at **180 vCPUs**. Determine the burst capacity required from the public cloud and the average cost.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

**Given:**
- $C_{private} = 100$ vCPUs (private cloud capacity)
- $L_{peak} = 180$ vCPUs (peak load)
- $H_{peak} = 9$ hours (peak duration)
- $H_{offpeak} = 15$ hours (off-peak duration, 24 − 9)
- $P_{hourly} = \$0.05$ per vCPU-hour (public cloud rate)

**Step 1: Calculate Burst Capacity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{burst} &= \max(0, L_{peak} - C_{private}) \\
&= \max(0, 180 - 100) \\
&= 80 \text{ vCPUs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Daily Public Cloud Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{daily} &= C_{burst} \times H_{peak} \times P_{hourly} \\
&= 80 \times 9 \times 0.05 \\
&= \$36.00 \text{ per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Monthly Cost (30 days)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{monthly} &= C_{daily} \times 30 \\
&= 36 \times 30 \\
&= \$1{,}080 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Cost Savings vs Full Public Cloud**

If the company provisioned **all 180 vCPUs** on the public cloud for 24 hours:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{fullpublic} &= 180 \times 24 \times 30 \times 0.05 \\
&= \$6{,}480 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{savings} &= C_{fullpublic} - (C_{private}^{fixed} + C_{monthly}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Hybrid cloud bursting reduces cost by **~83%** (from \$6,480 to \$1,080) for the variable component alone — this is the **single most compelling business case for hybrid cloud architecture** in KTU exam answers.
