# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each, Short Answer)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Define cloud bursting. Mention one scenario where it is commonly used. (2 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cloud bursting** is a hybrid cloud deployment pattern in which an application running in a private cloud **bursts into a public cloud** when the demand for compute capacity exceeds the private cloud's capacity threshold. The additional workload is automatically offloaded to the public cloud, and when demand subsides, the public cloud resources are released.
>
> **Scenario:** An e-commerce website during a **flash sale** (e.g., Amazon's Prime Day) experiences traffic 10x the normal level. The private cloud infrastructure (typically sized for baseline traffic) cannot handle the surge, so additional web servers are automatically spun up in a public cloud (AWS, Azure) to serve the excess traffic.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Differentiate between Public Cloud and Private Cloud in any two aspects. (2 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Public Cloud | Private Cloud |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Ownership** | Owned and managed by a third-party CSP (e.g., AWS, Azure). | Owned and managed by a single organization, on-premise or hosted. |
| **Tenancy** | Multi-tenant — resources shared among many customers. | Single-tenant — resources dedicated to one organization. |
| **Cost Model** | Pay-per-use OPEX (Operational Expenditure). | High upfront CAPEX (Capital Expenditure) plus ongoing OPEX. |
| **Security** | Standardized security, suitable for non-sensitive workloads. | Customized high security, suitable for sensitive/regulated data. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B Module 1 questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Each alternative typically has **sub-part (a) for 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) for 7 marks**. Sub-part (a) is usually a descriptive/theoretical question, while sub-part (b) is often a comparison, diagram, or numerical problem.

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain in detail the four major types of cloud deployment models with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four major cloud deployment models defined by NIST are:

**(i) Public Cloud:**
- Owned and operated by third-party cloud service providers such as **Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, or Google Cloud Platform (GCP)**.
- Resources (compute, storage, networking) are made available to the general public over the internet.
- **Multi-tenant** architecture — multiple organizations share the same physical infrastructure via logical isolation (hypervisors, containers, namespaces).
- **Advantages:** No capital investment, near-infinite scalability, pay-per-use pricing, no maintenance overhead.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited customization, potential data sovereignty concerns, dependency on internet connectivity.
- **Examples:** AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine.

**(ii) Private Cloud:**
- Provisioned for the **exclusive use of a single organization**.
- Can be hosted on-premises (within the organization's own data center) or in a dedicated off-premises facility.
- **Single-tenant** — no other organization shares the infrastructure.
- **Advantages:** Maximum control, customization, security, and compliance (HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS).
- **Disadvantages:** High CAPEX, requires skilled IT staff, limited scalability.
- **Examples:** OpenStack, VMware vSphere, Microsoft Azure Stack Hub, AWS Outposts.

**(iii) Hybrid Cloud:**
- A composition of **two or more distinct cloud infrastructures** (private + public, or public + public) bound by standardized or proprietary technology.
- Enables **data and application portability** between environments.
- The flagship use case is **cloud bursting** — offloading overflow workload to the public cloud.
- **Advantages:** Combines security of private with scalability of public; flexibility in workload placement.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex integration, requires unified networking, identity, and observability.
- **Examples:** Azure Arc, Google Anthos, AWS Outposts, IBM Cloud Satellite.

**(iv) Community Cloud:**
- Provisioned for the **exclusive use of a specific community of consumers** that share common concerns (mission, security, policy, compliance).
- Costs are shared among community members.
- **Advantages:** Shared compliance framework, collaborative cost model, high security within community.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited scalability (constrained to community size), governance complexity.
- **Examples:** AWS GovCloud (US government agencies), Azure Government Cloud, healthcare community clouds.

---

**(b) Compare and contrast the four cloud deployment models on the basis of ownership, scalability, security, cost, and best-fit use case. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Public Cloud | Private Cloud | Hybrid Cloud | Community Cloud |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Ownership** | Third-party CSP | Single organization | Org + CSP | Community/Consortium |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by physical hardware | Very high (bursting) | Moderate |
| **Security Level** | Medium to High | Very High (custom) | High | High (industry-fit) |
| **Cost Model** | OPEX (pay-per-use) | High CAPEX + OPEX | Mixed CAPEX + OPEX | Shared CAPEX |
| **Internet Dependency** | High | Low (works offline) | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Customization** | Low | Very High | High | Moderate |
| **Best-Fit Use Case** | Startups, web apps, dev/test | Banks, defense, healthcare | Enterprises with mixed workloads | Govt, banking consortium, healthcare groups |
| **Example Provider** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack, VMware | Azure Arc, Anthos | AWS GovCloud, Azure Gov |
| **Compliance Fit** | Public workloads | HIPAA, GDPR, PCI-DSS | Mixed | Industry-specific regulations |

**Conclusion:** The choice of cloud deployment model is driven primarily by **security requirements, cost constraints, scalability needs, and regulatory compliance**. Modern enterprises increasingly favor **Hybrid Cloud** as it offers the optimal balance between control and elasticity.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a Hybrid Cloud deployment. Discuss the concept of cloud bursting with a real-time example. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hybrid Cloud Architecture:**

A hybrid cloud architecture integrates a **private cloud** (on-premises or hosted) with one or more **public cloud providers** (AWS, Azure, GCP) through a **secure integration layer**. The key architectural components are:

1. **Private Cloud Side:** On-premises data center running core business applications, sensitive databases, and legacy systems.
2. **Public Cloud Side:** Public CSP (AWS/Azure/GCP) hosting elastic workloads, analytics, and CDN.
3. **Integration Layer:** This includes:
   - **Network Connectivity:** VPN tunnels, AWS Direct Connect, Azure ExpressRoute, Google Cloud Interconnect.
   - **Identity Federation:** Single Sign-On (SSO) via SAML/OAuth, Azure AD, Okta.
   - **Data Integration:** APIs, message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ), data sync tools (Azure Data Factory).
   - **Orchestration:** Unified management plane — Azure Arc, Google Anthos, AWS Systems Manager.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    a1[Private Cloud On Premise] --> b1[Hybrid Integration Platform]
    a2[Public Cloud AWS] --> b1
    a3[Public Cloud Azure] --> b1
    b1 --> c1[Unified Monitoring and Logging]
    b1 --> c2[Identity and Access Management]
    b1 --> c3[Data Sync and APIs]
    
    style a1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style a2 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style a3 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style b1 fill:#9333ea,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

**Cloud Bursting — Real-Time Example:**

**Scenario:** A retail company runs its inventory management system on a private cloud sized for normal weekday traffic (1,000 concurrent users). During a festive sale (e.g., Diwali, Black Friday), traffic spikes to **10,000 concurrent users**.

**Process:**
- The **load balancer** detects that the private cloud's capacity threshold is exceeded.
- The **orchestrator** (e.g., Azure Arc) automatically provisions **additional VMs in the public cloud** (say, 50 new EC2 instances on AWS).
- The new public cloud instances join the application cluster and share the traffic.
- After the sale ends, the public cloud instances are **automatically de-provisioned**, and the workload returns entirely to the private cloud.
- The customer pays only for the **burst hours** at public cloud rates.

This pattern saves the company from over-provisioning the private cloud for 365 days a year.

---

**(b) A company has a private cloud with 50 vCPUs capacity. The peak load reaches 120 vCPUs for 6 hours daily. The public cloud rate is \$0.04 per vCPU-hour. Calculate: (i) Burst capacity, (ii) Daily public cloud cost, (iii) Monthly cost savings compared to running the entire workload on the public cloud for 24 hours. Assume 30 days/month. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C_{private} = 50$ vCPUs
- $L_{peak} = 120$ vCPUs
- $H_{peak} = 6$ hours/day
- $P_{hourly} = \$0.04$ per vCPU-hour
- $D = 30$ days

**(i) Burst Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{burst} &= \max(0, L_{peak} - C_{private}) \\
&= \max(0, 120 - 50) \\
&= 70 \text{ vCPUs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily Public Cloud Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{daily} &= C_{burst} \times H_{peak} \times P_{hourly} \\
&= 70 \times 6 \times 0.04 \\
&= \$16.80 \text{ per day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Monthly Cost Savings:**

**Cost of burst strategy (public + private CAPEX ignored):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{hybrid,monthly} &= C_{daily} \times 30 \\
&= 16.80 \times 30 \\
&= \$504 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cost of full public cloud strategy (120 vCPUs × 24 hours × 30 days):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{fullpublic,monthly} &= 120 \times 24 \times 30 \times 0.04 \\
&= \$3{,}456 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Monthly Cost Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{savings} &= C_{fullpublic,monthly} - C_{hybrid,monthly} \\
&= 3{,}456 - 504 \\
&= \$2{,}952 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%S_{savings} &= \frac{S_{savings}}{C_{fullpublic,monthly}} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2{,}952}{3{,}456} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 85.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Answer:** Burst capacity = **70 vCPUs**, Daily cost = **\$16.80**, Monthly savings = **\$2,952** (i.e., **85.42% cost reduction**).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in "Types of Cloud" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Deployment Models with Service Models (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS).** Deployment = *where* the cloud lives. Service = *what* is offered. Mixing them = 2–3 marks deducted.
> 2. **Writing "Hybrid Cloud" when the answer is "Community Cloud."** The keyword "shared concern / community of organizations" is a strong Community Cloud indicator. Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Forgetting to draw diagrams in 14-mark answers.** A neat architecture diagram of hybrid or community cloud can fetch **2–3 extra marks** even if the text is average.
> 4. **Not stating the owner/provider in examples.** Writing "Public Cloud — AWS, Azure, GCP" is far more valuable than writing only "Public Cloud — third-party provider."
> 5. **Numerical errors in cloud bursting problems.** The most common mistake is multiplying 24 hours instead of peak hours. Always re-read the problem statement.
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion.** KTU examiners reward answers that end with a **justified recommendation** ("Hybrid cloud is best because...").

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for "Types of Cloud":**
>
> - The **four primary deployment models** are Public, Private, Hybrid, and Community (NIST-defined).
> - **Public Cloud** → Multi-tenant, third-party owned, OPEX, low control, high scalability (AWS, Azure, GCP).
> - **Private Cloud** → Single-tenant, single org owned, high CAPEX, maximum control, best for sensitive data (OpenStack, VMware).
> - **Hybrid Cloud** → Private + Public, enables **cloud bursting**, balanced control and scalability (Azure Arc, Anthos, AWS Outposts).
> - **Community Cloud** → Shared by organizations with **common concerns** (GovCloud, healthcare, banking consortium).
> - **Multi-Cloud** → Multiple public providers, avoids vendor lock-in (NOT the same as Hybrid).
> - **VPC (Virtual Private Cloud)** → Logical isolation inside a public cloud.
> - **Cloud Bursting** → Auto-provisioning in public cloud when private capacity is exceeded.
> - **CAPEX** → Upfront capital expenditure (favors Private Cloud).
> - **OPEX** → Pay-per-use operational expenditure (favors Public Cloud).
> - **SLA Uptime** → 99.9% (Three Nines) = 43.83 min downtime/month; 99.99% = 4.38 min/month.
> - The **TCO formula** sums compute, storage, network, and overhead costs over time.
> - The **weighted decision matrix** uses $TWS_{j} = \sum W_i \times S_{ij}$ to choose the optimal cloud type.
> - **Bursting capacity** formula: $C_{burst} = \max(0, L_{peak} - C_{private})$.
> - KTU exam scenarios: **Bank → Private/Hybrid**, **Startup → Public**, **Govt → Community**, **E-commerce sale → Hybrid with bursting**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Working of Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Computing** is a paradigm that enables ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. This definition is standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** and is the de-facto reference adopted in the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus.

The cloud model is composed of **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models** (collectively known as the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture).

### The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing
1. **On-Demand Self-Service** – A consumer can provision compute, storage, and networking capabilities automatically without human interaction with the service provider.
2. **Broad Network Access** – Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous thin or thick client platforms (mobile, laptop, PDA).
3. **Resource Pooling** – Provider resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a **multi-tenant model** with physical and virtual resources dynamically reassigned according to consumer demand.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** – Capabilities can be scaled out/in rapidly and automatically to match demand. To the consumer, the provisioning often appears unlimited.
5. **Measured Service** – Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the type of service (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Electricity Grid" Model**
> Think of cloud computing exactly like the **electricity supplied to your home**. You do not own a power generator in your backyard. Instead, you tap into a grid managed by the electricity board. You pay only for the units (kilowatt-hours) you consume. When you need more power (say, to run an AC during summer), the grid automatically scales to meet your demand. You never worry about fuel, maintenance, or infrastructure.
> 
> **Cloud = The Grid**, **Your Laptop/App = The Appliance**, **Pay-per-use billing = The Electricity Bill**, **Provider (AWS/Azure/GCP) = The Electricity Board**.

In this analogy, the **"cloud"** simply refers to a remote collection of servers, storage, databases, networking, software, and analytics hosted on the internet. When you draw a network diagram, that remote infrastructure is conventionally shown as a **cloud-shaped bubble** — hence the name *Cloud Computing*.

## 1.3 Why is it Important for KTU Examinations?

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage Note:** Questions on "Working of Cloud Computing" frequently appear in **Part A (3 marks – definition-based)** and as **Part B (14 marks – architecture/working model-based)** in End Semester Examinations. Mastering the NIST model, virtualization's role, and the front-end/back-end interaction guarantees at least **8–10 marks** per question paper.

### Key Standard Metrics You MUST Remember in Bold
- **Virtualization** is the foundational technology that makes cloud computing possible.
- **Multi-tenancy** is the architectural principle enabling resource pooling.
- **SLA (Service Level Agreement)** guarantees **99.9% (Three-Nines) to 99.99% (Four-Nines)** uptime in most commercial cloud SLAs.
- **Hypervisor** is the software layer that creates and runs **Virtual Machines (VMs)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How Cloud Computing Works – The Operational Flow

The working of cloud computing can be decomposed into **three logical layers** that interact continuously. Understanding this flow is the key to answering any KTU 14-mark "working of cloud" question.

### Layer 1: The Front-End (Client Side)
The front-end is what the **user interacts with**. It includes:
- The **client device** (laptop, smartphone, tablet).
- The **client-side application** (web browser, mobile app, thin client).
- The **network** (Internet or intranet) connecting the user to the back-end.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** When you open *Gmail* in your Chrome browser, the browser is the front-end. The act of typing your email and clicking "Send" generates a request that travels over the Internet to Google's data center.

### Layer 2: The Back-End (Provider Side)
The back-end is the **cloud itself** — a massive collection of data centers housing:
- **Compute resources** (physical servers running millions of VMs).
- **Storage resources** (SAN, NAS, Object Storage like AWS S3).
- **Network resources** (routers, switches, load balancers, firewalls).
- **Cloud management software** (OpenStack, VMware vSphere, Microsoft Azure Fabric Controller).
- **Hypervisors** (VMware ESXi, KVM, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen) that slice physical hardware into many isolated VMs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The back-end is responsible for **orchestrating, provisioning, monitoring, and billing** the resources. Without the back-end orchestration layer, cloud computing would simply be a collection of idle servers.

### Layer 3: The Network + Middleware (The Bridge)
This is the **middleware layer** (sometimes called the "cloud fabric") that:
- Routes user requests to the correct data center using **DNS-based Geo-Routing**.
- Applies **load balancing** to distribute traffic across multiple servers.
- Handles **authentication, authorization, and accounting (AAA)** via protocols like **SAML, OAuth 2.0, and OpenID Connect**.
- Enforces **encryption in transit (TLS/SSL)** and **encryption at rest (AES-256)**.

## 2.2 The Working Mechanism – Step-by-Step Logic

Here is the precise operational sequence whenever a user interacts with a cloud service:

1. **User Request Initiation:** The user issues a request through the front-end (e.g., uploads a file to Google Drive).
2. **Network Transmission:** The request is encrypted (TLS 1.3) and routed over the public Internet.
3. **Middleware Routing:** A global **load balancer** (e.g., AWS Elastic Load Balancer, F5 BIG-IP) receives the request and forwards it to an available back-end server.
4. **Hypervisor Invocation:** The **hypervisor** allocates a VM or container with the required CPU, RAM, and storage slice from the resource pool.
5. **Resource Pooling & Provisioning:** The VM executes the task. Resources are drawn from a **multi-tenant pool** shared across thousands of users.
6. **Measured Service / Metering:** A metering component (e.g., AWS CloudWatch, Azure Monitor) records the consumption (CPU-hours, GB transferred, API calls).
7. **Response Delivery:** The processed output is sent back to the front-end over the network.
8. **Billing:** At the end of the billing cycle, the metering data is used to generate a pay-per-use invoice.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This same mechanism powers Netflix streaming, Uber ride dispatch, WhatsApp messaging, and even B.Tech lab assignments hosted on cloud Jupyter notebooks. Mastering the flow makes you industry-ready.

## 2.3 The Role of Virtualization – The Heart of Cloud Working

Virtualization is the **enabling technology** that transforms static physical hardware into elastic, on-demand cloud resources.

| Component | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Hypervisor (VMM)** | Virtual Machine Monitor – software that creates & runs VMs. | Type 1 (bare-metal) is most common in cloud. |
| **Virtual Machine (VM)** | An isolated emulation of a physical computer with its own OS. | Provides multi-tenancy and isolation. |
| **Container (Docker)** | Lightweight OS-level virtualization sharing the host kernel. | Used in PaaS and modern microservices. |
| **vCPU, vRAM, vDisk** | Virtualized slices of physical CPU, memory, and disk. | The "currency" billed to the user. |
| **Live Migration** | Moving a running VM from one host to another with zero downtime. | Enables proactive maintenance in cloud. |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **formulas, definitions, and boundary conditions** most frequently tested in KTU ESE papers for the "Working of Cloud Computing" topic.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SLA Availability | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | 99.9% = ~8.77 hrs/yr downtime |
| 2 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | Hours |
| 3 | Mean Time To Repair | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | Hours |
| 4 | System Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq R \leq 1$ |
| 5 | CPU Utilization | $\text{Utilization} = \dfrac{\text{Active Cores}}{\text{Total Cores}} \times 100\%$ | 0% to 100% |
| 6 | Pay-Per-Use Cost | $\text{Cost} = \sum_{i} (P_i \times U_i)$ | $P_i$ = price/unit, $U_i$ = units used |
| 7 | Storage Provisioning | $\text{Total Storage} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{VM}_j^{\text{disk}} + \text{Replica Overhead}$ | GB or TB |
| 8 | Bandwidth Requirement | $\text{BW} = \dfrac{\text{Data Size}}{\text{Transfer Time}}$ | Mbps or Gbps |
| 9 | VM Density | $\text{Density} = \dfrac{\text{Number of VMs}}{\text{Physical Host}}$ | Ratio (typically 10:1 to 100:1) |
| 10 | Elasticity Coefficient | $E = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Resources}}{\Delta \text{Load}}$ | Higher = more elastic |

## 2.5 Cloud Deployment Models (How it is Deployed)

| Model | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Owned by third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP); open to public. | Startups, web apps |
| **Private Cloud** | Used exclusively by one organization; on-premise or hosted. | Banks, defense, hospitals |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Combination of public + private with orchestration between them. | Burst workloads, compliance |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., government). | Research consortiums |

## 2.6 Cloud Service Models (How it is Consumed)

| Model | Full Form | You Manage | Provider Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | OS, Apps, Data | Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | Apps, Data | OS, Middleware, Runtime | Google App Engine, Heroku |
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Only your data | Everything else | Gmail, Microsoft 365, Dropbox |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 – SLA Availability and Downtime Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **most frequently asked numerical in KTU ESE** for cloud computing basics. The examiner tests whether you can convert the SLA percentage to permissible annual downtime.

**Problem Setup:** A cloud provider advertises an SLA of **99.99% (Four-Nines)**. Calculate the maximum allowable annual downtime.

### Step 1: State the Availability Formula

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} \times 100\%
$$

### Step 2: Convert to Decimal Form

$$
A = 99.99\% = 0.9999
$$

### Step 3: Express Downtime as a Fraction of Total Time

$$
A = 1 - \frac{D}{T}
$$

where $D$ = Downtime and $T$ = Total time in the same unit.

### Step 4: Calculate Annual Downtime

For a full year, $T = 365 \times 24 = 8760$ hours.

$$
D = (1 - A) \times T
$$

$$
D = (1 - 0.9999) \times 8760
$$

$$
D = 0.0001 \times 8760
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 0.876 \text{ hours/year} \approx 52.56 \text{ minutes/year}}
$$

### Step 5: Comparative Analysis

| SLA Level | Decimal | Annual Downtime | Monthly Downtime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 99% (Two-Nines) | 0.99 | 87.60 hours | 7.30 hours |
| 99.9% (Three-Nines) | 0.999 | 8.76 hours | 43.80 minutes |
| 99.99% (Four-Nines) | 0.9999 | 52.56 minutes | 4.38 minutes |
| 99.999% (Five-Nines) | 0.99999 | 5.26 minutes | 26.30 seconds |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Moving from Three-Nines to Four-Nines requires a **10× reduction** in downtime, but the infrastructure cost increases disproportionately (often **4–5× higher**). This trade-off is the foundation of cloud economics.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 – VM Density and Capacity Planning

**Problem Setup:** A data center has **50 physical servers**. Each server hosts an average of **20 VMs** (Virtual Machines). The average VM requires **2 vCPUs and 4 GB RAM**. Calculate the total logical compute capacity delivered.

### Step 1: Calculate Total VMs

$$
N_{\text{VM}} = N_{\text{hosts}} \times \text{VM Density}
$$

$$
N_{\text{VM}} = 50 \times 20 = 1000 \text{ VMs}
$$

### Step 2: Calculate Total vCPUs

$$
\text{Total vCPUs} = N_{\text{VM}} \times \text{vCPUs per VM}
$$

$$
\text{Total vCPUs} = 1000 \times 2 = 2000 \text{ vCPUs}
$$

### Step 3: Calculate Total Virtual RAM

$$
\text{Total vRAM} = N_{\text{VM}} \times \text{RAM per VM}
$$

$$
\text{Total vRAM} = 1000 \times 4 = 4000 \text{ GB} = 4 \text{ TB}
$$

### Step 4: Derive the General Capacity Equation

$$
C_{\text{logical}} = N_h \times D_v \times R_{\text{per-VM}}
$$

where:
- $N_h$ = Number of physical hosts
- $D_v$ = VM density (VMs per host)
- $R_{\text{per-VM}}$ = Resources per VM

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** If the cloud provider prices a vCPU at **₹5/hour** and 4 GB RAM at **₹2/hour**, the **maximum theoretical billing** for 100% utilization of this data center is:
> 
> $$
> \text{Max Billing} = 1000 \times (\text{₹}5 \times 2 + \text{₹}2 \times 4) \times 730 \text{ hrs}
> $$
> 
> $$
> \text{Max Billing} = 1000 \times \text{₹}18 \times 730 = \text{₹}1,31,40,000 \text{ / month}
> $$
> 
> This is the **revenue ceiling** before considering multi-tenancy, discounts, and reserved instances.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Mapping Concepts to KTU Blooms Levels

| Bloom's Level | Cognitive Action | Sample Question Stem | Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1 – Remember** | Recall NIST characteristics | "List the five essential characteristics of cloud computing." | 3 marks (Part A) |
| **L2 – Understand** | Explain working mechanism | "Explain how a user request is processed in a cloud system." | 7 marks (Part B sub) |
| **L3 – Apply** | Compute SLA downtime | "An SLA of 99.95% means how many hours of allowable downtime per year?" | 3–7 marks |
| **L4 – Analyze** | Compare service models | "Compare IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS in terms of user control and provider responsibility." | 7–14 marks |
| **L5 – Evaluate** | Justify architecture choice | "Justify why a hybrid cloud is suitable for a banking application." | 14 marks |
| **L6 – Create** | Design cloud architecture | "Design a cloud-based solution for a university examination system." | 14 marks |

## 3.4 Exhaustive Working Model – End-to-End Logical Derivation

Let us derive a generalized working equation that represents the end-to-end latency experienced by a user in a cloud request.

### Step 1: Define Latency Components

The total time $T_{\text{total}}$ for a cloud request is:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{client}} + T_{\text{network}} + T_{\text{middleware}} + T_{\text{processing}} + T_{\text{response}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Each Term

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_c + \frac{D_{\text{up}}}{B_{\text{up}}} + T_{lb} + \frac{W}{P_{\text{eff}}} + \frac{D_{\text{down}}}{B_{\text{down}}}
$$

where:
- $T_c$ = Client-side processing time
- $D_{\text{up}}$ = Upload data size; $B_{\text{up}}$ = Upload bandwidth
- $T_{lb}$ = Load balancer queue + routing time
- $W$ = Workload (instructions); $P_{\text{eff}}$ = Effective processing power
- $D_{\text{down}}$ = Download data size; $B_{\text{down}}$ = Download bandwidth

### Step 3: Simplify Using Approximations

Assuming $D_{\text{up}} \approx D_{\text{down}} = D$ and $B_{\text{up}} \approx B_{\text{down}} = B$:

$$
T_{\text{total}} \approx T_c + \frac{2D}{B} + T_{lb} + \frac{W}{P_{\text{eff}}}
$$

### Step 4: Engineering Insight

> [!TIP]
> This equation teaches three optimization strategies:
> 1. **Reduce $D$** via compression (gzip, Brotli).
> 2. **Increase $B$** via CDNs and edge computing.
> 3. **Increase $P_{\text{eff}}$** via auto-scaling and efficient hypervisors.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Working Architecture of Cloud Computing

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the complete end-to-end working of a cloud computing system, mapping the front-end, middleware, and back-end interactions.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FE["FRONT END (Client Side)"]
        U["User Device Laptop or Mobile"]
        A["Client Application Browser or App"]
    end

    subgraph MW["MIDDLEWARE LAYER (Cloud Fabric)"]
        LB["Load Balancer Routes Traffic"]
        AUTH["Authentication Service OAuth 2.0 or SAML"]
        METER["Metering Module Tracks Usage"]
    end

    subgraph BE["BACK END (Provider Data Center)"]
        HV["Hypervisor KVM or ESXi or Hyper-V"]
        VM1["Virtual Machine 1 App Server"]
        VM2["Virtual Machine 2 DB Server"]
        VM3["Virtual Machine 3 Cache Server"]
        ST["Shared Storage SAN or Object Store"]
        NET["Virtual Network SDN Overlay"]
        MGMT["Cloud Management OpenStack or Azure Fabric"]
    end

    U -->|Issues Request| A
    A -->|HTTPS TLS Encrypted| LB
    LB --> AUTH
    AUTH -->|Authorized| HV
    HV --> VM1
    HV --> VM2
    HV --> VM3
    VM1 --> ST
    VM2 --> ST
    VM3 --> ST
    MGMT -->|Orchestrates| HV
    MGMT -->|Configures| NET
    METER -->|Records CPU RAM BW| VM1
    METER -->|Records CPU RAM BW| VM2
    METER -->|Records CPU RAM BW| VM3
    VM1 -->|Response| A
    VM2 -->|Response| A
    VM3 -->|Response| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** Arrows represent **data flow direction**. The **MGMT (Management)** node has bidirectional control over the **Hypervisor**, **Storage**, and **Network** — this is what makes the cloud *programmable* and *elastic*.

## 4.2 Request Lifecycle Flowchart (Step-by-Step Working)

This diagram traces a single user request from initiation to billing closure.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant F as Front End App
    participant L as Load Balancer
    participant A as Auth Service
    participant H as Hypervisor
    participant V as Virtual Machine
    participant S as Storage
    participant M as Metering Billing

    U->>F: 1. Initiates Request Example Upload File
    F->>L: 2. Sends Encrypted HTTPS Request
    L->>A: 3. Validates JWT or Session Token
    A-->>L: 4. Returns Authorized
    L->>H: 5. Requests VM Allocation
    H->>V: 6. Provisions VM with Required Resources
    V->>S: 7. Reads or Writes Data
    S-->>V: 8. Returns Data
    V-->>F: 9. Sends Response over Network
    F-->>U: 10. Displays Result to User
    V->>M: 11. Reports Resource Consumption
    M-->>U: 12. Generates Monthly Pay Per Use Bill
```

## 4.3 Service Model Responsibility Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IAAS["IaaS INFRASTRUCTURE AS A SERVICE"]
        I1["Provider Manages Servers Storage Networking"]
        I2["User Manages OS Middleware Apps Data"]
    end

    subgraph PAAS["PaaS PLATFORM AS A SERVICE"]
        P1["Provider Manages Servers Storage Networking OS Middleware Runtime"]
        P2["User Manages Apps and Data Only"]
    end

    subgraph SAAS["SaaS SOFTWARE AS A SERVICE"]
        S1["Provider Manages Everything"]
        S2["User Manages Only Their Data"]
    end

    IAAS --> PAAS --> SAAS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** As you move from **IaaS → PaaS → SaaS**, the **user's responsibility decreases** while the **provider's responsibility increases**. This is the core abstraction layering principle of cloud computing.

## 4.4 Resource Pooling & Multi-Tenancy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PP["SHARED RESOURCE POOL"]
        CPU["Physical CPUs 64 Cores"]
        RAM["Physical RAM 512 GB"]
        DSK["Physical Storage 100 TB"]
        NET["Physical Network 10 Gbps"]
    end

    subgraph HV["HYPERVISOR LAYER"]
        V1["vCPU 4 vRAM 8 GB"]
        V2["vCPU 8 vRAM 16 GB"]
        V3["vCPU 2 vRAM 4 GB"]
    end

    subgraph TN["MULTI TENANT ISOLATION"]
        T1["Tenant A Bank App"]
        T2["Tenant B E Commerce"]
        T3["Tenant C University ERP"]
    end

    CPU --> HV
    RAM --> HV
    DSK --> HV
    NET --> HV
    V1 --> T1
    V2 --> T2
    V3 --> T3
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw the "working of cloud computing," always include **(a) Front-end, (b) Back-end, (c) Middleware, (d) Hypervisor, and (e) Resource Pool.** Missing the hypervisor is the most common deduction of marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define cloud computing. List any four essential characteristics of cloud computing as per the NIST model.**

**Model Answer:**
Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction. *(NIST definition)*

**Four Essential Characteristics:**
1. **On-demand self-service**
2. **Broad network access**
3. **Resource pooling**
4. **Rapid elasticity**
5. **Measured service** *(any four)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Four characteristics (0.375 each = 1.5 marks) = **3 marks**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a hypervisor? Mention its two types.**

**Model Answer:**
A **hypervisor** (or Virtual Machine Monitor, VMM) is a software layer that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines (VMs) on a single physical host, enabling hardware resource sharing and isolation.

**Two Types:**
1. **Type 1 (Bare-Metal Hypervisor):** Runs directly on hardware without an underlying OS. Examples: VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Citrix XenServer.
2. **Type 2 (Hosted Hypervisor):** Runs as an application on a host OS. Examples: Oracle VirtualBox, VMware Workstation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks) + Two types (0.75 each = 1.5 marks) = **3 marks**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Internal Choice Pattern

### **Question Choice A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A. (i)** Explain in detail the **working of cloud computing** with a neat block diagram illustrating the front-end, middleware, and back-end components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The working of cloud computing can be understood as a coordinated three-layer architecture:

**1. Front-End Layer (Client Side)** — Contains the user device and application (browser/mobile app). The user initiates a request which is encrypted via TLS and transmitted over the Internet.

**2. Middleware Layer (Cloud Fabric)** — Comprises load balancers, authentication servers, API gateways, and metering modules. This layer routes, authenticates, and monitors every request.

**3. Back-End Layer (Provider Infrastructure)** — Houses the physical data center with servers, storage, and network hardware. A **hypervisor** virtualizes these physical resources into isolated VMs. A **cloud management platform** (e.g., OpenStack) orchestrates provisioning, scaling, and de-provisioning.

**Working Flow:**
- User → Front-end → Middleware (Auth + Load Balance) → Hypervisor → VM → Storage → Response → Metering → Billing.

A block diagram must show all five elements: **User, Network, Middleware, Hypervisor/VM, Storage, and Management.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Three-layer explanation (3 marks) + Step-by-step flow (2 marks) + Block diagram (2 marks) = **7 marks**

---

**A. (ii)** Compare and contrast the **three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)** in a tabular form. Which model is most suitable for a startup developing a mobile app with limited DevOps expertise? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **What you get** | Raw VMs, Storage, Network | Platform with OS, Runtime, Middleware | Ready-to-use software |
| **User controls** | OS, Apps, Data | Apps, Data | Only data |
| **Provider controls** | Hardware, Virtualization | Hardware, OS, Middleware | Everything |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, Google Compute | Heroku, Google App Engine | Gmail, Office 365 |
| **Flexibility** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Ease of use** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Cost** | Pay for resources | Pay for platform usage | Subscription-based |

**Best Choice for a Startup → SaaS or PaaS**

**Justification:**
- A startup building a mobile app has **limited DevOps staff** and wants to focus on product features, not infrastructure.
- **PaaS** (e.g., Firebase, Heroku) is ideal because it abstracts away server management, OS patching, and scaling — letting the startup deploy code with `git push`.
- **SaaS** is suitable only if the startup is *using* existing software (e.g., Slack for collaboration) rather than *building* one.
- **IaaS** is overkill for an early-stage startup and would require dedicated DevOps engineers, increasing burn rate.

**Recommendation:** **PaaS** is the optimal trade-off between control and convenience.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Comparison table (3 marks) + Choice identification (1 mark) + Justification (3 marks) = **7 marks**

---

### **Question Choice B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**B. (i)** With a neat diagram, explain the **role of virtualization in cloud computing**. Differentiate between **Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Virtualization:**
Virtualization is the **enabling technology** that allows a single physical server to host multiple isolated Virtual Machines (VMs), each with its own OS, applications, and resources. It enables:
1. **Server consolidation** (reduces hardware footprint).
2. **Resource pooling** across multiple tenants.
3. **Live migration** of VMs across physical hosts without downtime.
4. **Isolation and security** between tenants.
5. **Elastic scaling** by adding/removing VMs on demand.

**Diagram Requirement:** Draw a physical host at the bottom, a hypervisor layer in the middle, and 3–4 VMs at the top — each labeled with their tenant application (e.g., VM1 → Bank, VM2 → E-commerce, VM3 → University).

**Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisor:**

| Feature | Type 1 (Bare-Metal) | Type 2 (Hosted) |
|---|---|---|
| **Installation** | Directly on hardware | On top of a host OS |
| **Performance** | High (direct HW access) | Lower (double translation) |
| **Use Case** | Enterprise data centers, cloud | Development, testing, learning |
| **Examples** | VMware ESXi, Xen, Hyper-V | VirtualBox, VMware Workstation |
| **Stability** | Production-grade | Not recommended for production |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Virtualization role (3 marks) + Diagram (2 marks) + Type comparison (2 marks) = **7 marks**

---

**B. (ii)** A cloud provider advertises an **SLA of 99.95%**. Calculate the **maximum permissible annual downtime in hours and minutes**. If the same provider upgrades to **99.99%**, what is the **percentage reduction in downtime**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Annual Total Time**
$$
T = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Availability Formula for 99.95%**
$$
A_1 = 0.9995
$$
$$
D_1 = (1 - A_1) \times T = 0.0005 \times 8760
$$
$$
D_1 = 4.38 \text{ hours} = 4 \text{ hours } 22.8 \text{ minutes} \approx 4 \text{ hours } 23 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Downtime for 99.99%**
$$
A_2 = 0.9999
$$
$$
D_2 = (1 - A_2) \times T = 0.0001 \times 8760
$$
$$
D_2 = 0.876 \text{ hours} = 52.56 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4: Percentage Reduction in Downtime**
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{D_1 - D_2}{D_1} \times 100\%
$$
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{4.38 - 0.876}{4.38} \times 100\%
$$
$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{3.504}{4.38} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Moving from 99.95% to 99.99% reduces annual downtime by **80%** (from 4.38 hours to 52.56 minutes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Step 1 (1 mark) + Step 2 (2 marks) + Step 3 (2 marks) + Step 4 (2 marks) = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions in KTU ESE Papers:**
> 1. **Omitting the Hypervisor in diagrams** → loses **2 marks** in any "working of cloud" question. The hypervisor is the *bridge* between physical and virtual — examiners ALWAYS look for it.
> 2. **Confusing "Cloud Computing" with "Virtualization"** → Cloud = business model; Virtualization = enabling technology. Mixing them is a **1–2 mark** deduction.
> 3. **Writing "cloud = internet"** → The cloud is a *delivery model* for IT services over the internet, NOT the internet itself. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this loose definition.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numericals** → Always state hours/minutes/GB/Mbps. Missing units = **0.5 mark** deduction per question.
> 5. **Not mentioning "Pay-per-use" or "Measured Service"** in service model questions → loses **1 mark** as it is a NIST-mandated characteristic.
> 6. **Mermaid/Block diagram without arrows** → A diagram without labeled data flow arrows is considered "incomplete" and loses **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Cloud Computing = On-demand, ubiquitous access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (NIST).
- ✅ **Five Essential Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service.
- ✅ **Three Service Models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS (responsibility shifts from user → provider as you move up the stack).
- ✅ **Four Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- ✅ **Working Architecture:** Front-end (client) ↔ Middleware (auth, load balancer, metering) ↔ Back-end (hypervisor, VMs, storage, network).
- ✅ **Hypervisor is the HEART of cloud** — Type 1 (bare-metal) for production, Type 2 (hosted) for dev/test.
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **SLA Numbers to Remember:** 99% = 87.6 hr/yr, 99.9% = 8.76 hr/yr, 99.99% = 52.56 min/yr, 99.999% = 5.26 min/yr.
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy** = multiple users sharing the same physical resource pool, isolated via VMs/containers.
- ✅ **Virtualization** = the technology that makes cloud possible; **Cloud** = the business/service delivery model built on top.
- ✅ **Pay-per-use billing** is enabled by the **metering module** in the middleware layer.
- ✅ **Live migration** = moving a running VM between hosts with zero downtime (key cloud feature).
- ✅ **CDN, Load Balancer, API Gateway, Identity Provider** = essential middleware components.
- ✅ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** in 7+ mark questions — it is worth **2 marks** minimum.
- ✅ **For numericals:** Convert SLA % to decimal first, then multiply by 8760 hours to get annual downtime.
- ✅ **NIST Reference Architecture** is the gold standard for cloud definitions in KTU exams.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "O.B.R.R.M":**
> **O**n-demand **B**road access **R**esource pooling **R**apid elasticity **M**easured service — the five characteristics, in order.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing Architecture and Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing Architecture and Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cloud Computing Architecture** is the structural and logical composition of cloud-based systems, encompassing the front-end platform (client interface), the back-end platform (servers, storage, services), the network connecting them, and the cloud-based delivery of on-demand computing resources over the internet with pay-per-use pricing economics.

According to the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Special Publication 800-145**, cloud computing is defined as:

> *"A model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction."*

The **Five Essential Characteristics** of cloud computing as defined by NIST are:
1. **On-demand self-service**
2. **Broad network access**
3. **Resource pooling**
4. **Rapid elasticity**
5. **Measured service**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to differentiate between the three service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) and four deployment models (Public, Private, Hybrid, Community) with real-world examples. These appear frequently in Part A questions worth 3 marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**The Electricity Grid Analogy:**

Imagine cloud computing like the **electricity grid** in your home:

| Cloud Computing | Electricity Grid Analogy |
|---|---|
| Cloud Provider (AWS, Azure) | Power Plant |
| Virtual Machines / Servers | Electricity transmitted via wires |
| Pay-per-use pricing | Paying only for units (kWh) consumed |
| Elasticity / Scalability | Drawing more or less current as needed |
| Internet (broadband) | Power lines delivering electricity |

Just as you don't build your own power plant to watch television, you shouldn't need to build your own datacenter to host a web application. You simply **plug in**, pay for what you use, and the electricity (computing power) flows on demand.

Another intuition: Think of cloud computing as **renting a fully furnished apartment** versus **buying a house**. You don't worry about plumbing, structural maintenance, or buying furniture — you just move in and use what you need. The landlord (cloud provider) handles the infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The core shift in cloud computing is from **CAPEX (Capital Expenditure)** — buying hardware upfront — to **OPEX (Operational Expenditure)** — paying only for what you consume, when you consume it.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **standardized industry benchmarks** used in cloud architecture design:

- **Service Level Agreement (SLA):** Typically **99.9% ("three nines")** to **99.99% ("four nines")** availability
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Often **> 10,000 hours** for enterprise-grade cloud servers
- **Mean Time To Recovery (MTTR):** Industry target **< 1 hour** for critical services
- **RTO (Recovery Time Objective):** Range from seconds to 24 hours depending on tier
- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective):** Data loss tolerance from zero to several hours
- **CPU Virtualization Ratio (vCPU:pCPU):** Typically **4:1 to 16:1**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cloud Computing Architecture — Component Breakdown

The cloud architecture is broadly divided into **two interconnected halves** that communicate over a network (typically the internet or a private WAN).

### 2.1.1 Front-End (Client Side)

The front-end is what the user directly interacts with. It includes:

- **Client Devices:** Desktop PCs, laptops, tablets, smartphones, IoT devices
- **Client Operating Systems:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS
- **Client-Side Applications:** Web browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge), thin clients, thick clients, mobile apps
- **Network Interface:** The communication layer (HTTP/HTTPS, REST APIs, gRPC)

### 2.1.2 Back-End (Cloud Provider Side)

The back-end is the powerhouse that delivers the cloud services. It includes:

- **Application Layer:** SaaS applications, web services, microservices
- **Platform Layer:** OS, runtime environments, middleware, development tools (PaaS offerings)
- **Infrastructure Layer:** Virtual machines, physical servers, storage arrays, network equipment (IaaS offerings)
- **Virtualization Layer:** Hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM, Hyper-V) and containers (Docker, Kubernetes)
- **Storage Systems:** Block storage, object storage (S3), file storage (EFS, NFS)
- **Security & Management:** Identity management (IAM), monitoring (CloudWatch, Azure Monitor), orchestration

### 2.1.3 Network (The Communication Bridge)

The network layer connects front-end to back-end using:
- **Internet Protocol Suite (TCP/IP)**
- **Edge networks and CDNs (Content Delivery Networks)** like CloudFront, Akamai
- **Software-Defined Networking (SDN)** for dynamic routing
- **Load balancers** (L4 and L7) for traffic distribution

## 2.2 The Three Service Models — Explained Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The "Pizza as a Service" analogy is the most effective way to remember these layers — it appears in nearly every KTU semester question paper.

| Service Model | Pizza Analogy | What You Manage | What Provider Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service) | Make pizza at home (buy dough, sauce, cheese) | OS, Runtime, Applications, Data | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine |
| **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) | Order pizza to your door (oven and process handled) | Applications, Data | Runtime, Middleware, OS, Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Google App Engine |
| **SaaS** (Software as a Service) | Eat pizza at a restaurant (everything done for you) | Nothing (just use it) | Everything | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Dropbox |

### Why These Layers Exist — The 'Why' and 'How'

- **Why abstraction?** Each layer abstracts away the complexity of the layer below, allowing developers to focus on what matters most to them.
- **How does it work?** Through **virtualization** (creating virtual versions of physical resources) and **APIs** (Application Programming Interfaces) that allow programmatic access to resources.

## 2.3 The Four Deployment Models

| Deployment Model | Description | Use Case | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public Cloud** | Owned by third-party provider, open to public | Startups, variable workloads | AWS, Azure, GCP |
| **Private Cloud** | Used exclusively by single organization | Banking, defense, regulated industries | OpenStack, VMware vSphere on-premise |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Combination of public + private with orchestration | Burst capacity, disaster recovery | AWS Outposts, Azure Arc |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., compliance) | Government agencies, healthcare consortiums | GovCloud |

## 2.4 Key Enabling Technologies

The cloud architecture is powered by several underlying technologies that students must understand:

1. **Virtualization:** Creating virtual instances of physical resources using a hypervisor. **Type-1 (Bare-metal):** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V. **Type-2 (Hosted):** VirtualBox, VMware Workstation.

2. **Containerization:** Lightweight OS-level virtualization. Tools: **Docker** (runtime), **Kubernetes** (orchestration).

3. **Microservices Architecture:** Decomposing applications into small, independent services that communicate via APIs.

4. **Serverless Computing:** Executing functions in response to events without managing servers. Examples: **AWS Lambda**, **Azure Functions**.

5. **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA):** Predecessor to microservices, uses coarse-grained services over a network.

6. **Distributed Computing:** Coordinating multiple computers to solve a single problem (e.g., MapReduce, Hadoop).

7. **Blockchain Integration:** Emerging pattern for trustless distributed ledgers in cloud environments.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** For architecture topics, exam questions often test your ability to **compare and contrast** models. Memorize the differentiation axes.

### 2.5.1 Availability Calculation

The percentage of time a system is operational and accessible:

$$Availability = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$$

### 2.5.2 SLA "Nines" Mapping

| Availability % | Downtime per Year | Downtime per Month |
|---|---|---|
| 99% (two nines) | 3.65 days | 7.20 hours |
| 99.9% (three nines) | 8.76 hours | 43.20 minutes |
| 99.99% (four nines) | 52.56 minutes | 4.32 minutes |
| 99.999% (five nines) | 5.26 minutes | 25.90 seconds |

### 2.5.3 Virtualization Overcommitment Ratio

$$Overcommitment\ Ratio = \frac{Total\ vCPUs\ Allocated}{Total\ Physical\ pCPUs\ Available}$$

A ratio of **4:1** is common in production environments, meaning you provision 4 virtual CPUs for every physical CPU available.

### 2.5.4 Storage Capacity Calculation

$$Total\ Storage = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Disk_{size_i} \times Replication\ Factor_i)$$

For example, **3x replication** (HDFS default) means a 1 TB logical dataset requires 3 TB of physical storage.

### 2.5.5 Cost-Per-Use Economics

$$Total\ Cost = \sum_{t=0}^{T} (Resource_{unit\ cost}(t) \times Consumption(t))$$

The **pay-per-use** model means cost is a function of time and consumption, not a flat licensing fee.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cloud architecture patterns are used in:

- **Netflix:** Migrated from monolithic to microservices on AWS, achieving **99.99% availability** for 200+ million global subscribers.
- **Spotify:** Uses Google Cloud BigQuery and Kubernetes for music recommendation at scale.
- **NASA:** Leverages AWS for processing petabytes of satellite imagery (e.g., Mars rover data).
- **Banking Sector:** Hybrid cloud with private cloud for sensitive data + public cloud for analytics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Service and Deployment Models

> [!NOTE]
> This analytical matrix is a high-yield structure used in KTU 14-mark questions where students are asked to "compare and contrast" two or more cloud models.

### 3.1.1 IaaS vs. PaaS vs. SaaS — Multi-Parameter Comparison

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Level** | Lowest (raw infrastructure) | Medium (platform + tools) | Highest (ready-to-use software) |
| **User Responsibility** | Manage OS, apps, data | Manage apps and data | Just use the software |
| **Provider Responsibility** | Hardware, network, storage | All infrastructure + OS + middleware | Everything |
| **Flexibility** | Very high | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Time to Deploy** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours | Instant |
| **Skill Required** | Sysadmin, DevOps | Developer | End-user |
| **Cost Model** | Per VM-hour | Per app/DB usage | Per user/month |
| **Scalability** | Manual / scripted | Auto-scaling built-in | Automatic |
| **Example** | AWS EC2, Azure VM | Google App Engine, Heroku | Gmail, Office 365 |
| **Best For** | System administrators | Application developers | End users / business users |

### 3.1.2 Deployment Models — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Public | Private | Hybrid | Community |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ownership** | Third-party | Single org | Mixed | Multiple orgs (shared) |
| **Cost** | Lowest (shared infra) | Highest (dedicated) | Moderate | Shared cost |
| **Security** | Lower (shared) | Highest (controlled) | High (configurable) | Moderate-High |
| **Scalability** | Virtually unlimited | Limited by own capacity | High (burst to public) | Limited by member pool |
| **Control** | Least | Most | Balanced | Shared |
| **Compliance** | May not meet strict | Easily meets strict | Configurable | Common compliance |
| **Internet Dependency** | Required | Optional (on-prem) | Both | Optional |
| **Example** | AWS, Azure, GCP | OpenStack on-prem | AWS + on-prem | Healthcare consortium |

## 3.2 Detailed Working — How a Cloud Request Flows

Let us trace a **typical user request** through a cloud architecture, step by step:

### Step 1: User Initiates Request
The user opens a web browser and types `https://www.example.com`. This sends a **DNS query** to resolve the domain name to an IP address.

### Step 2: DNS Resolution
The DNS resolver returns the IP of the nearest **CDN edge node** (e.g., CloudFront edge). This minimizes latency through **geographic proximity**.

### Step 3: Edge Handling
The CDN edge checks its cache. If the requested content is cached, it returns it directly (**cache hit**). If not (**cache miss**), it forwards the request to the origin server.

### Step 4: Load Balancer
At the origin, an **L7 load balancer** (e.g., AWS ALB) receives the request. It applies routing rules and distributes traffic across multiple healthy backend instances using algorithms like:
- **Round Robin**
- **Least Connections**
- **Weighted Response Time**
- **IP Hash**

### Step 5: Application Server Processing
The selected application server (an EC2 instance or container) processes the request. It may need to:
- Authenticate the user via **IAM / OAuth / JWT**
- Query a database (e.g., **RDS, DynamoDB**)
- Call an internal microservice via **REST API or gRPC**

### Step 6: Database Layer
The database query is routed to a primary node for writes or a read replica for reads. Results are returned to the application server.

### Step 7: Response Assembly
The application server formats the HTTP response (often as JSON) and returns it up the chain.

### Step 8: Caching and Delivery
The response passes back through the load balancer, the CDN edge caches the response for future requests, and finally, the user's browser renders the page.

## 3.3 Mathematical Derivation — Availability of Multi-Component System

In a distributed cloud system, total availability depends on the availability of each component. Let us derive the availability for a **series system** and a **parallel system**.

### 3.3.1 Series System (Failure of any one component fails the system)

For two components in series with individual availabilities $A_1$ and $A_2$:

$$A_{series} = A_1 \times A_2$$

**Derivation:**
Probability that the system is up = Probability component 1 is up AND component 2 is up.
Since these are independent events, by the multiplication rule of probability:

$$P(System\ Up) = P(C_1\ Up) \times P(C_2\ Up)$$

$$A_{series} = A_1 \times A_2$$

For $n$ components in series:

$$A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$$

### 3.3.2 Parallel System (System fails only if ALL components fail)

For two components in parallel:

$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_1) \times (1 - A_2)$$

**Derivation:**
The system fails only if BOTH components fail simultaneously.
Probability of failure of component 1 = $(1 - A_1)$
Probability of failure of component 2 = $(1 - A_2)$
Probability of system failure = $(1 - A_1) \times (1 - A_2)$ (independent events)
Therefore:

$$A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$$

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example — Web Application

Consider a web app with three components in series:
- Load Balancer availability: $A_1 = 0.999$
- App Server availability: $A_2 = 0.995$
- Database availability: $A_3 = 0.999$

Total series availability:

$$A_{total} = 0.999 \times 0.995 \times 0.999$$

$$A_{total} = 0.993006 \approx 99.30\%$$

To improve this, we add **redundancy** — a second database instance in parallel:

$$A_{db\ parallel} = 1 - (1 - 0.999) \times (1 - 0.999) = 1 - 0.000001 = 0.999999$$

New total:

$$A_{new} = 0.999 \times 0.995 \times 0.999999 \approx 99.40\%$$

**Insight:** Redundancy in the database increased overall availability from 99.30% to 99.40%, demonstrating why cloud providers always recommend **multi-AZ (Availability Zone) deployments**.

## 3.4 Cloud Stack Layering — Stack Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Examiners frequently ask students to draw or describe the cloud stack. The most common version is the SPI stack (SaaS-PaaS-IaaS).

| Layer | Component | Example Provider Offering | User Activity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1: Hardware** | Servers, storage, network | AWS data centers | None (provider only) |
| **L2: Virtualization** | Hypervisor | VMware ESXi, KVM | None (provider only) |
| **L3: IaaS** | VMs, raw storage, networks | EC2, S3, VPC | Deploy OS, configure network |
| **L4: PaaS** | Runtime, DB, middleware | RDS, Beanstalk, App Engine | Deploy code only |
| **L5: SaaS** | Complete applications | Gmail, Salesforce, Slack | Use the app |
| **L6: Client** | Web browser, mobile app | Chrome, Safari | Interact with UI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Cloud Computing Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the complete cloud computing architecture, showing the front-end, back-end, and the network communication layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClientSide["FRONT END CLIENT SIDE"]
        direction TB
        userA[End User]
        devA[Mobile Device]
        devB[Desktop Browser]
        devC[Thin Client Terminal]
        userA --> devA
        userA --> devB
        userA --> devC
    end

    subgraph NetworkLayer["NETWORK LAYER INTERNET"]
        direction TB
        net1[Firewall and WAF]
        net2[CDN Edge Node]
        net3[Load Balancer L7]
        net1 --> net2
        net2 --> net3
    end

    subgraph BackEnd["BACK END CLOUD PROVIDER SIDE"]
        direction TB
        subgraph AppLayer["Application Layer"]
            app1[SaaS Web Apps]
            app2[Microservices APIs]
        end
        subgraph PlatLayer["Platform Layer PaaS"]
            plat1[App Engines]
            plat2[Database Services]
            plat3[Message Queues]
        end
        subgraph InfraLayer["Infrastructure Layer IaaS"]
            infra1[Virtual Machines]
            infra2[Object Storage]
            infra3[Container Clusters]
        end
        subgraph VirtLayer["Virtualization Layer"]
            virt1[Hypervisor Type 1]
            virt2[Container Engine Docker]
        end
        subgraph PhysLayer["Physical Hardware"]
            phys1[Rack Servers]
            phys2[SSD Disk Arrays]
            phys3[Network Switches]
        end
        AppLayer --> PlatLayer
        PlatLayer --> InfraLayer
        InfraLayer --> VirtLayer
        VirtLayer --> PhysLayer
    end

    subgraph MgmtLayer["MANAGEMENT AND SECURITY"]
        direction TB
        mgmt1[Identity and Access Mgmt]
        mgmt2[Monitoring and Logging]
        mgmt3[Billing and Metering]
        mgmt4[Auto Scaling Orchestrator]
    end

    ClientSide --> NetworkLayer
    NetworkLayer --> BackEnd
    MgmtLayer -.monitors.-> BackEnd
    MgmtLayer -.manages.-> NetworkLayer
```

## 4.2 Service Models Comparison — Block Architecture

The following functional block diagram compares the three service models in terms of **who manages what** at each stack level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StackL["Cloud Stack Layers"]
        direction TB
        L6[L6 Application Data]
        L5[L5 Applications]
        L4[L4 Runtime and Middleware]
        L3[L3 Operating System]
        L2[L2 Virtualization]
        L1[L1 Servers Storage Network]
    end

    subgraph IaaS_Box["IaaS Block"]
        iaas1[User Manages]
        iaas2[Provider Manages]
    end

    subgraph PaaS_Box["PaaS Block"]
        paas1[User Manages]
        paas2[Provider Manages]
    end

    subgraph SaaS_Box["SaaS Block"]
        saas1[User Manages]
        saas2[Provider Manages]
    end

    L6 --> IaaS_Box
    L6 --> PaaS_Box
    L6 --> SaaS_Box
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** In IaaS, the user manages Layers 3 through 6. In PaaS, the user manages only Layers 5 and 6. In SaaS, the user manages only Layer 6 (their data). Everything below is the provider's responsibility.

## 4.3 Cloud Deployment Models — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DeployModels["CLOUD DEPLOYMENT MODELS"]
        direction TB
        D1[PUBLIC CLOUD]
        D2[PRIVATE CLOUD]
        D3[HYBRID CLOUD]
        D4[COMMUNITY CLOUD]

        D1 --> D1a[Open to general public]
        D1 --> D1b[Owned by provider]
        D1 --> D1c[Example AWS Azure GCP]

        D2 --> D2a[Single organization only]
        D2 --> D2b[On premise or hosted]
        D2 --> D2c[Example OpenStack VMware]

        D3 --> D3a[Public plus Private combined]
        D3 --> D3b[Burst capacity to public]
        D3 --> D3c[Example AWS Outposts]

        D4 --> D4a[Shared by specific community]
        D4 --> D4b[Common policy or mission]
        D4 --> D4c[Example Government Cloud]
    end
```

## 4.4 Request Flow Sequence — Cloud Web Application

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[User Browser] -->|HTTPS request| B[DNS Resolver]
    B -->|IP of nearest edge| C[CDN Edge]
    C -->|Cache miss| D[Load Balancer]
    D -->|Round robin| E[App Server 1]
    D -->|Round robin| F[App Server 2]
    E -->|Auth check IAM| G[Auth Service]
    F -->|Auth check IAM| G
    E -->|Query data| H[Primary Database]
    F -->|Query data| I[Read Replica]
    H -->|Replica sync| I
    E -->|JSON response| D
    F -->|JSON response| D
    D -->|Cache at edge| C
    C -->|Render page| A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked to "explain the cloud architecture with a diagram" in a 14-mark question, this kind of labeled block diagram with request flow is worth **6-7 marks by itself**. Combine it with a written explanation for full marks.
