# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Cloud Computing and list its essential characteristics.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

Cloud computing is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

**Five Essential Characteristics (NIST):**
1. On-demand self-service
2. Broad network access
3. Resource pooling
4. Rapid elasticity
5. Measured service

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for listing the five characteristics)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS with one example each.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Infrastructure as a Service | Platform as a Service | Software as a Service |
| **User Manages** | OS, applications, data | Applications, data | Nothing (just data) |
| **Example** | AWS EC2 | Google App Engine | Gmail |
| **Use Case** | Hosting custom applications | Application development | End-user productivity |

*(3 marks — 1 mark per service model with example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write IaaS/PaaS/SaaS in a single table without explaining. Examiners award marks for **explicit comparison** using parameters.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Skipping the diagram in "Explain cloud architecture" questions. A labeled diagram carries **4-5 marks by itself**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not providing real-world provider names (AWS, Azure, GCP) — this shows lack of practical awareness and costs **1-2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up "deployment models" (Public/Private/Hybrid) with "service models" (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS). They are orthogonal concepts.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain the cloud computing architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss the roles of front-end, back-end, and network. [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Architecture**
Cloud computing architecture refers to the components and subcomponents required for cloud computing. It combines two components — front end and back end — connected via a network.

**Step 2: Front-End (Client Side) — 2 marks**
The front end is the user-facing portion. It includes:
- **Client devices:** PCs, laptops, tablets, smartphones, IoT devices
- **Operating systems:** Windows, macOS, Linux, Android
- **Client applications:** Web browsers (Chrome, Firefox), mobile apps, thin clients
- **User interface:** The graphical UI through which users interact with cloud services

**Step 3: Back-End (Cloud Provider Side) — 3 marks**
The back end consists of:
- **Application layer:** SaaS apps, microservices
- **Platform layer:** PaaS offerings (app engines, databases, middleware)
- **Infrastructure layer:** IaaS — virtual machines, storage, networking
- **Virtualization layer:** Hypervisors (VMware ESXi, KVM) and container runtimes (Docker)
- **Physical hardware:** Rack servers, SSD arrays, switches, routers
- **Storage:** Object storage (S3), block storage (EBS), file storage (EFS)
- **Security & management:** IAM, monitoring, billing

**Step 4: Network Layer — 1 mark**
The network (Internet or private WAN) uses TCP/IP, HTTP/HTTPS, and is enhanced by CDNs, load balancers, and SDN for routing and traffic distribution.

**Step 5: Labeled Diagram — 1 mark**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram — the Mermaid block diagram of front-end, network, and back-end layers with management overlay.)*

---

#### Part (b): Compare and contrast the three service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS) using a suitable analogy and a comparison table covering at least 6 parameters. [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Pizza as a Service Analogy — 2 marks**

This classic analogy maps cloud service models to pizza preparation:

- **On-Premises:** You make pizza from scratch — buy ingredients, knead dough, bake it yourself. *Equivalent to running your own data center.*
- **IaaS:** You buy ready-made dough, sauce, and cheese from the store and bake it at home. *You control the oven and toppings, but the heavy lifting (dough preparation) is outsourced.* This is like AWS EC2 — you bring your OS and applications but rent the infrastructure.
- **PaaS:** You order a pizza delivered to your home. The oven, ingredients, and cooking are all handled. *You just provide the address and consume the pizza.* This is like Heroku — you bring your code, the platform handles the runtime, OS, and infrastructure.
- **SaaS:** You go to a restaurant and eat pizza. *Everything is done for you.* This is like Gmail — you just use the application; the provider manages everything.

**Step 2: Comparison Table — 4 marks**

| Parameter | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction Level** | Low (raw VMs) | Medium (platform) | High (ready app) |
| **User Responsibility** | OS, runtime, app, data | App and data | Just data |
| **Flexibility** | Very high | Moderate | Low |
| **Time to Deploy** | Hours to days | Minutes to hours | Instant |
| **Example Provider** | AWS EC2, Azure VM | Heroku, App Engine | Gmail, Office 365 |
| **Target User** | Sysadmins, DevOps | Developers | End users |

**Step 3: Conclusion — 1 mark**
The choice of service model depends on the user's technical expertise, control requirements, and time-to-market constraints. IaaS offers maximum control, PaaS offers developer productivity, and SaaS offers end-user convenience.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a): Describe the four deployment models of cloud computing with examples. Under what circumstances would you choose a hybrid cloud over a public cloud? [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Public Cloud — 1.5 marks**
Public cloud is owned and operated by a third-party provider and delivers computing resources over the public internet. Resources are shared across multiple tenants (multi-tenant architecture).
- **Examples:** Amazon Web Services (AWS), Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
- **Use case:** Startups, variable workloads, web hosting

**Step 2: Private Cloud — 1.5 marks**
Private cloud is used exclusively by a single organization. It can be hosted on-premises (in the organization's own data center) or by a third party with restricted access.
- **Examples:** OpenStack, VMware vSphere, IBM Private Cloud
- **Use case:** Banking, defense, government agencies with strict compliance

**Step 3: Hybrid Cloud — 1.5 marks**
Hybrid cloud combines public and private clouds, allowing data and applications to be shared between them. It provides orchestration, integration, and burst capacity.
- **Examples:** AWS Outposts, Azure Arc, Google Anthos
- **Use case:** Organizations needing to keep sensitive data on-premises while leveraging public cloud for analytics

**Step 4: Community Cloud — 1 mark**
Community cloud is shared by several organizations with common concerns such as regulatory compliance, jurisdiction, or mission.
- **Examples:** Government community clouds (GovCloud), healthcare consortiums
- **Use case:** Government agencies, hospitals sharing common compliance

**Step 5: When to Choose Hybrid over Public — 1.5 marks**
Choose **hybrid cloud** when:
1. **Data sovereignty:** Some data must remain on-premises for legal/compliance reasons
2. **Burst capacity:** Need to handle unpredictable spikes by bursting to public cloud
3. **Disaster recovery:** Public cloud acts as a backup site for the private cloud
4. **Legacy integration:** On-premises systems must remain connected for gradual migration

Choose **public cloud** when cost is the primary concern, the workload is not regulated, and the team lacks infrastructure management expertise.

---

#### Part (b): Explain the NIST cloud computing reference architecture. List and briefly explain the five essential characteristics of cloud computing. [7 marks]

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: NIST Reference Architecture Overview — 1.5 marks**
The NIST SP 500-292 "Cloud Computing Reference Architecture" defines a conceptual model that identifies major actors, activities, and functions in cloud computing. It has five major actors:

1. **Cloud Consumer:** The end user who uses cloud services
2. **Cloud Provider:** The entity that supplies cloud services (e.g., AWS)
3. **Cloud Auditor:** Conducts independent assessment of cloud services (security, performance, compliance)
4. **Cloud Broker:** Manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services, negotiating relationships between providers and consumers
5. **Cloud Carrier:** Provides connectivity and transport of cloud services (e.g., ISP networks)

**Step 2: Five Essential Characteristics — 4 marks** *(0.8 marks each)*

**(i) On-Demand Self-Service:** Consumers can provision computing capabilities (server time, storage) automatically without requiring human interaction with the service provider.

**(ii) Broad Network Access:** Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms used by heterogeneous client platforms (mobile, laptop, PDA).

**(iii) Resource Pooling:** The provider's resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model. Physical and virtual resources are dynamically assigned and reassigned according to consumer demand.

**(iv) Rapid Elasticity:** Capabilities can be elastically provisioned and released to scale rapidly outward and inward with demand. To the consumer, the capabilities appear unlimited and can be appropriated in any quantity at any time.

**(v) Measured Service:** Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at a level appropriate to the service (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts). Resource usage is monitored, controlled, and reported, providing transparency to both provider and consumer.

**Step 3: Practical Implications — 1.5 marks**
- The "measured service" characteristic is the foundation of the **pay-per-use pricing model**.
- "Rapid elasticity" enables **auto-scaling** — adding or removing VMs based on CPU utilization.
- "Resource pooling" leads to economies of scale, lowering costs for end users.
- "Broad network access" supports the modern BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) workforce.
- "On-demand self-service" eliminates provisioning delays — what took weeks now takes minutes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this just before entering the exam hall!**

### ✅ Definitions to Memorize Verbatim
- [ ] Cloud computing definition (NIST)
- [ ] The five essential characteristics
- [ ] IaaS, PaaS, SaaS full forms and one example each
- [ ] Four deployment models with examples

### ✅ Conceptual Distinctions
- [ ] **Service models** (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) = **WHAT** is offered
- [ ] **Deployment models** (Public/Private/Hybrid/Community) = **WHERE** it is deployed
- [ ] These are **orthogonal** — a SaaS application can run on a hybrid deployment

### ✅ The Pizza as a Service Analogy
- [ ] On-Premises = Make pizza from scratch
- [ ] IaaS = Buy dough, bake at home
- [ ] PaaS = Pizza delivered to door
- [ ] SaaS = Eat at restaurant

### ✅ Key Numbers to Remember
- [ ] **99.9% (three nines)** = 8.76 hours downtime/year
- [ ] **99.99% (four nines)** = 52.56 minutes downtime/year
- [ ] **AWS S3** = object storage launched 2006
- [ ] **EC2** = Elastic Compute Cloud (IaaS flagship)

### ✅ Real-World Provider Mapping
- [ ] IaaS examples: **EC2, Azure VM, GCP Compute Engine**
- [ ] PaaS examples: **Heroku, Google App Engine, Elastic Beanstalk**
- [ ] SaaS examples: **Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce, Slack, Dropbox**
- [ ] Hybrid: **AWS Outposts, Azure Stack, Google Anthos**

### ✅ NIST Reference Architecture Actors
- [ ] **Consumer** (uses services)
- [ ] **Provider** (supplies services)
- [ ] **Auditor** (security/compliance assessment)
- [ ] **Broker** (intermediary)
- [ ] **Carrier** (network connectivity)

### ✅ Diagram Skills
- [ ] Always draw a **labeled block diagram** for any "explain architecture" question
- [ ] Use **rectangular boxes for components** and **arrows for data flow**
- [ ] Mark which side is **user-managed** vs **provider-managed** in service model comparisons

### ✅ Common Exam Traps
- [ ] Don't confuse **elasticity** (scale up/down) with **scalability** (ability to scale)
- [ ] Don't write "Cloud = Internet" — the **internet is a transport mechanism**, not the cloud itself
- [ ] Don't forget **multi-tenancy** when discussing public cloud
- [ ] Remember that **community cloud** is for organizations with **common concerns**, not general public

### ✅ High-Value Phrases to Use in Answers
- *"The shift from CAPEX to OPEX..."*
- *"Multi-tenant architecture with resource pooling..."*
- *"Pay-per-use metering enables cost transparency..."*
- *"Rapid elasticity provides the illusion of infinite resources..."*

topics.title: Cloud Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Cloud Architecture** refers to the structured composition of hardware, software, networking resources, and service components that together deliver on-demand, scalable, and reliable cloud computing services to end-users. It is a **multi-layered engineering blueprint** that defines how compute, storage, networking, virtualization, and service management components are interconnected, abstracted, and orchestrated to provide elastic, metered, and self-service capabilities over a network (typically the Internet).

According to the NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) reference model, Cloud Architecture comprises **five essential characteristics** (On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service), **three service models** (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), and **four deployment models** (Private, Public, Hybrid, Community).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Focus:** Cloud Architecture is a high-weightage topic in Module 1. Questions typically ask students to *draw and label the layered cloud architecture diagram*, distinguish between service models, and explain the role of virtualization.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Cloud Architecture** like the design of a massive **hotel chain** — let's call it "CloudStay Hotels":

- **The Building Infrastructure (Physical Layer):** Just as a hotel has buildings, rooms, and electricity, the cloud has physical servers, data centers, power supplies, and cooling systems.
- **The Room Allocation System (Virtualization Layer):** A hotel manager can split one large suite into smaller rentable rooms. Similarly, virtualization splits one physical server into multiple **Virtual Machines (VMs)**.
- **The Front Desk Services (Service Layer):** Depending on what a guest needs, the hotel offers: just the room (IaaS — you cook your own food), room + meals (PaaS — you bring your app, hotel hosts it), or full hospitality (SaaS — you just enjoy the stay).
- **The Reservation System (Orchestration & Management Layer):** Software that automatically books rooms based on demand. This is **auto-scaling** in the cloud.
- **The Billing Counter (Metering Layer):** You pay only for the nights you stayed. The cloud's **pay-per-use** model is identical.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Just as a hotel's architecture decides how guests experience their stay, Cloud Architecture decides how developers and businesses experience computing — abstraction, elasticity, and cost-efficiency.

### Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis)

- **5** — Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing
- **3** — Service Models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)
- **4** — Deployment Models
- **99.9% (Three-Nines)** — Standard cloud SLA availability target
- **99.99% (Four-Nines)** — Premium tier cloud SLA
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### A. The Layered Reference Architecture of Cloud Computing

Cloud Architecture is conventionally represented as a **stack of logical layers**, each encapsulating a distinct functional responsibility. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following standard layered model:

#### Layer 1 — Physical Layer (Hardware Infrastructure)
- Comprises actual **data centers**, server racks, network switches, storage arrays, and cooling/power systems.
- Modern hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, GCP) operate **geographically distributed data centers** organized into **Regions** and **Availability Zones (AZs)**.
- **High-density computing** and **energy-efficient designs** (PUE — Power Usage Effectiveness) are key engineering concerns.

#### Layer 2 — Virtualization Layer
- The **abstraction engine** of cloud computing.
- Uses a **Hypervisor** (Type-1: bare-metal like VMware ESXi, Xen, KVM; Type-2: hosted like VirtualBox) to create multiple isolated VMs on one physical machine.
- Enables **multi-tenancy** — multiple users sharing the same physical hardware with logical isolation.
- Modern alternative: **Containerization** (Docker, Kubernetes) provides OS-level virtualization, lighter and faster than VMs.

#### Layer 3 — Resource Pool Layer
- Aggregates compute, storage, and network resources into unified **pools** that can be dynamically allocated.
- **Resource Pooling** is one of NIST's 5 essential characteristics.
- Pools are sliced into **logical units** (vCPUs, virtual RAM, virtual disks) and assigned to tenants on demand.

#### Layer 4 — Service Orchestration / Middleware Layer
- The "brain" of the cloud — coordinates provisioning, deployment, scaling, and monitoring.
- Includes **Service Orchestrators** (e.g., OpenStack Heat, AWS CloudFormation) and **Workflow Engines**.
- Implements **SLA management**, **load balancing**, and **fault tolerance** policies.

#### Layer 5 — Service / Application Layer
- The user-facing tier exposing the three service models:
  - **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service): EC2, Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine.
  - **PaaS** (Platform as a Service): Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku.
  - **SaaS** (Software as a Service): Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce.

#### Layer 6 — User / Client Layer
- Interfaces through which consumers access services: **Web browsers, mobile apps, REST APIs, CLI tools**.

### B. The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Layer

| Layer | Why It Exists | How It Works |
|---|---|---|
| Physical | Real machines must exist somewhere | Geographically distributed data centers with redundant power/cooling |
| Virtualization | Maximize hardware utilization, enable isolation | Hypervisors abstract physical resources into VMs |
| Resource Pool | Enable on-demand, elastic allocation | Pooling of CPU, RAM, storage into shareable inventories |
| Orchestration | Automate complex multi-step provisioning | Policy-driven engines that deploy, scale, heal workloads |
| Service | Different users have different abstraction needs | Pre-packaged stacks for IaaS / PaaS / SaaS consumption |
| Client | Provide ubiquitous access | Standardized APIs, SDKs, and web portals |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Explain the layered architecture of cloud computing with a neat diagram."* — This is a **14-mark question**. Always draw all **6 layers**, label arrows showing request flow, and briefly explain the function of each layer.

### C. KTU High-Yield Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is your **one-page revision sheet** for the cloud architecture topic.

| **Concept** | **Definition** | **Example** | **Key Metric / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Virtualization | Abstracting physical resources into logical units | VMware, KVM, Hyper-V | VM density $\vert$ 1 physical : $N$ virtual |
| Multi-tenancy | Single instance serves multiple clients with isolation | Salesforce multi-tenant DB | Tenants per instance |
| Elasticity | Auto-scale resources based on workload | AWS Auto Scaling Group | Scale-out time (seconds) |
| Scalability | Handle increased load by adding resources | Horizontal: add VMs $\vert$ Vertical: add RAM | $O(n)$ provisioning time |
| Fault Tolerance | Continue functioning despite component failure | Replicas, multi-AZ deployment | $99.99\%$ uptime SLA |
| Load Balancing | Distribute traffic across multiple servers | Nginx, AWS ELB | Requests per second (RPS) |
| Service Orchestration | Automated coordination of cloud services | Kubernetes, Terraform | Deployment success rate |
| Metering | Measure resource usage for billing | CloudWatch, Azure Monitor | $USD \cdot vCPU \cdot hour^{-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In all LaTeX expressions above, the vertical bar $\vert$ is used instead of the pipe character $\vert$ to avoid corrupting markdown table syntax.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Architectural Analysis & Design Logic

### A. Derivation of the Cloud Service Model Stack — Layering Principle

The **Service Model Stack** is derived from a single architectural principle: **progressive abstraction**. Each successive layer hides the complexity of the layer beneath it, transferring more management responsibility from the user to the cloud provider.

Let us formally model the **Total Management Responsibility ($TMR$)** distributed between the Cloud Provider ($P$) and the User ($U$) across each service model.

$$
TMR_{model} = R_P + R_U = 1.0
$$

Where:
- $R_P$ = Fraction of responsibilities handled by the **Provider**
- $R_U$ = Fraction of responsibilities handled by the **User**
- $TMR_{model}$ = Total = 1.0 (normalized)

#### Step 1: Traditional On-Premises (No Cloud)
In on-premises, the user handles *everything*:

$$
R_P^{OnPrem} = 0.0, \quad R_U^{OnPrem} = 1.0
$$

#### Step 2: IaaS — User manages OS, runtime, and applications

$$
R_P^{IaaS} = \frac{\text{Hardware + Virtualization + Storage + Network}}{\text{Total Stack}} \approx 0.4
$$

$$
R_U^{IaaS} = 1.0 - R_P^{IaaS} = 0.6
$$

#### Step 3: PaaS — Provider additionally manages OS, middleware, and runtime

$$
R_P^{PaaS} = 0.4 + \frac{\text{OS + Middleware + Runtime}}{\text{Total Stack}} \approx 0.7
$$

$$
R_U^{PaaS} = 1.0 - R_P^{PaaS} = 0.3
$$

#### Step 4: SaaS — Provider manages everything except user data and access

$$
R_P^{SaaS} = 0.7 + \frac{\text{Application + Data + Access Control from Provider Side}}{\text{Total Stack}} \approx 0.95
$$

$$
R_U^{SaaS} = 1.0 - R_P^{SaaS} = 0.05
$$

#### Step 5: Consolidated Responsibility Distribution

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_P^{OnPrem} &< R_P^{IaaS} < R_P^{PaaS} < R_P^{SaaS} \\
0.0 &< 0.4 < 0.7 < 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

This monotonic progression is the **core mathematical justification** for the "stack" representation in cloud architecture diagrams.

### B. Derivation of Elasticity Efficiency Metric

**Elasticity** is a defining feature of cloud architecture. Let us define the **Elasticity Efficiency ($\eta$)** metric used in academic and industry cloud literature:

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{provision} - T_{idle}}{T_{provision}} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $T_{provision}$ = Total time a resource is provisioned (in hours)
- $T_{idle}$ = Time the resource remains idle/unused

For a perfectly elastic cloud where resources scale down to zero when not in use:

$$
\eta_{ideal} = \frac{T_{provision} - 0}{T_{provision}} = 1.0 = 100\%
$$

For a static on-premises server that is always on:

$$
\eta_{onPrem} = \frac{24h - 8h}{24h} = \frac{16}{24} \approx 66.67\%
$$

The **delta improvement** of cloud elasticity over on-premises:

$$
\Delta \eta = \eta_{ideal} - \eta_{onPrem} = 100\% - 66.67\% = 33.33\%
$$

This is the **engineering justification** for adopting cloud architecture from a cost-efficiency perspective.

### C. Step-by-Step Logical Flow of a Cloud Request

1. **User Login** → Authentication via Identity Provider (IdP).
2. **API Call** → User requests a resource (e.g., launch a VM) via REST API.
3. **Orchestrator** → Receives the request and consults the scheduler.
4. **Scheduler** → Identifies the optimal physical host using algorithms like **First-Fit**, **Best-Fit**, or **Ant Colony Optimization**.
5. **Hypervisor** → Creates a VM on the chosen host.
6. **Network Configuration** → Assigns IP, security groups, and load balancers.
7. **Service Activation** → VM is reachable; user is notified via API response.
8. **Metering Begins** → Usage is recorded for billing.
9. **Monitoring Loops** → Health checks; auto-scaling triggers if CPU > 80%.
10. **Termination** → User releases the resource; metering stops; VM is destroyed or recycled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When asked to "explain the working of cloud architecture," always structure your answer around these **10 logical steps** of the request lifecycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Layered Cloud Architecture Reference Model (6-Layer Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L6[LAYER 6 - USER CLIENT LAYER\nWeb Browsers, Mobile Apps, REST APIs, CLI Tools]
    L5[LAYER 5 - SERVICE APPLICATION LAYER\nSaaS Apps, PaaS Platforms, IaaS Virtual Servers]
    L4[LAYER 4 - ORCHESTRATION MIDDLEWARE LAYER\nService Orchestrators, SLA Managers, Auto Scalers, Load Balancers]
    L3[LAYER 3 - RESOURCE POOL LAYER\nCompute Pools, Storage Pools, Network Pools, Database Pools]
    L2[LAYER 2 - VIRTUALIZATION LAYER\nHypervisors Type 1 and Type 2, Container Engines, VM Managers]
    L1[LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL HARDWARE LAYER\nData Centers, Servers, Storage Arrays, Network Switches, Cooling Systems]

    L6 --> L5
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Arrows point **downward** to indicate the *abstraction direction* — each upper layer depends on and abstracts the layer below. A complete cloud service flows upward from the physical layer to the user.

### Diagram 2: Cloud Service Models — Responsibility Stack

```mermaid

    end
    subgraph IaaSBlock[IAAS SERVICE MODEL]

    end

    UserP[USER MANAGES]:::userstyle
    ProvP[PROVIDER MANAGES]:::provstyle

    UserP -.-> IaaSApp
    UserP -.-> IaaSData

    ProvP -.-> IaaSHW

    UserP -.-> PaaSApp

    classDef userstyle fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000
    classDef provstyle fill:#b0e0e6,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000
```

### Diagram 3: Functional Architecture Flow — Cloud Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER CLIENT] -->|REST API Call| B[AUTHENTICATION MODULE]
    B -->|Validated Token| C[ORCHESTRATION ENGINE]
    C -->|Resource Request| D[SCHEDULER]
    D -->|Host Selection Algorithm| E[PHYSICAL RESOURCE POOL]
    E -->|VM Provisioning| F[HYPERVISOR LAYER]
    F -->|VM Ready| G[NETWORK CONFIGURATION]
    G -->|IP and Security| H[SERVICE READY STATE]
    H -->|Notification| A
    H -->|Usage Telemetry| I[METERING AND BILLING MODULE]
    H -->|Health Metrics| J[MONITORING AND AUTO SCALER]
    J -->|Scale Decision| C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Conventions:** Always use **rectangular boxes** for components, **arrows** for data/request flow, and **labels on arrows** indicating the type of interaction. Mermaid `flowchart` syntax is the safest format for module 1 architecture questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Cloud Architecture and list its essential characteristics as per the NIST reference model.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Cloud Architecture is the structured composition of hardware, software, network, and service components designed to deliver on-demand, scalable, and reliable computing services over a network. As per the NIST model, its five essential characteristics are:

1. **On-demand self-service**
2. **Broad network access**
3. **Resource pooling**
4. **Rapid elasticity**
5. **Measured service**

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing all 5 characteristics.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **IaaS**, **PaaS**, and **SaaS** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| What is provided | Virtualized hardware | Platform for app development | Ready-to-use software |
| User manages | OS, runtime, apps, data | Apps and data only | Data and access only |
| Example | Amazon EC2 | Google App Engine | Microsoft Office 365 |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row.)*

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU 2024 ESE pattern — *Answer any ONE full question from Module 1.* All full questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **layered reference architecture of cloud computing** with a neat diagram. Describe the function of each layer.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The cloud architecture is best understood as a **6-layer reference model**, where each layer progressively abstracts the complexity of the layer below it.

**Layer 1 — Physical Hardware Layer:** Contains the actual data centers, server racks, storage arrays, network switches, and supporting infrastructure like power backup and cooling. Hyperscalers distribute these across multiple **Regions and Availability Zones** for fault tolerance.

**Layer 2 — Virtualization Layer:** Uses a **hypervisor** (Type-1 bare-metal: VMware ESXi, KVM; Type-2 hosted: VirtualBox) to create multiple isolated **Virtual Machines** on a single physical host. This layer enables **multi-tenancy** and maximizes hardware utilization. Modern systems also use **containerization** (Docker) as a lighter alternative.

**Layer 3 — Resource Pool Layer:** Aggregates compute, storage, and network resources into unified pools. These are dynamically sliced and assigned to tenants. Implements **resource pooling**, a NIST essential characteristic.

**Layer 4 — Orchestration Middleware Layer:** The "control brain" of the cloud. Manages automated provisioning, deployment, scaling, monitoring, and SLA enforcement. Tools include **OpenStack Heat, AWS CloudFormation, Kubernetes**.

**Layer 5 — Service Application Layer:** Exposes the three service models — **IaaS (EC2), PaaS (App Engine), SaaS (Gmail)** — to the consumer. Each model packages a different level of abstraction.

**Layer 6 — User Client Layer:** The access tier — web browsers, mobile apps, CLI tools, and REST API clients that consume cloud services.

*(Award 1 mark per layer explained + 1 mark for the diagram.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role of virtualization** in cloud architecture. Compare **Type-1 and Type-2 hypervisors**.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Role of Virtualization:**
Virtualization is the **core abstraction engine** of cloud architecture. It decouples software from hardware, enabling:
1. **Multi-tenancy** — multiple users sharing one physical machine with isolation.
2. **Resource optimization** — higher hardware utilization rates.
3. **Rapid provisioning** — VMs can be created in seconds.
4. **Live migration** — VMs can be moved between hosts with zero downtime.
5. **Hardware independence** — applications run on any compatible VM regardless of underlying hardware.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Type-1 Hypervisor** | **Type-2 Hypervisor** |
|---|---|---|
| Installation | Directly on bare metal | On top of a host OS |
| Performance | High (direct hardware access) | Lower (double translation) |
| Use case | Enterprise data centers, production clouds | Development, testing, learning |
| Examples | VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Xen, KVM | Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels, VMware Workstation |
| Security | More secure (no host OS attack surface) | Less secure (host OS compromise = guest compromise) |

**Conclusion:** Production cloud architectures (AWS, Azure) exclusively use **Type-1 hypervisors** for performance and isolation. Type-2 hypervisors are reserved for development environments.

*(Award 4 marks for role + 3 marks for comparison table.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)** with a diagram showing the responsibility split between provider and user.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The three service models represent different levels of abstraction and responsibility:

**1. IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service):**
- Provider supplies: Virtualized compute, storage, and network.
- User manages: Operating system, middleware, runtime, application, and data.
- Example: **Amazon EC2, Microsoft Azure VMs, Google Compute Engine.**
- Use case: When users want maximum control over their environment.

**2. PaaS (Platform as a Service):**
- Provider supplies: All of IaaS + OS + middleware + runtime + development tools.
- User manages: Application code and data only.
- Example: **Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk.**
- Use case: Rapid application development without managing infrastructure.

**3. SaaS (Software as a Service):**
- Provider supplies: Fully managed ready-to-use software application.
- User manages: User data, access credentials, and personal settings only.
- Example: **Gmail, Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Dropbox.**
- Use case: End-user productivity without any technical management.

**Responsibility Distribution (analytical representation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_P^{IaaS} &\approx 0.4 \\
R_P^{PaaS} &\approx 0.7 \\
R_P^{SaaS} &\approx 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

As we move from IaaS $\to$ PaaS $\to$ SaaS, the **provider's responsibility increases** and the **user's burden decreases**. The diagram should depict a stack where SaaS consumes all of PaaS, which consumes all of IaaS.

*(Award 2 marks per model + 1 mark for the layered diagram.)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four deployment models** of cloud computing. When would you choose a **hybrid cloud** over a pure public cloud?

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**1. Public Cloud:**
- Infrastructure is owned by a third-party provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) and shared across multiple tenants over the public Internet.
- **Pros:** Low cost, no maintenance, high scalability.
- **Cons:** Less control, data sovereignty concerns.

**2. Private Cloud:**
- Infrastructure is dedicated to a single organization, either on-premises or hosted by a third party.
- **Pros:** Maximum control, customization, security compliance.
- **Cons:** High capital expenditure, underutilization risk.

**3. Hybrid Cloud:**
- Combines public and private clouds with **orchestration** between them (e.g., **AWS Outposts, Azure Arc**).
- **Pros:** Flexibility, burst-to-cloud capability, data residency compliance.
- **Cons:** Complex integration, higher management overhead.

**4. Community Cloud:**
- Shared infrastructure for a specific community with shared concerns (e.g., government agencies, healthcare organizations).
- **Pros:** Shared cost, compliance with sectoral regulations.
- **Cons:** Limited scalability, governance complexity.

**When to Choose Hybrid over Pure Public:**

A **hybrid cloud** is preferred when:
1. **Data sensitivity:** Sensitive data must remain on private cloud; non-sensitive workloads burst to public cloud.
2. **Regulatory compliance:** Laws like GDPR or India's DPDP Act require certain data to stay within geographic boundaries.
3. **Legacy integration:** Existing on-premises applications cannot be easily migrated to the public cloud.
4. **Cost optimization:** Predictable baseline workloads run on cheaper private infrastructure; spikes burst to public.
5. **Disaster recovery:** Public cloud serves as a backup site for private cloud workloads.

*(Award 1.5 marks per deployment model + 1 mark for the "hybrid choice" justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to draw the diagram:** A 14-mark question without a diagram can lose **2–3 marks** even if the text is perfect. Always include a labeled Mermaid/hand-drawn diagram.
> 2. **Confusing the service models:** Many students mix up which layer the user manages in IaaS vs PaaS. Memorize: *IaaS = User manages OS; PaaS = User manages only App + Data; SaaS = User just uses it.*
> 3. **Skipping NIST definitions:** Examiners specifically look for the phrase **"as per the NIST reference model"** when defining cloud architecture. Omitting this phrasing can cost a mark.
> 4. **No real-world examples:** A 14-mark answer without AWS/Azure/GCP examples is considered incomplete.
> 5. **Writing in paragraph form for diagrams:** Always use **bulleted lists** with sub-headings — KTU board examiners skim, and structured answers fetch higher marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** Cloud Architecture = structured blueprint of components that deliver on-demand, scalable cloud services.
- [ ] **NIST 5 Characteristics:** On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service. *(Mnemonic: OBRM)*
- [ ] **3 Service Models:** IaaS, PaaS, SaaS — each shifts more responsibility to the provider.
- [ ] **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- [ ] **6-Layer Architecture:** Client $\to$ Service $\to$ Orchestration $\to$ Resource Pool $\to$ Virtualization $\to$ Physical.
- [ ] **Virtualization:** Core abstraction engine; **Type-1 hypervisors** are used in production clouds.
- [ ] **Key Metrics:** Availability (99.9% / 99.99% SLA), Elasticity Efficiency ($\eta$), Provider Responsibility Share ($R_P$).
- [ ] **Modern Trends:** Containers (Docker/Kubernetes) complementing VMs; Serverless computing extending PaaS.
- [ ] **Diagrams to memorize:** (i) 6-Layer Stack, (ii) Service Model Responsibility Stack, (iii) Cloud Request Lifecycle Flow.
- [ ] **Famous Examples:** AWS EC2 (IaaS), Google App Engine (PaaS), Gmail (SaaS).
- [ ] **Examiner Keywords to Use:** *abstraction, multi-tenancy, elasticity, on-demand, metered service, orchestration, hypervisor, SLA.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** In the 14-mark question, allocate **5 minutes for diagram + 20 minutes for text**. Always present answers in **bulleted sub-sections** with **bold keywords**. Use **examples (AWS, Azure, GCP)** liberally — they elevate the answer from "average" to "excellent."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Modelling and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cloud Modelling and Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Cloud Modelling and Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Modelling and Design** is the disciplined engineering process of abstracting, specifying, and orchestrating the logical, physical, and operational components of a cloud system so that it satisfies functional, non-functional (QoS), economic, and scalability requirements. It comprises two complementary sub-activities:
> - **Cloud Modelling**: Representing the cloud system structurally (entities, relationships, behaviour, constraints) using reference architectures, conceptual models, and mathematical/simulative abstractions.
> - **Cloud Design**: Translating those models into a concrete, deployable architecture that specifies service layers, deployment topology, resource provisioning, and interaction protocols.

The **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Cloud Computing Reference Model** is the canonical framework used worldwide (and endorsed by KTU 2024 syllabus) to model any cloud system. It identifies **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models**, and it defines the actor roles of **Cloud Provider**, **Cloud Consumer**, **Cloud Broker**, **Cloud Auditor**, and **Cloud Carrier**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Electricity Grid

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Cloud as a Public Utility (like electricity):**
> Imagine you are building a house. You do **not** dig your own coal mine, build a generator, lay transmission lines, and learn electrical engineering. You simply plug appliances into a wall socket, and the utility (provider) supplies metered power on demand, billed per unit consumed.
>
> In the same way, **Cloud Modelling** is the *blueprint* of the power grid (turbines, substations, cables, meters, regulations), and **Cloud Design** is the act of wiring a building to use the grid optimally. The cloud consumer only specifies *what is needed* (compute, storage, network), and the cloud provider *delivers it as a metered, on-demand service*.

### 1.3 The Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud (NIST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five characteristics **must** be present for a system to be classified as a true cloud:

| # | Characteristic | Definition | Plain-English Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **On-Demand Self-Service** | Consumer provisions compute/storage/network automatically without human interaction. | Like a vending machine — you insert a coin and get a soda instantly, no shopkeeper. |
| 2 | **Broad Network Access** | Capabilities are available over the network and accessed through standard mechanisms (HTTP, REST, TCP/IP). | Reach your data from a laptop, phone, or tablet anywhere. |
| 3 | **Resource Pooling** | Provider's resources are pooled to serve multiple consumers using a multi-tenant model. | A single big hotel serves many guests; rooms are dynamically assigned. |
| 4 | **Rapid Elasticity** | Capabilities can be scaled out/in rapidly and, to the consumer, appear unlimited. | A rubber band — stretch (scale up) when traffic spikes, snap back (scale down) when it ends. |
| 5 | **Measured Service** | Cloud systems automatically control and optimize resource use by leveraging metering at an appropriate level of abstraction (storage, processing, bandwidth, active user accounts). | Pay only for the water you actually drink, measured by a meter. |

### 1.4 The Three Service Models — What is being Delivered

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cloud "Pizza-as-a-Service" Analogy:**
> - **On-Premises** = You make the pizza yourself (buy ingredients, knead, bake).
> - **IaaS (Infrastructure-as-a-Service)** = You buy raw dough, sauce, cheese and bake it at home.
> - **PaaS (Platform-as-a-Service)** = A kitchen is provided; you just bring toppings and bake.
> - **SaaS (Software-as-a-Service)** = A hot pizza is delivered to your door — you just eat it.

| Service Model | What the Consumer Manages | What the Provider Manages | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SaaS** — Software-as-a-Service | Just uses the application via a thin client / browser. | Everything: app, data, runtime, OS, middleware, virtualization, servers, storage, network. | **Gmail, Microsoft 365, Salesforce, Zoom** |
| **PaaS** — Platform-as-a-Service | Application + data only. | Runtime, OS, middleware, virtualization, servers, storage, network. | **AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Google App Engine, Azure App Services, Heroku** |
| **IaaS** — Infrastructure-as-a-Service | Application, data, runtime, middleware, OS. | Virtualization, servers, storage, network. | **Amazon EC2, Google Compute Engine, Azure VMs, OpenStack** |

### 1.5 The Four Deployment Models — Where the Cloud Lives

| Deployment Model | Definition | Who Uses It |
|---|---|---|
| **Private Cloud** | Provisioned for exclusive use by a single organization. May be on-premise or off-premise. | Banks, defence, government |
| **Public Cloud** | Open for general public use; owned by a third-party provider (CSP). | Start-ups, SMEs, public apps |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Composition of two or more distinct cloud infrastructures (private + public) bound by standardized technology enabling data/app portability. | Enterprises with sensitive + public workloads |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by several organizations with shared concerns (mission, security, policy, compliance). | Healthcare consortium, university federations |

### 1.6 The Five Key Actors in Cloud (NIST Roles)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize for KTU exams — these roles are repeated almost every year.**

1. **Cloud Consumer** — Uses the cloud services (the person who plugs appliances into the socket).
2. **Cloud Provider** — Maintains the service; supplies the resources (the electricity board).
3. **Cloud Auditor** — Conducts independent assessment of cloud controls, security, performance (the electricity inspector).
4. **Cloud Broker** — Manages the use, performance, and delivery of cloud services; negotiates relationships between providers and consumers (a power-management consultant).
5. **Cloud Carrier** — Provides the network connectivity and transport of cloud services from providers to consumers (the transmission lines).

### 1.7 The Cloud Reference Model — Visual Layered View

The NIST reference model places the consumer's responsibilities at the top and the provider's responsibilities at the bottom, meeting at the **Service Layer** boundary (the "socket" or API).

> **Provider-Managed Layers (Bottom-Up):**
> Physical Resources → Virtualization Layer → Resource Pool → Cloud Software (Platform/Services)
>
> **Consumer-Managed Layers (Top-Down):**
> Application → Data → Runtime → Middleware → Operating System
>
> **The boundary** is the **Service Layer** (XaaS interface).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (CCRA)

> [!NOTE]
> The CCRA is the *de facto* architectural model used to "design" a cloud. It links every actor (from Section 1.6) to specific services and control activities.

The CCRA specifies the following logical components that a cloud system must provide:

1. **Service Deployment** — how a service is made available (front-end interface, e.g., web portal, CLI, API).
2. **Service Orchestration** — arranging, coordinating, and managing cloud resources to fulfil consumer requirements (the "conductor of the orchestra").
3. **Cloud Service Management** — all the business, operational, and administrative functions needed to manage the cloud environment (provisioning, monitoring, billing, SLA management).
4. **Security** — confidentiality, integrity, availability, authentication, authorisation, auditing (cross-cutting concern).
5. **Privacy** — proper handling of personally identifiable information (PII) — mandated by regulations like GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023.
6. **Portability & Interoperability** — ability to move workloads and data between providers (avoids vendor lock-in) — typically achieved through containers, Kubernetes, Terraform, multi-cloud APIs.

### 2.2 Design Principles of Cloud Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 Golden Rules of Cloud Design (frequently asked in KTU):**

| # | Principle | Meaning | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Loose Coupling** | Components interact through well-defined interfaces (APIs, message queues), not tight integration. | Allows independent scaling and failure isolation. |
| 2 | **Statelessness** | Application servers do not store session data locally; state is kept in DB/cache. | Any server can handle any request → easy horizontal scaling. |
| 3 | **Horizontal Scalability (Scale Out/In)** | Add/remove identical instances, not bigger boxes. | Cheaper and faster than vertical scaling. |
| 4 | **Asynchronous Communication** | Use message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) instead of synchronous RPC. | Prevents cascading failures; improves throughput. |
| 5 | **Elasticity / Auto-Scaling** | Resources scale automatically based on demand metrics. | Optimizes cost; handles traffic spikes. |
| 6 | **Redundancy & Fault Tolerance** | No single point of failure (SPOF). Multi-AZ deployment. | Ensures high availability (typically 99.99% SLA). |
| 7 | **Security by Design (Zero-Trust)** | Every request is authenticated/authorised; perimeter is not trusted. | Defends against lateral movement of attackers. |

### 2.3 Cloud Modelling Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> **Modelling** answers: *"What does the system look like?"*
> **Design** answers: *"How do we build it?"*

Cloud modelling typically uses three orthogonal viewpoints (per the **4+1 Architectural View Model** adapted for cloud):

| Viewpoint | Question Answered | Stakeholder | Artefact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Logical View** | What functions and services are offered? | Business / End-User | Service catalogue, UML component diagram |
| **Development View** | How is the system decomposed for build/test? | Developer | Module/class diagrams, microservices map |
| **Process / Runtime View** | How do components communicate at runtime? | Operations / DevOps | Sequence diagrams, data-flow diagrams |
| **Physical / Deployment View** | Where do components run? Which servers/regions/VMs? | Infrastructure Engineer | Deployment diagram, topology map |
| **Scenarios (Use Cases)** | How is the system used end-to-end? | All | Use-case diagrams, performance scenarios |

### 2.4 Workload Modelling and Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workload** is the resource demand placed on the cloud by the consumer application. Modelling workload is the first step in **capacity planning**.

**Common workload types:**
- **Static (Predictable)**: Uniform demand (e.g., internal payroll system).
- **Periodic**: Diurnal / weekly / monthly cycles (e.g., e-commerce peak at 8 PM).
- **Unpredictable / Spiky**: Sudden bursts (e.g., viral video, IPL match streaming).
- **Once-in-a-lifetime**: Batch jobs (e.g., year-end reporting).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cloud Cost, Capacity & Performance)

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **the most frequently tested derivations** in KTU Module 1.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables / Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Availability (per Service)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures (hours), $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair (hours) | Calculating SLA compliance |
| 2 | **Combined Availability (Series)** | $A_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | $A_i$ = availability of component $i$ | Components in dependency chain |
| 3 | **Combined Availability (Parallel/Redundant)** | $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - A_i)$ | $A_i$ = availability of redundant component $i$ | Fault-tolerant systems |
| 4 | **Total Downtime per Year** | $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ minutes | $A$ as a fraction (e.g., 0.9999) | SLA penalty calculation |
| 5 | **Capacity Requirement** | $C_{req} = \mu \times N_{users} \times k$ | $\mu$ = avg per-user demand, $N_{users}$ = peak users, $k$ = safety factor (1.2–1.5) | Sizing VMs/DBs |
| 6 | **Throughput (Little's Law)** | $L = \lambda \times W$ | $L$ = concurrent requests, $\lambda$ = arrival rate (req/s), $W$ = avg time in system (s) | Performance modelling |
| 7 | **Response Time (Queueing)** | $W = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ for M/M/1 queue | $\mu$ = service rate, $\lambda$ = arrival rate | Predicting latency under load |
| 8 | **Cost per Request (Pay-per-Use)** | $C_{req} = \dfrac{C_{resource} \times T_{usage}}{N_{requests}}$ | $C_{resource}$ = hourly cost, $T_{usage}$ = total hours, $N_{requests}$ = total requests | Cost-benefit analysis of cloud vs on-premise |
| 9 | **Storage Tier Cost (3-Tier)** | $C_{total} = c_h S_h + c_w S_w + c_c S_c$ | $c_h, c_w, c_c$ = cost/GB of hot/warm/cold, $S_h, S_w, S_c$ = data in each tier (GB) | Optimising storage bills |
| 10 | **Scaling Out (Horizontal)** | $N_{instances} = \left\lceil \dfrac{L_{peak}}{\mu_{single}} \right\rceil$ | $L_{peak}$ = peak load, $\mu_{single}$ = capacity of one instance | Determining auto-scaling limits |

> **SLA Tiers to Remember (KTU favourite):**
> - **99% (Two 9s)** → ~3.65 days downtime/year
> - **99.9% (Three 9s)** → ~8.76 hours downtime/year
> - **99.99% (Four 9s)** → ~52.6 minutes downtime/year
> - **99.999% (Five 9s)** → ~5.26 minutes downtime/year — used by AWS, Google core services.

### 2.6 Cloud Service Level Agreements (SLA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Service Level Agreement (SLA)** is a formal contract between cloud provider and consumer that defines:
> - **Service Level Objectives (SLOs)** — specific measurable targets (uptime %, latency ms).
> - **Service Level Indicators (SLIs)** — the actual measured values.
> - **Penalties / Credits** — what happens if SLO is breached (e.g., 10% bill credit for 99.9% < uptime < 99%).
> - **Exclusions** — force majeure, customer-induced outages.

### 2.7 Practical Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters in industry:**
> 1. **Migration Decisions**: Cloud modelling produces the Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and ROI comparisons used in "lift-and-shift" vs "re-architect" decisions.
> 2. **Vendor Selection**: Modelling the reference architecture helps compare AWS vs Azure vs GCP feature-by-feature.
> 3. **Disaster Recovery (DR) Design**: The series/parallel availability formulas directly translate to choosing active-active vs active-passive DR.
> 4. **Capacity Planning**: Workload models drive auto-scaling policies and reserved-instance purchases.
> 5. **Compliance & Audit**: Cloud auditors use the CCRA roles to map who controls what — essential for SOC2, ISO 27001, DPDP Act audits.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Availability

**Problem:** A cloud-hosted web application uses an Application Server (availability 99.95%) and a Database (availability 99.9%) in series. What is the combined availability? Calculate the annual downtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{app} &= 99.95\% = 0.9995 \\
A_{db} &= 99.9\% = 0.9990 \\
A_{series} &= A_{app} \times A_{db} \\
A_{series} &= 0.9995 \times 0.9990 \\
A_{series} &= 0.9985005 \\
A_{series} &\approx 99.85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Convert downtime to minutes per year:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{year} &= (1 - A_{series}) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \\
D_{year} &= (1 - 0.9985005) \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{year} &= 0.0014995 \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{year} &\approx 788.07 \text{ minutes} \\
D_{year} &\approx 13.13 \text{ hours per year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: Combining two highly available components in series **drastically reduces overall availability** because the failure modes multiply. This is why every component in the chain must be carefully engineered.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel (Redundant) System Availability

**Problem:** To improve reliability, the database is replaced with **two redundant databases in active-passive** configuration, each having 99.9% availability. Compute the new combined system availability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{db1} &= 0.9990,\ A_{db2} = 0.9990 \\
A_{parallel} &= 1 - (1 - A_{db1})(1 - A_{db2}) \\
A_{parallel} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9990)(1 - 0.9990) \\
A_{parallel} &= 1 - (0.0010)(0.0010) \\
A_{parallel} &= 1 - 0.000001 \\
A_{parallel} &= 0.999999 \\
A_{parallel} &\approx 99.9999\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combined system availability:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= A_{app} \times A_{parallel} \\
A_{total} &= 0.9995 \times 0.999999 \\
A_{total} &\approx 0.999499 \\
A_{total} &\approx 99.9499\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Annual downtime:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{year} &= (1 - 0.999499) \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{year} &= 0.000501 \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{year} &\approx 263.32 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.39 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: Adding redundancy dramatically cuts downtime from 13.13 hours to 4.39 hours, achieving **Three 9s** with one extra DB instance.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Little's Law for Capacity Planning

**Problem:** An e-commerce website receives an average of **200 requests per second** ($\lambda = 200$). Each request takes on average **0.05 seconds** to process in the application server. Calculate (a) the average number of concurrent requests, (b) the number of app-server instances required if one instance can handle 100 concurrent requests safely.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation (using $L = \lambda \times W$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= 200 \text{ req/s} \\
W &= 0.05 \text{ s} \\
L &= \lambda \times W = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 \text{ concurrent requests}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Number of instances required:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{instances} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{L}{\mu_{single}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{10}{100} \right\rceil = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Now consider a marketing campaign that causes a 10× spike in traffic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{peak} &= 10 \times 200 = 2000 \text{ req/s} \\
L_{peak} &= 2000 \times 0.05 = 100 \text{ concurrent requests} \\
N_{peak} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{100}{100} \right\rceil = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Edge case interpretation**: The minimum is 1, but real systems add a safety factor $k = 1.5$ to absorb CPU/memory headroom:

$$
N_{safe} = \left\lceil \dfrac{100 \times 1.5}{100} \right\rceil = 2
$$

> **Design Insight**: Auto-scaling policy should trigger when concurrent load exceeds 100 (one instance's safe limit), adding a second instance automatically.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Storage Tier Cost Optimisation

**Problem:** A company stores **5 TB total** in the cloud. 20% is "hot" (frequently accessed), 50% is "warm" (monthly), 30% is "cold" (archival). The cost per GB-month is: Hot = $0.023, Warm = $0.010, Cold = $0.001. Compute the monthly storage cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-step derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{hot} &= 0.20 \times 5 \text{ TB} = 1.0 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB} \\
S_{warm} &= 0.50 \times 5 \text{ TB} = 2.5 \text{ TB} = 2560 \text{ GB} \\
S_{cold} &= 0.30 \times 5 \text{ TB} = 1.5 \text{ TB} = 1536 \text{ GB} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{hot} &= 0.023 \times 1024 = 23.552 \\
C_{warm} &= 0.010 \times 2560 = 25.600 \\
C_{cold} &= 0.001 \times 1536 = 1.536 \\
C_{total} &= 23.552 + 25.600 + 1.536 = 50.688 \text{ USD/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Optimisation Insight**: If access patterns shift so that hot data drops from 20% to 5%, the cost of hot tier drops to $\approx 5.89$, a **saving of $17.66/month** — a tangible example of cloud design modelling reducing cost.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Cloud Modelling Trade-offs

| Design Choice | Performance Impact | Cost Impact | Complexity | When to Choose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vertical Scaling (bigger VM) | + (short-term) | $$ \uparrow $$ (non-linear) | Low | Databases with strict consistency |
| Horizontal Scaling (more VMs) | + (long-term) | $ \downarrow $ (linear) | Medium | Stateless web servers |
| Multi-AZ Deployment | High availability | $$ \uparrow\uparrow $$ (2× data transfer) | Medium | Production critical apps |
| Multi-Region Active-Active | Disaster recovery | $$ \uparrow\uparrow\uparrow $$ | High | Global SaaS (e.g., Netflix) |
| Cold Storage Tiering | Slow retrieval | $ \downarrow\downarrow $ | Low | Backups, logs, archives |
| Reserved Instances | Same performance | $ \downarrow $ (~30–60% off) | Low | Predictable workloads |
| Spot/Preemptible VMs | Same performance | $ \downarrow\downarrow $ (~70% off) | High | Batch, ML training |
| Serverless (Lambda/Functions) | Auto-scaling, cold-start | Pay per invocation | Low | Event-driven, sporadic workloads |

### 📘 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students commonly **omit diagrams** for the reference model question — losing 2–3 marks. Always include the **NIST layered stack** and the **actor-interaction diagram**.
> - Students write the **definition of cloud** instead of the **definition of cloud modelling & design** — this is a 14-marker; do not waste 200 words on preamble.
> - For numerical problems, **units must be carried throughout**. Mark deduction of 0.5 per missing unit.
> - Always **state assumptions** explicitly (e.g., "assuming M/M/1 queueing model", "ignoring network latency").

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model** in detail. Discuss the **five essential characteristics**, **three service models**, and **four deployment models**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A cloud-hosted e-learning portal uses **two web servers in active-active load-balanced** mode (each with availability 99.95%) and **one database** with availability 99.9%. Calculate the **overall system availability** and **annual downtime in minutes**. Suggest **two design improvements** to push availability above 99.99%.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** The NIST Cloud Computing Reference Model is a generic, high-level conceptual model that identifies the major actors, their activities, and the functions/elements in cloud computing.

**2. Essential Characteristics (2 marks):** Provide a 1-line description for each: On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service.

**3. Service Models (2 marks):** Provide IaaS, PaaS, SaaS definitions + one example each.

**4. Deployment Models (1 mark):** Private, Public, Hybrid, Community — define each in one line.

**5. NIST Diagram (1 mark):** The **layered responsibility stack** showing consumer layers (App, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS) on top and provider layers (Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking) on bottom, meeting at the service interface. (Draw the diagram from Section 4.2 above.)

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Web tier (parallel) availability (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{web1} &= 0.9995,\ A_{web2} = 0.9995 \\
A_{web} &= 1 - (1 - 0.9995)(1 - 0.9995) \\
A_{web} &= 1 - (0.0005)(0.0005) \\
A_{web} &= 1 - 0.00000025 \\
A_{web} &= 0.99999975 \approx 99.99998\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Overall system (series with DB) (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{system} &= A_{web} \times A_{db} \\
A_{system} &= 0.99999975 \times 0.9990 \\
A_{system} &\approx 0.99899975 \approx 99.90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual downtime (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{year} &= (1 - 0.99899975) \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{year} &= 0.00100025 \times 525{,}600 \\
D_{year} &\approx 525.73 \text{ minutes} \approx 8.76 \text{ hours/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Design improvements to cross 99.99% (3 marks):**
1. **Deploy a redundant database** (e.g., active-passive DB pair or DB cluster with synchronous replication) — this pushes DB availability to 99.9999%, making the system 99.999%+.
2. **Add a Content Delivery Network (CDN)** in front of the web tier to absorb traffic and reduce dependency on origin servers.
3. **Use multiple Availability Zones (Multi-AZ)** for both web and DB tiers so that a single data-centre failure does not impact availability.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **key design principles** of cloud architecture. Describe the **five actor roles** in the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture (CCRA) with a suitable diagram.

**(b) [7 marks]** A startup uses a PaaS service to host a chat application. The platform costs **$0.10 per hour per instance**, and each instance handles an average of **500 requests per hour**. During peak hours, the application receives **20,000 requests per hour**.
- **(i)** Calculate the number of instances required during peak hours. (2 marks)
- **(ii)** If the application runs 24×7 with an average of 25% peak load over the month (30 days), compute the **monthly bill**. (3 marks)
- **(iii)** Suggest **two cost-optimisation strategies** to reduce this bill by at least 30%. (2 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Design Principles (4 marks — list any 4 with 1 mark each):**
1. **Loose Coupling** — components interact via APIs/messages, not direct calls.
2. **Statelessness** — session state stored in DB/Redis, not in app server memory.
3. **Horizontal Scalability** — scale out by adding instances.
4. **Elasticity / Auto-Scaling** — resources scale with demand.
5. **Redundancy & Fault Tolerance** — no SPOF.
6. **Security by Design** — zero-trust model.

**Actor Roles with diagram (3 marks):**
List **Cloud Consumer, Cloud Provider, Cloud Auditor, Cloud Broker, Cloud Carrier** with a 1-line description for each (2 marks). Draw a **simple block diagram** showing the relationships (e.g., Consumer ↔ Broker ↔ Provider; Auditor → audits → Provider; Carrier → transports data → between Consumer & Provider) (1 mark).

**(b) [7 marks]**

**(i) Peak-hour instance count (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{peak} &= \left\lceil \dfrac{20{,}000}{500} \right\rceil = 40 \text{ instances}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Monthly bill (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Peak load} &= 25\% \text{ of the month} \\
\text{Average instances} &= 0.25 \times 40 = 10 \text{ instances} \\
\text{Hours in month} &= 30 \times 24 = 720 \text{ hours} \\
\text{Monthly bill} &= 10 \text{ instances} \times 0.10 \text{ USD/hr} \times 720 \text{ hrs} \\
\text{Monthly bill} &= 720 \text{ USD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Cost optimisation strategies (2 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Use Reserved Instances / Savings Plans** for the baseline 10 always-on instances — saves up to 40% on those hours.
2. **Implement Auto-Scaling** to scale to 40 instances only during peak hours, dropping to a minimum (e.g., 5) at off-peak — reduces average instance count from 10 to ~6–7.
3. **Move to Spot / Preemptible VMs** for non-critical workloads — 70% cheaper, ideal for batch / analytics jobs.

> Combined, these can cut the $720 bill to under $500 (≈30% saving), satisfying the question.

---

- ✅ **SLA Tiers**: 99% → 3.65 days, 99.9% → 8.76 hrs, 99.99% → 52.6 min, 99.999% → 5.26 min downtime/year.
- ✅ **Availability Formulas**:
  - Series: $A_{series} = \prod A_i$
  - Parallel: $A_{parallel} = 1 - \prod(1 - A_i)$
- ✅ **Downtime Formula**: $D = (1 - A) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60$ minutes.
- ✅ **Little's Law**: $L = \lambda \times W$ (concurrent = rate × time).

- ✅ **DR RTO / RPO**:
  - **RTO** (Recovery Time Objective) = max acceptable downtime.
  - **RPO** (Recovery Point Objective) = max acceptable data loss.
