  1. NIST CCRA actor diagram.
  2. Layered responsibility stack (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS).
  3. Cloud design flow (Requirement → Model → Design → Deploy).
  4. Workload decision tree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip**: KTU examiners give **2–3 marks just for a clean, labelled diagram**. Never skip the visual artefact in a 7-mark sub-question. Always carry **units** in every numerical step. State the **assumptions** before plugging in numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Virtualization - Foundations, Grid, Cloud and Virtualization, Virtualization and Cloud Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Virtualization - Foundations, Grid, Cloud and Virtualization, Virtualization and Cloud Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Virtualization — The Foundational Definition

**Virtualization** is the strategic abstraction of physical computing resources (CPU, memory, storage, and networking) from their physical hardware infrastructure, enabling the creation of logical, isolated, and software-defined instances known as **Virtual Machines (VMs)** or **Virtual Resources**. It is the technological cornerstone that allows a single physical server to function as multiple independent computing environments, each running its own Operating System (OS) and applications, governed by a **Hypervisor** (Virtual Machine Monitor - VMM).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Virtualization is a foundational technology in cloud computing that decouples the operating system, applications, and data from the underlying hardware, allowing efficient resource pooling, isolation, and on-demand provisioning of compute resources.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Apartment Building" Model

Imagine a large plot of land (the **Physical Server**) that traditionally hosts only one large independent house (one OS + one set of applications). The land, however, is underutilized. **Virtualization** is the act of building a high-rise **Apartment Complex** on that same land, where:

- The **Plot of Land** = Physical Hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk)
- The **Architectural Plan (Blueprint)** = **Hypervisor** (Layer that divides resources)
- Each **Apartment** = An independent **Virtual Machine (VM)** with its own OS
- The **Apartment Owner** = The end-user or tenant
- The **Building Management** = Resource scheduler and allocator

Just as tenants share common infrastructure (water lines, electricity, parking) but enjoy **complete isolation** (own locks, own electricity meter), VMs share the physical hardware but maintain logical isolation, security, and dedicated resource quotas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Pillars of Virtualization (Remember for 14-mark answers):**
> 1. **Partitioning** — Dividing one physical resource into many virtual units
> 2. **Isolation** — VMs are independent; failure of one does not affect others
> 3. **Encapsulation** — Entire VM state can be saved, copied, and migrated as a single file

## 1.3 Grid Computing — Formal Definition

**Grid Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that coordinates networked, heterogeneous, and geographically dispersed computing resources (clusters, servers, workstations, storage systems) to function as a single, unified **Virtual Organization (VO)**. The primary goal is to solve massive computational problems (scientific, engineering, or data-intensive) by aggregating idle CPU cycles and storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Grid Computing Analogy — The "Power Grid" Model:**
> Just as an electrical power grid delivers electricity from multiple generating stations (coal, hydro, solar, nuclear) to homes and industries on demand without the user knowing the source, a **Computational Grid** pools processing power from many distributed machines and delivers computational services to users transparently. The hallmark is **cooperative resource sharing** across administrative domains.

## 1.4 Cloud Computing — Formal Definition (NIST-Aligned)

**Cloud Computing** is an evolution of grid computing that delivers **on-demand, self-service, scalable, measured, and pooled** computing resources (networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) over the Internet with **minimal management effort** from the consumer. It is defined by the **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology) Model** through five essential characteristics, three service models, and four deployment models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud Computing:**
> 1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Resources provisioned automatically without human interaction
> 2. **Broad Network Access** — Available over the network via standard mechanisms
> 3. **Resource Pooling** — Multi-tenant model with dynamic reallocation
> 4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Scale out/in quickly, often appearing infinite to the user
> 5. **Measured Service** — Pay-per-use metering at appropriate levels of abstraction

## 1.5 The Triad: Virtualization, Grid, and Cloud

These three technologies are **not competitors** — they are **complementary layers** in modern distributed computing:

| Layer | Role in the Stack |
|:------|:-----------------|
| **Virtualization** | The *enabling substrate* — abstracts physical hardware |
| **Grid Computing** | The *cooperative middleware* — aggregates distributed resources |
| **Cloud Computing** | The *service delivery model* — provides utility-style consumption |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Statement for KTU Board Exam:**
> "Cloud Computing could not exist at scale without Virtualization as its foundational technology. Grid Computing provided the early architectural philosophy of resource pooling, while Virtualization provided the engineering mechanism to implement it efficiently, leading to the modern Cloud model."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Virtualization — Architectural Building Blocks

### 2.1.1 The Hypervisor (VMM) — The Heart of Virtualization

The **Hypervisor** is the software layer that creates, runs, and manages virtual machines. It sits between the hardware and the guest operating systems, intercepting and emulating hardware access requests.

**Two Architectural Types:**

**Type 1 Hypervisor (Bare-Metal / Native)**
- Installed **directly on physical hardware**
- No host OS required
- Examples: **VMware ESXi**, **Microsoft Hyper-V Server**, **Xen (Citrix)**, **KVM**
- Performance: Higher, since no middleman OS
- Used in **Enterprise Data Centers** and **Cloud Infrastructure (AWS, Azure)**

**Type 2 Hypervisor (Hosted)**
- Runs **as an application on a host operating system**
- Examples: **VMware Workstation**, **Oracle VirtualBox**, **Parallels Desktop**
- Performance: Lower, due to double layer of indirection (Host OS + Hypervisor)
- Used in **Development, Testing, and Education**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Exam Hint:** Always draw a **layered diagram** showing the difference between Type 1 and Type 2. Examiners award 2-3 marks specifically for a *clean comparative diagram*.

### 2.1.2 Types of Virtualization (Complete Taxonomy)

| Type | What is Abstracted | Example Use Case |
|:-----|:-------------------|:----------------|
| **Server Virtualization** | Physical server into multiple VMs | Data center consolidation |
| **Storage Virtualization** | Physical storage into logical pools | SAN, NAS unification |
| **Network Virtualization** | Physical network into virtual networks | VLANs, SDN, VXLAN |
| **Desktop Virtualization (VDI)** | User desktop into VM | Remote work, BYOD |
| **Application Virtualization** | Application from host OS | Citrix XenApp, Docker |
| **Data Virtualization** | Data sources into unified view | Data federation, APIs |
| **Memory Virtualization** | RAM across machines | Memory overcommit, Memory pooling |
| **CPU Virtualization** | CPU cores into virtual CPUs (vCPUs) | Intel VT-x, AMD-V |
| **GPU Virtualization** | GPU resources for AI/ML workloads | NVIDIA vGPU |

### 2.1.3 Full Virtualization vs Para-Virtualization vs Hardware-Assisted Virtualization

- **Full Virtualization:** Guest OS is **unmodified**; hypervisor traps and emulates privileged instructions. No guest OS awareness.
- **Para-Virtualization:** Guest OS is **modified** to include hypercalls (direct calls to hypervisor). Faster but requires OS modification (e.g., early Xen).
- **Hardware-Assisted Virtualization:** CPU has special instructions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) that make virtualization efficient. **Modern standard.**

### 2.1.4 VM Migration Techniques

- **Live Migration:** Moving a running VM from one physical host to another **with zero downtime** (e.g., VMware vMotion). Critical for load balancing in cloud.
- **Cold Migration:** VM is powered off, then moved.
- **Storage Migration:** Moving VM disks between storage arrays.

## 2.2 Grid Computing — Deep Architectural Analysis

### 2.2.1 Grid Architecture Layers (Conceptual Stack)

| Layer | Function |
|:------|:---------|
| **Application Layer** | User-facing scientific/business applications |
| **Collective Layer** | Directory services, co-scheduling, data replication |
| **Resource Layer** | Manages individual resources (CPUs, storage, networks) |
| **Connectivity Layer** | Communication protocols (TCP/IP, GRAM, GridFTP) |
| **Fabric Layer** | Physical resources (servers, clusters, instruments) |

### 2.2.2 Key Grid Characteristics (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Heterogeneity** — Resources are diverse (different OS, hardware, networks)
2. **Geographical Distribution** — Spans continents
3. **Cross-Organizational Sharing** — Members of a Virtual Organization pool resources
4. **Coordinated Resource Sharing** — Solving problems too large for any single entity
5. **Standard Open Protocols & Interfaces** — **OGSA (Open Grid Services Architecture)**, **WSRF (Web Services Resource Framework)**
6. **Decentralized Control** — No single administrative authority

> [!NOTE]
> **Famous Real-World Grid Project:** **SETI@home** (Search for Extraterrestrial Intelligence) — used 5+ million volunteer PCs to analyze radio telescope data. Also **EGEE (Enabling Grids for E-Science)**, **BOINC**.

## 2.3 Cloud Computing — Service and Deployment Models

### 2.3.1 The Three Service Models (KTU MUST-KNOW)

| Model | Acronym | Customer Controls | Provider Controls | Example |
|:------|:--------|:------------------|:------------------|:--------|
| **Software as a Service** | SaaS | Only their data & user settings | Everything else | Gmail, MS Office 365, Salesforce |
| **Platform as a Service** | PaaS | Applications they deploy | OS, runtime, middleware, servers | Google App Engine, Heroku, Azure App Service |
| **Infrastructure as a Service** | IaaS | OS, runtime, applications, data | Hardware, storage, network, virtualization | AWS EC2, Azure VM, Google Compute Engine |

### 2.3.2 The Four Deployment Models

| Model | Description |
|:------|:------------|
| **Public Cloud** | Open to general public; owned by provider (AWS, Azure, GCP) |
| **Private Cloud** | Exclusively for one organization; on-premises or hosted |
| **Hybrid Cloud** | Composition of two or more clouds (public + private) bound by orchestration |
| **Community Cloud** | Shared by organizations with common concerns (e.g., government agencies, healthcare consortiums) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

For virtualization and cloud problems, the following engineering metrics are critical:

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Description |
|:-----------------|:---------|:------------|
| **Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)** | $SCR = \dfrac{N_{VMs}}{N_{Hosts}}$ | Number of VMs hosted per physical server |
| **Hypervisor Overhead** | $O_{hv} = \dfrac{T_{hv}}{T_{native}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage performance loss vs native execution |
| **CPU Overcommit Ratio** | $R_{cpu} = \dfrac{\sum vCPUs_{allocated}}{Cores_{physical}}$ | Usually 2:1 to 6:1 in production |
| **Memory Overcommit Ratio** | $R_{mem} = \dfrac{\sum RAM_{allocated}}{RAM_{physical}}$ | Up to 1.5:1 with ballooning/swapping |
| **VM Density** | $D_{vm} = \dfrac{VMs_{active}}{Server_{physical}}$ | VMs per physical host |
| **Cloud TCO (Total Cost of Ownership)** | $TCO = C_{capex} + C_{opex} \times t$ | Capital + Operational cost over time $t$ |
| **Pay-per-Use Cost** | $Cost = \sum_{i} U_i \times P_i$ | Sum of (Usage $\times$ Price) for each resource $i$ |
| **Elasticity Efficiency** | $\eta_{el} = \dfrac{U_{peak} - U_{idle}}{U_{allocated}}$ | How well resources match demand |
| **Availability (Cloud SLA)** | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | Mean Time Between Failure / Total Time |
| **Virtualization ROI Period** | $t_{roi} = \dfrac{C_{virt\_initial}}{S_{monthly} - C_{virt\_monthly}}$ | Months to recover virtualization investment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When solving numerical problems, always **state the unit** of the final answer (e.g., months for ROI, percentage for overhead, ratio for consolidation). Examiners in KTU deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Server Virtualization** powers **AWS, Azure, GCP** — every EC2 instance is a VM.
- **Grid Computing** powers **scientific research** — CERN's LHC data analysis, protein folding (Folding@home), weather prediction.
- **Cloud Computing** powers **Netflix streaming**, **Uber's ride matching**, **Instagram's photo processing** — all running on virtualized infrastructure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the Server Consolidation Ratio (SCR)

**Problem Statement:**
An organization has consolidated **120 physical servers** into a virtualized infrastructure of **15 physical host servers**, each running an average of **8 virtual machines**. Compute the SCR, the total VMs, and the percentage reduction in physical hardware footprint.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:

**Step 1: Compute the total number of virtual machines deployed**

$$N_{VMs} = N_{Hosts} \times VMs_{per\_host}$$

$$N_{VMs} = 15 \times 8 = 120 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Server Consolidation Ratio**

$$SCR = \dfrac{N_{VMs}}{N_{Hosts}} = \dfrac{120}{15} = 8.0$$

**Interpretation:** Each physical host is hosting **8 virtual machines**, achieving a **consolidation ratio of 8:1**.

**Step 3: Compute the percentage reduction in physical servers**

$$Reduction_{\%} = \dfrac{N_{original} - N_{virtualized}}{N_{original}} \times 100\%$$

$$Reduction_{\%} = \dfrac{120 - 15}{120} \times 100\%$$

$$Reduction_{\%} = \dfrac{105}{120} \times 100\% = 87.5\%$$

**Conclusion:** The organization achieved an **87.5% reduction** in physical server count, with an **8:1 consolidation ratio**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hypervisor Performance Overhead Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A benchmark application runs in **45 seconds** on a **native (non-virtualized)** Linux system. The same application runs in **54 seconds** inside a **Type 2 hypervisor** (VMware Workstation). Calculate the hypervisor overhead percentage.

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:

**Step 1: Identify native and virtualized execution times**

$$T_{native} = 45 \text{ seconds}$$

$$T_{virtualized} = 54 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 2: Compute the time overhead introduced by the hypervisor**

$$\Delta T = T_{virtualized} - T_{native}$$

$$\Delta T = 54 - 45 = 9 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3: Apply the hypervisor overhead formula**

$$O_{hv} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_{native}} \times 100\%$$

$$O_{hv} = \dfrac{9}{45} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

**Conclusion:** The Type 2 hypervisor introduces a **20% performance overhead** for this workload. A Type 1 hypervisor would typically yield 2–8% overhead for CPU-bound workloads.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cloud TCO Comparison (In-House vs Cloud)

**Problem Statement:**
A startup needs to host a web application. **Option A (In-House):** Buy 2 servers at ₹5,00,000 each, plus ₹50,000/month power, cooling, and IT staff. **Option B (Cloud IaaS):** Pay ₹1,20,000/month on-demand. Over **3 years**, which is cheaper? (Ignore maintenance/upgrade costs for in-house.)

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:

**Step 1: Calculate Option A — In-House Capital + Operational Cost**

$$C_{capex} = 2 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

$$C_{opex\_3y} = 50{,}000 \times 36 \text{ months} = 18{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

$$TCO_A = 10{,}00{,}000 + 18{,}00{,}000 = 28{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR over 3 years}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Option B — Cloud Pay-per-Use**

$$TCO_B = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 36 = 43{,}20{,}000 \text{ INR over 3 years}$$

**Step 3: Compute the differential and percentage savings**

$$\Delta TCO = TCO_B - TCO_A = 43{,}20{,}000 - 28{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}20{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

$$Savings_{\%} = \dfrac{\Delta TCO}{TCO_B} \times 100\% = \dfrac{15{,}20{,}000}{43{,}20{,}000} \times 100\% \approx 35.19\%$$

**Conclusion:** **Option A (In-House) saves ₹15.2 Lakhs (≈35%)** over 3 years. However, cloud offers **elasticity, no upfront cost, and no depreciation risk** — these are the qualitative factors students must mention for full marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Grid vs Cloud vs Virtualization Comparison

| Parameter | Virtualization | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing |
|:----------|:--------------|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Resource abstraction & consolidation | Cooperative problem solving | Utility-style service delivery |
| **Scope** | Single physical machine | Multi-organizational, global | Global, internet-scale |
| **Architecture** | Hypervisor-based | Middleware + OGSA protocols | Multi-tenant + Service-oriented |
| **Resource Type** | CPU, RAM, Storage, Network | Compute cycles, datasets, instruments | Compute, Storage, Platform, Software |
| **User Awareness** | Admin / DevOps | Scientific researchers | End users / developers / enterprises |
| **Elasticity** | Limited (within host) | Moderate (across grid) | High (automatic scaling) |
| **Business Model** | Capex (license) | Grant-funded / academic | Pay-per-use / Subscription |
| **Key Standard** | OVF (Open Virtualization Format) | OGSA, WSRF | NIST 800-145, ISO/IEC 22123 |
| **Example** | VMware vSphere | SETI@home, EGEE | AWS, Azure, GCP |
| **Relationship to others** | **Enabling layer** for both Grid and Cloud | **Architectural precursor** to Cloud | **Built upon** Virtualization & Grid |

---

## 3.5 Derivation: VM Density & Resource Allocation

**Problem Statement:**
A host server has **64 GB RAM** and **16 physical CPU cores**. The administrator plans to deploy VMs, each requiring **4 vCPUs and 8 GB RAM**. The CPU overcommit ratio is set to **4:1** and memory overcommit to **1:1**. How many VMs can be deployed?

### Step-by-Step Analytical Solution:

**Step 1: Determine total allocatable vCPUs**

$$vCPUs_{total} = Cores_{physical} \times R_{cpu} = 16 \times 4 = 64 \text{ vCPUs}$$

**Step 2: Determine total allocatable RAM**

$$RAM_{alloc} = RAM_{physical} \times R_{mem} = 64 \times 1 = 64 \text{ GB}$$

**Step 3: Compute VM count constrained by vCPUs**

$$N_{vm\_cpu} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{vCPUs_{total}}{vCPUs_{per\_vm}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{64}{4} \right\rfloor = 16 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 4: Compute VM count constrained by RAM**

$$N_{vm\_ram} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{RAM_{alloc}}{RAM_{per\_vm}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{64}{8} \right\rfloor = 8 \text{ VMs}$$

**Step 5: The bottleneck (limiting factor) is RAM**

$$N_{vm\_actual} = \min(N_{vm\_cpu}, N_{vm\_ram}) = \min(16, 8) = 8 \text{ VMs}$$

**Conclusion:** Only **8 VMs can be deployed** — RAM is the **bottleneck resource**, not CPU. This illustrates the engineering principle of **bottleneck-aware capacity planning** in virtualized data centers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Virtualization Architecture — Type 1 vs Type 2 Hypervisor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Type1["TYPE 1 HYPERVISOR - BARE METAL"]
        direction TB
        A1["Guest OS 1"] --> HV1["Hypervisor ESXi or Hyper-V or KVM"]
        A2["Guest OS 2"] --> HV1
        A3["Guest OS 3"] --> HV1
        HV1 --> HW1["Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk Network"]
    end

    subgraph Type2["TYPE 2 HYPERVISOR - HOSTED"]
        direction TB
        B1["Guest OS 1"] --> HV2["Hypervisor VMware Workstation or VirtualBox"]
        B2["Guest OS 2"] --> HV2
        HV2 --> HOS["Host Operating System Windows or Linux"]
        HOS --> HW2["Physical Hardware CPU RAM Disk Network"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram bottom-up:** In Type 1, the hypervisor talks directly to hardware. In Type 2, the hypervisor must go through a host OS, creating an extra layer of indirection.

## 4.2 The Relationship Triad: Virtualization → Grid → Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V["VIRTUALIZATION
    Hardware Abstraction Layer"] -->|Enables| G["GRID COMPUTING
    Distributed Resource Pool"]
    V -->|Powers| C["CLOUD COMPUTING
    On-Demand Service Delivery"]
    G -->|Architectural Foundation| C
    C -->|Delivers to| U["End Users SaaS PaaS IaaS"]
    G -->|Serves| S["Scientific and Research Communities"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Diagram Interpretation for KTU Board Exam:**
> Virtualization is the **enabling technology**, Grid Computing is the **architectural philosophy**, and Cloud Computing is the **commercialized, user-facing service model** — all three form a layered, complementary ecosystem.

## 4.3 Cloud Service Models — IaaS, PaaS, SaaS (Stacked Control Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SaaS["SaaS - SOFTWARE AS A SERVICE"]
        S1["User only manages DATA and USER SETTINGS"]
    end
    subgraph PaaS["PaaS - PLATFORM AS A SERVICE"]
        P1["User manages APPLICATIONS and DATA"]
        P2["Provider manages OS, Runtime, Middleware, Virtualization, Hardware"]
    end
    subgraph IaaS["IaaS - INFRASTRUCTURE AS A SERVICE"]
        I1["User manages OS, Runtime, Middleware, APPLICATIONS, DATA"]
        I2["Provider manages Virtualization, Storage, Network, Hardware"]
    end
    SaaS -.Inherits all layers below.-> PaaS
    PaaS -.Inherits all layers below.-> IaaS
    IaaS -.Built on.-> VIRT["VIRTUALIZATION LAYER"]
    VIRT --> HW["PHYSICAL HARDWARE"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Memorization Aid:** As you go **upward** (SaaS → PaaS → IaaS), the user has **less** control but **more** convenience. As you go **downward** (IaaS → PaaS → SaaS), the provider manages **more** and the user manages **less**.

## 4.4 Grid Computing — Virtual Organization Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VO["VIRTUAL ORGANIZATION VO"]
        direction LR
        ORG1["University A
        Cluster of 50 servers"] --> MIDDLEWARE
        ORG2["Research Lab B
        HPC Supercomputer"] --> MIDDLEWARE
        ORG3["Industry Partner C
        Storage and Datasets"] --> MIDDLEWARE
    end
    MIDDLEWARE["Grid Middleware
    Globus Toolkit
    GRAM GridFTP MDS"] --> USER["Researcher Runs Job
    Unified View of Resources"]
    USER --> RESULT["Aggregated Compute and Storage
    Delivered as Single Resource"]
```

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Virtualization to Cloud Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PHASE1["PHASE 1 PHYSICAL LAYER
    Servers, Storage, Network, Power, Cooling"] -->|Virtualized by| PHASE2
    PHASE2["PHASE 2 HYPERVISOR LAYER
    Type 1 Bare-Metal VMM"] -->|Creates| PHASE3
    PHASE3["PHASE 3 VIRTUAL MACHINES
    Encapsulated compute units with own OS"] -->|Pooled into| PHASE4
    PHASE4["PHASE 4 RESOURCE POOL
    Elastic, Multi-Tenant, Metered"] -->|Delivered as| PHASE5
    PHASE5["PHASE 5 CLOUD SERVICES
    IaaS, PaaS, SaaS to End Users"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** "Explain how virtualization enables cloud computing." The above pipeline diagram is a **complete, mark-fetching answer** to this question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Virtualization. List and briefly explain any two types of virtualization.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Virtualization is the technology of creating software-based (virtual) representations of physical computing resources such as servers, storage, networks, and desktops, enabled by a software layer called the **Hypervisor (VMM)** that abstracts the underlying hardware.

**Two Types (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Server Virtualization:** Divides a single physical server into multiple isolated virtual servers (VMs), each running its own OS and applications. Enables server consolidation and improved hardware utilization. Example: VMware vSphere, Microsoft Hyper-V.

2. **Storage Virtualization:** Combines physical storage from multiple network storage devices into what appears to be a single logical storage unit. Enables better management, backup, and disaster recovery. Example: SAN-based storage virtualization.

*(Alternative acceptable: Network Virtualization — VLANs, SDN; Desktop Virtualization — VDI)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Grid Computing and Cloud Computing in terms of architecture, purpose, and user access.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Grid Computing | Cloud Computing |
|:----------|:---------------|:----------------|
| **Purpose** | Pooled resource sharing for large-scale scientific/technical problems | On-demand delivery of IT services over the Internet |
| **Architecture** | Loosely-coupled, distributed across organizations | Tightly-integrated, provider-managed data centers |
| **User Access** | Members of a Virtual Organization (VO) | General public or enterprise users (broad access) |
| **Resource Model** | Cooperative sharing | Pay-per-use utility model |
| **Example** | SETI@home, EGEE, Folding@home | AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud |

**Key Distinction (0.5 mark):** Grid focuses on **collaborative computation**; Cloud focuses on **service consumption** with elastic, metered delivery.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice means you answer **either Question A OR Question B**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Explain the different types of hypervisors with neat diagrams. Compare Type 1 and Type 2 hypervisors on parameters like performance, isolation, scalability, and use case.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of Hypervisor (1 mark):**
A Hypervisor or Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) is the software layer that creates, runs, and manages multiple virtual machines on a single physical host by abstracting and allocating hardware resources.

**Type 1 Hypervisor (3 marks):**
- **Architecture:** Installed directly on physical hardware (bare-metal). No host OS.
- **Examples:** VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V Server, Citrix XenServer, KVM.
- **Performance:** High — direct hardware access, minimal overhead (2–8%).
- **Isolation:** Strong — VMs are fully isolated at the hardware level.
- **Use Case:** Enterprise data centers, cloud infrastructure (AWS, Azure).
- **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 Type 1 subgraph.

**Type 2 Hypervisor (3 marks):**
- **Architecture:** Runs as an application on top of a host operating system.
- **Examples:** VMware Workstation, Oracle VirtualBox, Parallels Desktop.
- **Performance:** Lower — extra OS layer adds 10–25% overhead.
- **Isolation:** Weaker — host OS failures can crash all VMs.
- **Use Case:** Development, testing, learning, desktop virtualization.
- **Diagram Reference:** See Section 4.1 Type 2 subgraph.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Discuss the benefits and challenges of virtualization in the context of cloud computing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Benefits (3.5 marks — 0.7 each):**

1. **Server Consolidation** — Multiple VMs on one host drastically reduce hardware footprint and cost (typical consolidation ratio 5:1 to 15:1).
2. **Resource Efficiency** — Dynamic resource allocation improves CPU, RAM, and storage utilization from ~15% to 70–80%.
3. **Isolation & Security** — Each VM runs in its own sandbox; compromise of one does not affect others.
4. **Rapid Provisioning** — A new VM can be launched in minutes (vs. days for physical server procurement).
5. **Live Migration** — Workloads can be moved between physical hosts with zero downtime (VMware vMotion), enabling maintenance without service disruption.

**Challenges (3.5 marks — 0.7 each):**

1. **Performance Overhead** — Hypervisor adds latency and CPU overhead, especially for I/O-intensive workloads.
2. **Security Concerns (VM Escape)** — If a hypervisor is compromised, all VMs on that host are at risk.
3. **Resource Contention** — "Noisy neighbor" effect — one VM consuming excessive resources starves others.
4. **Complexity of Management** — Requires specialized skills (vSphere, Hyper-V administration).
5. **Licensing Costs** — Enterprise virtualization platforms (VMware) have high per-host licensing fees.
6. **Single Point of Failure** — Hardware failure of the physical host crashes all VMs on it (mitigated by clustering).
7. **Storage I/O Bottleneck** — Multiple VMs sharing storage can saturate disk bandwidth.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of virtualization. Discuss the role of the hypervisor in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram — Type 1 architecture is preferred for full marks)*

The architecture consists of **5 layers**, from bottom to top:
1. **Physical Hardware Layer** — CPU, RAM, Disk, Network Interface Cards
2. **Hypervisor Layer** — Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) that mediates all hardware access
3. **Virtual Hardware Layer** — Virtual CPU (vCPU), Virtual RAM, Virtual Disk, Virtual NIC presented to each VM
4. **Guest OS Layer** — Each VM runs its own complete operating system
5. **Application Layer** — End-user applications running inside each VM

**Role of the Hypervisor (4 marks):**

1. **Resource Allocation:** Dynamically distributes physical CPU, memory, and storage to VMs based on configured shares, limits, and reservations.

2. **Instruction Translation:** Intercepts and translates **privileged instructions** (ring 0 operations) from guest OS, since direct hardware access by VMs would crash the system.

3. **VM Lifecycle Management:** Handles creation, starting, pausing, resuming, snapshotting, and deletion of VMs.

4. **Isolation Enforcement:** Guarantees that one VM cannot access the memory or storage of another VM, providing strong security and fault isolation.

5. **Hardware Emulation:** Emulates virtual devices (e.g., virtual network adapters, virtual disks) so guest OS can run as if on dedicated hardware.

6. **Live Migration Support:** Enables moving running VMs between physical hosts (vMotion) for load balancing and hardware maintenance.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Compare and contrast Grid Computing, Cloud Computing, and Virtualization. Explain how these three technologies relate to each other in a modern cloud data center.**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**
*(Refer to the complete Analytical Matrix in Section 3.4 of these notes.)*

Key contrasts:
- **Virtualization** operates at the **single-machine** level; **Grid** operates at the **multi-organization** level; **Cloud** operates at the **internet-scale, service-delivery** level.
- **Virtualization** is a **technology**; **Grid** is a **computing paradigm**; **Cloud** is a **business and service model**.

**Relationship in Modern Cloud Data Center (3 marks):**

1. **Virtualization as the Foundation:** Every modern cloud data center (AWS, Azure, GCP) is built upon server virtualization using Type 1 hypervisors. Every EC2 instance, Azure VM, or GCP Compute Engine is essentially a virtual machine running on shared physical hardware.

2. **Grid Concepts in the Backend:** The resource pooling, scheduling, and distributed job management concepts pioneered by grid computing (Globus Toolkit) evolved into the **cloud orchestration platforms** like Kubernetes, OpenStack, and Apache Mesos.

3. **Cloud as the Commercialized Successor:** Cloud computing is often called the "commercial evolution of grid computing" — it took the architectural ideas of grid (resource pooling, distributed computing) and added **utility billing, self-service portals, and elastic scaling** to make it accessible to businesses and consumers.

**Conclusion:** The three technologies are **not in competition** — they form a **layered, synergistic stack** where Virtualization provides the engine, Grid provides the architectural philosophy, and Cloud provides the customer-facing service delivery.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Virtualization / Grid / Cloud Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **No diagram in 14-mark questions** = loses **2 to 3 marks** minimum. Always include a clean, labeled layer diagram.
> 2. **Writing "Type 1 hypervisor is better than Type 2"** without context = partial marks. Correct answer is *context-dependent* (Type 1 for production, Type 2 for development).
> 3. **Confusing PaaS and SaaS** — remember: in PaaS, the developer deploys **their own code**; in SaaS, the user only uses **provider's software**.
> 4. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** (e.g., writing "8" instead of "8:1 ratio" or "20" instead of "20%") — deducts 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 5. **Defining Cloud Computing without citing NIST's 5 characteristics** — examiners expect the **NIST-aligned definition** for full marks.
> 6. **Stating "Grid is obsolete" or "Cloud replaced Grid"** — incorrect. Grid is still used in scientific computing (CERN, weather forecasting). Cloud is a **commercial evolution**, not a replacement.
> 7. **Missing the "live migration" keyword** in VM management answers — it is a high-value technical term examiners expect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ High-Yield Rapid-Revision Summary — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

### **Virtualization Essentials**
- [x] Definition: Hardware abstraction via **Hypervisor (VMM)**
- [x] **Type 1 = Bare-metal** (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM); **Type 2 = Hosted** (Workstation, VirtualBox)
- [x] **Three pillars:** Partitioning, Isolation, Encapsulation
- [x] **Key term:** Live Migration (vMotion) — zero-downtime VM movement
- [x] **Para-virtualization** = modified guest OS; **Full virtualization** = unmodified guest OS
- [x] **Intel VT-x / AMD-V** = hardware-assisted virtualization (modern standard)

### **Grid Computing Essentials**
- [x] **Virtual Organization (VO)** = core concept
- [x] **OGSA, Globus Toolkit, GRAM, GridFTP** = key middleware standards
- [x] **Goal:** Aggregate geographically-distributed, heterogeneous resources
- [x] **Examples:** SETI@home, Folding@home, EGEE, CERN LHC Grid
- [x] **Grid is mostly for scientific/research workloads**, not commercial

### **Cloud Computing Essentials (NIST-Aligned)**
- [x] **5 Characteristics:** On-Demand Self-Service, Broad Network Access, Resource Pooling, Rapid Elasticity, Measured Service
- [x] **3 Service Models:** SaaS, PaaS, IaaS (mnemonic: **SPI** = Software, Platform, Infrastructure)
- [x] **4 Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community
- [x] **Virtualization is the FOUNDATION of Cloud Computing**
- [x] **Examples:** AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), IBM Cloud

### **The Interrelationship (One-Liner to Remember)**
> "**Virtualization** is the engine, **Grid Computing** is the architectural philosophy, and **Cloud Computing** is the customer-facing service model — all three are complementary layers in modern distributed computing."

### **Numerical Formulas to Memorize**
- [x] **SCR** = Number of VMs / Number of Physical Hosts
- [x] **Overhead %** = (Virtualized Time − Native Time) / Native Time × 100
- [x] **Overcommit Ratio** = Allocated vCPUs / Physical Cores (typical 2:1 to 6:1)
- [x] **TCO** = CapEx + (OpEx × Time Period)
- [x] **Bottleneck Detection:** Always find $\min(N_{vm\_cpu}, N_{vm\_ram}, N_{vm\_storage})$

### **Key Differentiators (Often Asked in KTU 2-Mark Questions)**
- [x] **IaaS vs PaaS:** In IaaS, user manages the OS; in PaaS, provider manages the OS.
- [x] **Public vs Private Cloud:** Public = shared infrastructure; Private = dedicated to one organization.
- [x] **Grid vs Cloud:** Grid = cooperative; Cloud = commercial service model.
- [x] **Virtualization vs Cloud Computing:** Virtualization = technology; Cloud = business model using that technology.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Storage and Cloud Computing - Data Storage, Cloud Storage, Cloud Storage from LANs to WANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Storage and Cloud Computing - Data Storage, Cloud Storage, Cloud Storage from LANs to WANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Data Storage?

**Data Storage** refers to the persistent retention of digital information using electromagnetic, optical, or silicon-based media. In a computing system, it is the subsystem responsible for **recording (writing)**, **preserving**, and **retrieving (reading)** data through a hierarchy of memory and storage devices.

From a KTU examination perspective, data storage is classified along three fundamental axes:

1. **By Architecture / Connectivity**: Direct Attached Storage (DAS), Network Attached Storage (NAS), Storage Area Network (SAN), and Cloud Storage.
2. **By Access Pattern / Interface**: Block Storage, File Storage, and Object Storage.
3. **By Performance Tier**: Primary (Hot) Storage, Secondary (Warm) Storage, and Archival (Cold) Storage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):** *Data Storage* is the mechanism — both hardware and software — used to capture, retain, and manage digital information across volatile and non-volatile media in a way that supports reliability, availability, scalability, and data integrity for distributed computing systems.

---

## 1.2 What is Cloud Storage?

**Cloud Storage** is a service model in which digital data is stored, managed, and backed up on remote, distributed infrastructure that is exposed to consumers over a public or private network (typically the Internet / WAN) and is accessed on-demand as a utility service.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (NIST SP 800-145, widely adopted by KTU):** *Cloud Storage* is a model for enabling ubiquitous, convenient, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing/storage resources (e.g., networks, servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort or service provider interaction.

The four essential characteristics of Cloud Storage (carried over from NIST's definition of cloud computing) are:

- **On-demand self-service** — provisioning without human intervention at the provider side.
- **Broad network access** — reachable from any client device over standard protocols (HTTP, HTTPS, S3 API, NFS, iSCSI, etc.).
- **Resource pooling** — storage from multiple tenants is multiplexed onto the same physical infrastructure.
- **Rapid elasticity** — capacity can scale up or down, appearing as effectively unlimited to the user.
- **Measured service** — usage is metered (per-GB stored, per-GB transferred, per-I/O request).

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rental Warehouse" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Intuition for First-Time Learners**

Imagine you run a small business in Kerala and have **10,000 files of important documents**.

- **Owning a hard drive (DAS)** is like keeping all your files in a **locker in your own shop**. It is fast, private, and cheap — but if your shop floods (server crashes), everything is lost. You can never expand beyond the locker.

- **A NAS on your office LAN** is like keeping the locker in a **shared back room of your office building** that all staff can access over the internal corridor. Faster than running to another building, but if the building burns, the data is gone.

- **A SAN** is like a **dedicated, climate-controlled warehouse just next door**, connected to your office by a private express tunnel. Multiple shops in the same area share the tunnel.

- **Cloud Storage (WAN scale)** is like **renting space in Amazon's Pan-India warehouse network**. Your boxes are duplicated across warehouses in Bangalore, Mumbai, and Chennai. You can store 10 boxes or 10 million boxes. You pay only for the floor area and the number of times a delivery boy (data request) visits. If one warehouse burns, two more still have your boxes.

This analogy makes it intuitive why **Cloud Storage = Storage + WAN + Multi-tenancy + Elastic Billing**.

---

## 1.4 The Journey: From LAN Storage to WAN Cloud Storage

The evolution of storage architectures is one of the **most frequently tested topics** in KTU Module 2. The journey follows a clear progression in **geographic scope, sharing, and abstraction**:

| Generation | Architecture | Network Scope | Sharing | Typical Use |
|------------|--------------|---------------|---------|-------------|
| **G0 — Local Disk** | Direct Attached Storage (DAS) | Inside the server box | Single host | Boot drives, OS |
| **G1 — File Server on LAN** | File Server (NFS/CIFS) | LAN (≤1 km, ≤1 Gbps) | Departmental | Shared folders |
| **G2 — Dedicated LAN** | NAS | LAN | Workgroup | Home/SMB backups |
| **G3 — Dedicated High-Speed LAN** | SAN (Fibre Channel / iSCSI) | Dedicated LAN (up to 16 Gbps FC) | Enterprise | Databases, VMs |
| **G4 — Internet-Scale** | Cloud Storage (S3, Blob, GCS) | WAN (Internet) | Global / Multi-tenant | Web-scale apps, archives |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** The fundamental shift from LAN to WAN storage is that **the network itself stops being a bottleneck and starts being a feature**. Latency rises, but **reach, elasticity, and multi-tenancy** become possible. The protocols must therefore become **resilient, secure (TLS), and idempotent** — properties that LAN protocols (NFS, SMB) historically were not designed for.

---

## 1.5 The Three Core Data Storage Models

KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes that *Cloud Storage* is delivered through three primary abstractions, regardless of the underlying network (LAN or WAN):

1. **Block Storage** — Data is split into fixed-size *blocks* (typically **4 KB to 256 KB**), each with a unique address. The OS mounts the device as a raw volume. Highest performance; used for databases and boot disks.
2. **File Storage** — Data is exposed as a hierarchical *folder/file* tree via protocols such as **NFS (Linux)** and **SMB/CIFS (Windows)**. Shared, POSIX-like semantics. Used for content management and home directories.
3. **Object Storage** — Data is stored as discrete *objects* (data + metadata + unique ID) inside a flat namespace, accessed via **RESTful HTTP APIs** (e.g., Amazon S3 PUT/GET). Massively scalable, used for backups, media, and big data.

> [!NOTE]
> A common 3-mark KTU question is: *"Compare Block, File, and Object Storage."* The comparison table in Section 2.4 of these notes is calibrated exactly for that style of question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Cloud Storage System

A production-grade cloud storage stack is layered. From bottom to top:

1. **Physical Media Layer** — HDDs (spinning magnetic platters), SSDs (NAND flash), and Tape (LTO). Each has different $/GB and IOPS characteristics.
2. **Disk / Node Abstraction Layer** — Individual drives are aggregated into **JBODs** (Just a Bunch Of Disks) or hardware **RAID** controllers.
3. **Distributed Filesystem / Object Store Layer** — Software such as HDFS, Ceph, MinIO, or OpenStack Swift spreads data across many commodity nodes.
4. **Virtualization / Multi-tenancy Layer** — Hypervisors and storage virtualization engines (VASA, VMware vSAN) carve logical LUNs / buckets from the physical pool.
5. **API / Protocol Layer** — Exposes S3-compatible REST, NFS, SMB, iSCSI, or WebDAV endpoints to clients.
6. **Tenant & Billing Layer** — Metering, quotas, and authentication (IAM, OAuth).

> [!TIP]
> **Why It Matters:** KTU examiners often ask *why* cloud storage is more reliable than a single server. The answer lies in layers 2 and 3: **redundancy is enforced at multiple levels (RAID, replication, erasure coding)**, so failure of any single node is non-fatal.

---

## 2.2 The LAN-to-WAN Storage Evolution — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 Direct Attached Storage (DAS)
- Disk is physically inside or directly cabled (SATA, SAS, USB, Thunderbolt) to a single server.
- **Network**: None.
- **Access**: Only the host's operating system.
- **Limit**: No sharing, no scaling beyond chassis.

### 2.2.2 Network Attached Storage (LAN-Scale File Sharing)
- A dedicated **file server appliance** exposes file systems over the LAN.
- **Protocols**: NFS v3/v4 (Linux/UNIX), SMB/CIFS (Windows), AFP (legacy macOS).
- **Network**: Standard Ethernet LAN (1 Gbps, 10 Gbps).
- **Advantages**: Simple, cheap, supports mixed-OS clients, file locking.
- **Limitations**: Limited by LAN bandwidth; single point of failure if not clustered; not designed for WAN latency.

### 2.2.3 Storage Area Network (High-Speed LAN)
- A **dedicated, lossless, switched fabric** (Fibre Channel or iSCSI over Ethernet) connects servers to a pool of disks.
- **Protocols**: Fibre Channel (FC) up to **32 Gbps** per link; iSCSI over **10/25/100 GbE**; FCoE.
- **Advantages**: Block-level access, deterministic low latency, ideal for databases, virtual machine datastores.
- **Limitations**: Very expensive, requires specialized HBAs and switches, geographically limited to a single data centre campus.

### 2.2.4 Cloud Storage (WAN-Scale Object / Block / File)
- Storage is offered **as a service** over the public Internet (WAN) or a private MPLS link.
- **Protocols**: S3-compatible HTTP REST, Azure Blob REST, Google Cloud Storage JSON API, NFS-over-WAN.
- **Architectural Pillars**:
  - **Geographic Distribution** — Data replicated across **3 or more availability zones** within a region.
  - **Erasure Coding / Replication** — Instead of RAID, systems use **Reed-Solomon codes** like (10, 4) — 10 data + 4 parity shards — tolerating 4 simultaneous node failures.
  - **Eventual Consistency** — Writes propagate asynchronously; reads are strongly consistent in modern systems like Amazon S3 (post-2020).
  - **Multi-tenancy** — Logical isolation via namespaces, encryption keys (BYOK, SSE-KMS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked *"Why did storage evolve from LAN to WAN?"* answer along four vectors: **(a) Scalability** (LAN storage caps at the rack), **(b) Cost** (CAPEX → OPEX), **(c) Accessibility** (anywhere, any device), **(d) Resilience** (single-tenant hardware cannot match multi-AZ replication).

---

## 2.3 Key Engineering Metrics for Storage Systems

These metrics are **guaranteed to appear** in KTU numerical questions, derivations, and Part A definitions.

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Typical Unit |
|--------|--------|------------|--------------|
| **Capacity** | $C$ | Total storable bytes | TiB, PiB |
| **IOPS** | $I$ | I/O Operations Per Second | ops/s |
| **Throughput** | $T$ | Data transferred per unit time | MB/s, Gbps |
| **Latency** | $\tau$ | Time to complete a single I/O | ms, µs |
| **Replication Factor** | $r$ | Number of copies kept | dimensionless |
| **Durability** | $D$ | Probability of data survival | "eleven 9s" = 99.999999999% |
| **Availability** | $A$ | Fraction of time the service is reachable | 99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999% |

---

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comparison + Equations)

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Formatting Note for Students:** In KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners use vertical bars to denote "given" (e.g., "$\vert$ used for absolute value). The equations below are reproduced using standard mathematical notation.

### 2.4.1 Storage Capacity with Replication

When data of size $S$ is stored with replication factor $r$, the **physical (gross) storage** consumed is:

$$
C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot r
$$

When **erasure coding** $(k, m)$ is used (i.e., $k$ data shards and $m$ parity shards):

$$
C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot \frac{(k + m)}{k} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Storage Efficiency} \; \eta = \frac{k}{k+m}
$$

### 2.4.2 RAID Capacity Table

| RAID Level | Min. Disks | Fault Tolerance | Usable Capacity Formula | Read Perf | Write Perf |
|------------|-----------|-----------------|--------------------------|-----------|------------|
| RAID 0 | 2 | 0 | $n \cdot D$ | High | High |
| RAID 1 | 2 | $n-1$ | $D$ | High | Medium |
| RAID 5 | 3 | 1 | $(n-1) \cdot D$ | High | Medium |
| RAID 6 | 4 | 2 | $(n-2) \cdot D$ | High | Medium-Low |
| RAID 10 | 4 | 1 per mirror | $(n/2) \cdot D$ | Very High | High |

Here $n$ is the number of disks and $D$ is the capacity of a single disk.

### 2.4.3 RAID 5 Usable Capacity Derivation

$$
C_{\text{usable}}^{\text{RAID5}} = (n - 1) \cdot D
$$

The single parity disk is "spent" for fault tolerance, so one disk's worth of capacity is reserved.

### 2.4.4 Data Transfer Time Across a Network

Given a file of size $F$ (in bits) and a link bandwidth $B$ (in bits per second):

$$
t_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{F}{B} + \tau_{\text{RTT}} \cdot N_{\text{chunks}}
$$

where $N_{\text{chunks}}$ is the number of TCP/HTTP requests and $\tau_{\text{RTT}}$ is the round-trip time of the WAN.

### 2.4.5 Annual Downtime from Availability SLA

$$
\text{Downtime per year} = (1 - A) \cdot 365 \cdot 24 \cdot 60 \; \text{minutes}
$$

For $A = 99.99\%$ (commonly called "four nines"):

$$
\text{Downtime} = 0.0001 \cdot 525600 \approx 52.56 \; \text{minutes/year}
$$

### 2.4.6 Durability of Replicated Data

If a single disk has an Annual Failure Rate (AFR) of $f$, and data is replicated across $r$ independent disks, the probability of losing **all** copies in a year is:

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = f^r
$$

The **durability** is:

$$
D = 1 - f^r
$$

> [!TIP]
> Amazon S3 quotes durability as $99.999999999\%$ (eleven 9s), achieved by storing objects across **at least 3 availability zones** and using **cross-region replication** for critical buckets.

### 2.4.7 Object Storage vs. Block Storage Throughput Bound

For a sequentially read object of size $F$ over a network of bandwidth $B$:

$$
T_{\text{observed}} = \min\!\left( T_{\text{disk}},\; B \right)
$$

The bottleneck is whichever is smaller — disk read speed or WAN bandwidth.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas are not academic. They are used daily by:

- **Cloud Architects** sizing S3 buckets, EBS volumes, and Azure Disks.
- **SREs** calculating SLA-driven storage cost ($/GB-month) versus the value of the data.
- **Database Engineers** choosing between local NVMe, EBS io2, and FSx for Lustre.
- **Data Engineers** sizing HDFS DataNodes using the $(k,m)$ erasure-coding parameters.
- **Network Engineers** sizing WAN links between on-premises and AWS Direct Connect / Azure ExpressRoute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Module 2 questions test whether you can **derive usable capacity** and **downtime from availability percentages**. Practice the formulas in 2.4.1 through 2.4.5 — they appear in Part A (3 marks) and as sub-parts of Part B (14 marks).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Mandate:** Every algebraic step below is written out in full. No shortcuts. KTU valuation gives **partial marks for intermediate steps**, so learning the *derivation path* matters as much as the final number.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Usable Capacity in a Replicated Cloud Object Store

**Problem Setup (Typical 7-mark sub-question):**
A company stores **12 TB of data** in an S3-compatible cloud object store. The provider replicates every object across **3 independent availability zones**. Each object also has **one versioning copy** retained for 30 days, which is stored on **Infrequent Access (IA) tier** that is also 3-way replicated. Calculate:
1. The gross physical storage consumed by the primary data.
2. The gross physical storage consumed by the IA-versioned data, if the versioning adds exactly **25%** of the primary data volume.
3. The total cost if the primary tier costs **₹1.80/GB-month** and the IA tier costs **₹0.80/GB-month**.

### Step 1 — Convert TB to GB (using the storage-industry convention $1 \text{ TiB} \approx 1.0995 \text{ TB}$, but for KTU we use $1 \text{ TB} = 1000 \text{ GB}$ to match the question's unit).

$$
S = 12 \text{ TB} = 12 \times 1000 = 12000 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the replication formula $C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot r$.

$$
C_{\text{primary}} = 12000 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 36000 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the IA-versioned data volume.

$$
S_{\text{IA}} = 0.25 \times 12000 = 3000 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply replication to the IA tier.

$$
C_{\text{IA}} = 3000 \text{ GB} \times 3 = 9000 \text{ GB}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute monthly cost (cost = capacity in GB × per-GB-month rate).

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{primary}} = 36000 \times 1.80 = 64800 \; \text{INR/month}
$$

$$
\text{Cost}_{\text{IA}} = 9000 \times 0.80 = 7200 \; \text{INR/month}
$$

### Step 6 — Sum the costs.

$$
\text{Total Cost} = 64800 + 7200 = 72000 \; \text{INR/month}
$$

### Final Answer (Model-Answer Format for KTU Valuation):

| Item | Capacity (GB) | Rate (₹/GB-mo) | Cost (₹/mo) |
|------|--------------:|---------------:|------------:|
| Primary (3-way replicated) | 36000 | 1.80 | 64800 |
| IA Versioned (3-way replicated) | 9000 | 0.80 | 7200 |
| **Total** | **45000** | — | **72000** |

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the replication factor and compute cost on raw 12000 GB, losing **2 of the 7 marks**. Always restate the formula $C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot r$ before plugging in numbers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SLA-Driven Downtime Calculation

**Problem:** A cloud storage service offers an availability SLA of **99.95%**. How many minutes of downtime is a customer allowed per **calendar year**? Per **typical 30-day month**?

### Step 1 — Compute annual downtime in minutes.

A non-leap year has $365 \times 24 \times 60 = 525600$ minutes.

$$
\text{Allowed Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525600
$$

$$
= 0.0005 \times 525600
$$

$$
= 262.80 \;\text{minutes/year}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to hours and minutes for the model answer.

$$
262.80 \text{ min} = 4 \text{ hours } 22 \text{ min } 48 \text{ seconds}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the 30-day equivalent.

A 30-day month has $30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43200$ minutes.

$$
\text{Allowed Downtime}_{\text{month}} = 0.0005 \times 43200 = 21.6 \text{ min/month}
$$

### Final Answer:

| Period | Allowed Downtime |
|--------|------------------|
| Per year (525600 min) | 262.80 min ≈ 4 h 22 min |
| Per 30-day month (43200 min) | 21.60 min |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Substituting leap-year 366 × 24 × 60 is acceptable; both 525600 and 527040 will be marked correct. The **substitution step** is what carries the marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Storage Capacity with Erasure Coding

**Problem:** A data lake uses **HDFS-style erasure coding** with parameters $(k, m) = (6, 3)$. A dataset of **24 TB** is to be stored.

1. What is the gross physical capacity consumed?
2. What is the storage efficiency $\eta$?
3. How many simultaneous disk failures can the system tolerate?

### Step 1 — Apply the erasure-coding gross capacity formula.

$$
C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot \frac{k + m}{k} = 24 \cdot \frac{6+3}{6}
$$

$$
= 24 \cdot 1.5 = 36 \text{ TB}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the storage efficiency.

$$
\eta = \frac{k}{k+m} = \frac{6}{9} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%
$$

### Step 3 — Tolerance to failure.

The system tolerates up to $m = 3$ simultaneous disk or node failures.

### Final Answer:

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|------:|
| Gross capacity | 36 TB |
| Storage efficiency | 66.67% |
| Failure tolerance | 3 simultaneous failures |

> [!TIP]
> **Compare with 3-way replication:** $r = 3 \Rightarrow C_{\text{gross}} = 72 \text{ TB}, \eta = 33.33\%$. Erasure coding is **twice as storage-efficient** while still tolerating 3 failures. This is why modern cloud object stores (Azure Blob, Google GCS Coldline) use erasure coding by default.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Network Transfer Time for Backing Up to Cloud

**Problem:** A 50 GB database is to be uploaded from a KTU campus in Trivandrum to an S3 bucket in the Mumbai region. The WAN link has a measured bandwidth of **100 Mbps** and a round-trip time of **40 ms**. The upload is performed in 10 MB HTTP chunks. Calculate the total transfer time.

### Step 1 — Convert units.

$$
F = 50 \text{ GB} = 50 \times 8 \times 1024 = 409600 \; \text{Mbits}
$$

$$
B = 100 \text{ Mbps}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the number of chunks.

$$
N_{\text{chunks}} = \frac{50 \times 1024}{10} = 5120 \text{ chunks}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the transfer time formula.

$$
t_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{F}{B} + \tau_{\text{RTT}} \cdot N_{\text{chunks}}
$$

$$
= \frac{409600}{100} + 0.040 \times 5120
$$

$$
= 4096 \text{ s} + 204.8 \text{ s} = 4300.8 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to minutes.

$$
t_{\text{transfer}} \approx 71.68 \text{ minutes}
$$

### Step 5 — Observation: In this example, **throughput-bound**, not latency-bound.

If the chunks were **5 MB each**, $N_{\text{chunks}}$ would double to 10240 and the latency overhead would jump to 409.6 s, becoming significant. KTU examiners may ask: *"At what chunk size does the protocol overhead become negligible (say, < 5%)?"* The answer is found by setting $\tau_{\text{RTT}} \cdot N < 0.05 \cdot F / B$, which is a great 3-mark follow-up.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Block vs. File vs. Object Storage

This is the **most common 7-mark KTU question** in Module 2.

| Feature | Block Storage | File Storage | Object Storage |
|---------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Access Granularity** | Fixed-size blocks (4 KB–256 KB) | Files and directories | Whole objects via unique key |
| **Interface** | iSCSI, FC, NVMe-oF | NFS, SMB/CIFS, AFP | REST/HTTP (S3, Azure Blob) |
| **Metadata** | LBA only (block address) | POSIX metadata (name, mtime, ACL) | Custom, rich, user-defined |
| **Mutability** | Yes — partial overwrites | Yes — partial overwrites | No — write-once, replace whole object |
| **Scalability** | Limited to a single LUN | Limited to filesystem (e.g., 2^32 inodes) | Effectively unlimited (10^11 objects) |
| **Performance** | Highest (sub-ms latency) | Moderate (ms latency) | Variable (10 ms – 1 s over WAN) |
| **Cost per GB** | Highest | Moderate | Lowest |
| **Typical Use** | Databases, OS boot | Home dirs, CMS, NFS shares | Backups, media, big data, archives |
| **Cloud Examples** | AWS EBS, Azure Disk | AWS EFS, Azure Files | AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS |

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Write This in the Exam:** Use a 3-column table (Storage Type as column headers, features as rows). Always end with one line of synthesis: *"Object storage is the only model that scales to WAN/Internet scope because it sacrifices POSIX partial-write semantics for HTTP-based flat addressing."*

---

## 3.6 Durability Calculation — Eleven Nines of S3

Amazon S3 famously claims **99.999999999%** (eleven 9s) of durability. Let us *verify* this by reverse-engineering the AFR.

Let us model S3 as storing an object with $r = 3$ replicas across independent availability zones. Assume each disk has an AFR of $f = 0.78\%$ (industry average for enterprise HDDs).

### Step 1 — Probability that all 3 replicas fail in the same year.

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = f^3 = (0.0078)^3 = 4.74 \times 10^{-7}
$$

### Step 2 — Durability.

$$
D = 1 - 4.74 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.9999995 \;(\text{only six nines})
$$

### Step 3 — To achieve **eleven nines** ($D = 1 - 10^{-11}$), the system cannot rely on a 3-way replication of HDDs alone. It must additionally:

- Use **erasure coding with cross-region replication** to reduce $P_{\text{loss}}$ below $10^{-11}$.
- Perform continuous **integrity checksumming** and self-healing (e.g., scrubbing).
- Re-replicate data as soon as a failure is detected, well within the AFR window.

This is a classic *why cloud storage is more reliable than on-premises* KTU justification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below use **Mermaid syntax** with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safety rules applied — alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no markdown formatting inside labels.

---

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Evolution of Storage Architectures from LAN to WAN

This block diagram shows how storage systems have evolved in **geographic scope**, **sharing model**, and **underlying network**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DAS - Direct Attached Storage\nInside server chassis\nSATA or SAS cables\nNo Network"] --> nodeB["File Server on LAN\nNFS or SMB protocol\nStandard Ethernet 1 Gbps\nSingle Department"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["NAS - Network Attached Storage\nDedicated file appliance\nMixed NFS and SMB\nWorkgroup Scale"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["SAN - Storage Area Network\nFibre Channel or iSCSI\nUp to 32 Gbps FC\nEnterprise Datacenter"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Object Storage on WAN\nS3 or Azure Blob REST\nHTTPS over Internet\nGlobal Multi-Tenant"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Cloud Native Storage\nMulti-AZ Replication\nErasure Coding\nGeo-Distributed"]

    classDef dasStyle fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef lanStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef sanStyle fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef wanStyle fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef cloudStyle fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000

    class nodeA dasStyle
    class nodeB,nodeC lanStyle
    class nodeD sanStyle
    class nodeE,nodeF wanStyle
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read this diagram in the exam:** Move from **left (DAS)** to **right (Cloud)** and notice that **the network scope widens** while **the physical control shrinks**. KTU loves the phrase *"control is traded for elasticity."*

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Layered Architecture of a Cloud Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["Layer 6 - Tenant and Billing API\nIAM Authentication\nMetering and Quotas\nPer-GB Billing"]
    layer2["Layer 5 - Protocol and API Endpoint\nS3 REST or NFS or SMB or iSCSI"]
    layer3["Layer 4 - Virtualization and Multi-Tenancy\nLogical LUNs or Buckets\nEncryption at Rest"]
    layer4["Layer 3 - Distributed Object Store\nCeph or HDFS or MinIO\nErasure Coding and Replication"]
    layer5["Layer 2 - Disk Aggregation\nJBOD or RAID 6\nHardware Controller"]
    layer6["Layer 1 - Physical Media\nHDD Spinning Disks\nSSD NAND Flash\nLTO Tape for Cold Tier"]

    layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4 --> layer5 --> layer6
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If asked *"How is data protected against hardware failure?"*, walk through this diagram from bottom to top: *physical disk fails → RAID tolerates it → node fails → erasure coding tolerates it → datacenter fails → cross-region replication tolerates it*. This 4-level defense is the *core* of cloud storage reliability.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Data Flow for an Object Upload to S3

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    clientA["Client Application\nBrowser or Mobile App"] -->|HTTPS PUT\nmultipart upload| gatewayA["API Gateway or Load Balancer\nTLS Termination\nAuthentication"]
    gatewayA --> authA["IAM Service\nVerifies Credentials\nSigns Request"]
    authA --> storeA["Object Storage Node\nS3 Bucket\nErasure Coded Shards"]
    storeA --> repA1["Availability Zone 1\n3 Copies Across Racks"]
    storeA --> repA2["Availability Zone 2\n3 Copies Across Racks"]
    storeA --> repA3["Availability Zone 3\n3 Copies Across Racks"]
    storeA --> metaA["Metadata Service\nVersion ID\nObject Tag Index"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **metadata service** is what makes object storage searchable by *user-defined tags* like `customer=KTU` or `region=Kerala` — something a traditional filesystem cannot do efficiently.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block Diagram of a Cloud Storage System (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blockA["Input\nClient Devices and Apps"] --> blockB["Frontend Layer\nLoad Balancer and API Gateway"]
    blockB --> blockC["Metadata Layer\nIndex and Authentication"]
    blockC --> blockD["Storage Layer\nDistributed Object Store\nWith Erasure Coding"]
    blockD --> blockE["Replication Layer\nCross-AZ and Cross-Region"]
    blockE --> blockF["Output\nPersistent Durable Storage\n99.999999999 percent SLA"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Pattern Note:** KTU 2024 ESE for OECST722 follows the standard template: **Part A — 3-mark short answers (5–8 questions, answer any 3 to 5)** and **Part B — 14-mark long answers with internal choice (answer 2 out of 3)**. The questions below are calibrated to this exact pattern.

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]`

> **Q: Differentiate between Direct Attached Storage (DAS), Network Attached Storage (NAS), and Cloud Storage. Mention the network scope and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Key Style):**

| Type | Network Scope | Limitation |
|------|---------------|------------|
| DAS | None (inside the server) | Cannot be shared across servers; single point of failure |
| NAS | LAN (1–10 Gbps Ethernet) | Bandwidth and latency degrade with concurrent users; not designed for WAN |
| Cloud Storage | WAN (Internet, MPLS, Direct Connect) | Higher latency than LAN; dependent on Internet uptime and egress cost |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]`

> **Q: List any three characteristics of Cloud Storage as per the NIST definition.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **On-demand self-service** — Users can provision storage automatically without human interaction with the provider.
2. **Broad network access** — Storage is reachable over standard protocols (HTTP, NFS, SMB) from any client device.
3. **Resource pooling** — Physical storage is multiplexed across multiple tenants using virtualization.
4. *(Optional 4th)* **Rapid elasticity** — Capacity scales up/down with workload; **measured service** — usage is metered and billed.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Model QP based on Dec 2023 pattern]`

> **Q: With the help of a neat diagram, explain the evolution of storage architectures from Direct Attached Storage (DAS) to Cloud Storage. Discuss the key changes in network scope, sharing model, and reliability mechanisms at each stage. (14 marks)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Evolution Diagram and Description (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The evolution of storage architectures reflects the increasing demand for **sharing, scalability, and geographic reach**. The journey can be summarized in five stages:

1. **Direct Attached Storage (DAS)** — Disks are physically inside or directly attached to a single server. The network scope is **zero**; the sharing model is **single-host**; reliability relies on **local RAID**.

2. **File Server on LAN** — A dedicated server exposes file systems over a **LAN** using **NFS** (UNIX) or **SMB** (Windows). Sharing is **departmental**; reliability is improved via server clustering.

3. **Network Attached Storage (NAS)** — A purpose-built file-serving appliance with its own operating system, optimized for **mixed-OS file sharing**. Network scope remains LAN, but reliability is enhanced with **RAID-5/6, dual controllers, and snapshots**.

4. **Storage Area Network (SAN)** — A **dedicated high-speed fabric** (Fibre Channel at 8/16/32 Gbps or iSCSI over 10/25 GbE) carries **block-level** traffic. SANs are deployed within a single datacenter. Reliability mechanisms include **dual-fabric paths, redundant switches, and replication between SAN arrays**.

5. **Cloud Storage (WAN scale)** — Storage is delivered as a service over the **Internet (WAN)** using RESTful APIs. Reliability is achieved through **multi-AZ replication, erasure coding, and cross-region replication**, providing **eleven 9s of durability** (e.g., Amazon S3).

*(Attach the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 for the full 7 marks.)*

#### Sub-part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 marks)

| Feature | DAS | NAS | SAN | Cloud Storage |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|----------------|
| **Network** | None | LAN | Dedicated FC/iSCSI LAN | WAN (Internet) |
| **Protocol** | SATA/SAS | NFS/SMB | FC, iSCSI | HTTP/REST, S3 API |
| **Access Type** | Block | File | Block | Object/Block/File |
| **Sharing** | Single host | Departmental | Enterprise | Global multi-tenant |
| **Reliability** | RAID | RAID + clustering | Dual-fabric paths | Erasure coding + geo-replication |
| **Cost Model** | High CAPEX | Moderate CAPEX | High CAPEX | OPEX (pay-per-GB) |
| **Scalability** | Limited to chassis | Limited to LAN | Limited to datacenter | Virtually unlimited |

> **Synthesis (for the final 1–2 marks):** The transition from LAN to WAN is driven by the need for **elasticity, global access, and zero-touch reliability**. Cloud Storage achieves this by replacing dedicated hardware with **virtualized, software-defined, multi-tenant infrastructure**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Model QP based on June 2024 pattern]`

> **Q: (a) Compare Block, File, and Object Storage in terms of access granularity, interface protocols, scalability, and typical use cases. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A cloud provider offers an SLA of 99.9% for its object storage service. Calculate the maximum allowed downtime per year and per 30-day month. If the same data is replicated across 3 independent disks each with an Annual Failure Rate (AFR) of 1%, what is the resulting durability? Comment on whether this meets the eleven-9s SLA of S3. (7 marks)**

#### Sub-part (a) — Block vs. File vs. Object Storage (7 marks)

| Feature | Block Storage | File Storage | Object Storage |
|---------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Access Granularity** | Fixed-size blocks (4 KB–256 KB) | Files and folders | Whole objects identified by unique key |
| **Interface Protocols** | iSCSI, FC, NVMe-oF | NFS, SMB/CIFS | REST/HTTP, S3 API |
| **Metadata** | Block address (LBA) only | POSIX metadata (name, mtime, owner) | User-defined, rich, custom tags |
| **Mutability** | Partial overwrites allowed | Partial overwrites allowed | Write once, replace whole object |
| **Scalability** | Limited to a single LUN | Limited to ~10⁹ inodes | 10¹¹+ objects per bucket |
| **Typical Use Cases** | Databases, OS boot, VMs | Home directories, CMS | Backups, media archives, data lakes |
| **Cloud Examples** | AWS EBS, Azure Disk | AWS EFS, Azure Files | AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS |

**Synthesis:** *Object Storage is the only model natively designed for WAN-scale access because it replaces POSIX semantics with HTTP-based flat addressing and rich user-defined metadata.*

#### Sub-part (b) — Downtime and Durability Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Allowed annual downtime at 99.9% SLA.**

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{year}} = (1 - 0.999) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60
$$

$$
= 0.001 \times 525600 = 525.6 \text{ min/year} \approx 8.76 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — Allowed monthly downtime.**

$$
\text{Downtime}_{\text{month}} = 0.001 \times 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 432 \text{ min/month} \approx 7.2 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Durability with 3-way replication at AFR = 1%.**

$$
P_{\text{loss}} = f^3 = (0.01)^3 = 10^{-6}
$$

$$
D = 1 - 10^{-6} = 0.999999 = 99.9999\% \quad (\text{only six nines})
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with S3's eleven 9s.**

$$
D_{\text{S3}} = 1 - 10^{-11} = 99.999999999\%
$$

The 3-way replication model with 1% AFR yields only **six nines**, which is **5 orders of magnitude worse** than S3. To reach eleven 9s, the provider must additionally use **erasure coding, cross-region replication, continuous integrity checksumming, and self-healing** beyond simple 3-way replication.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic — 5 Critical Pitfalls**

1. **Forgetting the replication factor in capacity calculations.** The single most common error in KTU cloud computing papers. Always state $C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot r$ **before** plugging in numbers. Examiners award marks for showing the formula.

2. **Confusing Availability with Durability.** Availability is *uptime of the service* (99.9% per year). Durability is *long-term survival of data* (eleven 9s per year). Many students write "durability = 99.99%" and lose 2 marks.

3. **Writing "Cloud Storage = Internet"** — Cloud Storage can be deployed over **private WANs, MPLS, or Direct Connect** circuits. Over-generalization costs marks.

4. **Confusing NAS with SAN.** NAS = **file-level** access over **Ethernet LAN**. SAN = **block-level** access over **Fibre Channel or iSCSI fabric**. This distinction is a guaranteed 3-mark short-answer question.

5. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** "1 TB" vs. "1 TiB" vs. "1000 GB" vs. "1024 GB" — examiners are strict. Use $1 \text{ TB} = 1000 \text{ GB}$ unless the question explicitly says TiB.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this checklist on your wall before the exam.**

### A. Definitions (Memorize Verbatim for 2-Mark Questions)
- **DAS** — Storage physically attached to a single host. No network.
- **NAS** — File-level storage served over LAN via NFS/SMB.
- **SAN** — Block-level storage over a dedicated high-speed fabric (FC/iSCSI).
- **Cloud Storage** — Storage delivered as a service over WAN with elasticity and multi-tenancy.
- **Block Storage** — Data addressed as fixed-size blocks.
- **Object Storage** — Data addressed as flat-namespace objects with rich metadata, accessed via REST.

### B. Key Numbers (Memorize for Numericals)
- Replication factor of typical cloud object stores: **$r = 3$**
- Erasure coding defaults: **$(6, 3)$** for HDFS, **$(10, 4)$** for Azure Blob
- S3 durability: **$1 - 10^{-11}$ (eleven 9s)**
- One non-leap year: **525600 minutes**
- Default block size: **4 KB to 256 KB**
- Default HTTP chunk for multipart upload: **5 MB – 100 MB**

### C. Key Formulas (Practice Twice)
- $C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot r$
- $C_{\text{gross}} = S \cdot \dfrac{k+m}{k}$ (erasure coding)
- $\text{Downtime} = (1 - A) \times 525600 \; \text{min/year}$
- $P_{\text{loss}} = f^r$, $D = 1 - f^r$
- $t_{\text{transfer}} = F/B + \tau_{\text{RTT}} \cdot N_{\text{chunks}}$

### D. Common 14-Mark Question Templates
- *"Compare DAS/NAS/SAN/Cloud with a diagram"* — always include a **synthesis sentence**.
- *"Calculate gross capacity and cost for an S3-like store"* — show replication factor clearly.
- *"Derive availability / downtime from SLA"* — show the year-minute constant 525600.
- *"Compare Block / File / Object storage"* — use a 3-column table.

### E. Last-Minute Exam Phrases (Use These Wordings)
- *"Cloud Storage trades physical control for elasticity and global reach."*
- *"Reliability in cloud storage is layered: RAID + replication + erasure coding + cross-region copy."*
- *"Object storage is the natural fit for WAN because it replaces POSIX semantics with RESTful flat addressing."*
- *"Durability and Availability are independent dimensions of cloud storage SLAs."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
