topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing Services - Cloud Computing Elements, Understanding Services and Applications by Type, Cloud Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 3 — Cloud Computing Services

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OECST722 — Module 3)**
> *Cloud Computing Services* encompass the on-demand, network-accessible, pay-per-use delivery of IT resources — including compute, storage, networking, platforms, and software — over the Internet with minimal management effort from the consumer. The **Cloud Computing Elements** form the building blocks of any cloud ecosystem, while the **Service Models** (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS, etc.) define the abstraction layer at which a consumer interacts with the cloud.

### 🔑 The Five Core Cloud Computing Elements
According to the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture and KTU module prescription, every cloud system is composed of **five fundamental elements**:

| # | Element | One-line Academic Meaning |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Client (Front-End)** | The device + interface through which the user consumes cloud resources. |
| 2 | **Services (Cloud Layer)** | The actual logical resources (compute, storage, network, applications) provided on demand. |
| 3 | **Application (Software)** | The SaaS layer — software delivered over the Internet (e.g., Gmail, Office 365). |
| 4 | **Platform (Runtime/Middleware)** | The PaaS layer — frameworks, OS, DBs, and runtimes on which apps are built. |
| 5 | **Infrastructure (Servers & Storage)** | The IaaS layer — virtualized physical hardware (CPU, RAM, Disk, Network). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva**: *"C — S — A — P — I"* → **C**lient, **S**ervices, **A**pplications, **P**latform, **I**nfrastructure. Always enumerate from the user side inward.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electricity Grid"

Imagine cloud computing exactly like the **electricity supplied by the KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board)** to your home:

- You do **not** own a power plant at home. You just plug in.
- You pay **only for the units** (kilowatt-hours) you consume.
- The **wires** (network/Internet) carry the electricity (data/services) to your home.
- The **meter box** at your doorstep is like the **Client/Front-End** — it measures and displays what you use.
- The **transformer and grid station** are the **Infrastructure (IaaS)**.
- The **standard 230V socket and the wiring standards** are the **Platform (PaaS)**.
- The **actual appliance** (fan, fridge, TV) is the **Application/Software (SaaS)**.

Thus, the *service* is electricity, the *elements* are the grid components, and the *abstraction* (socket vs. raw current) defines the **service type** you consume.

### 📐 Key Standard Metrics (KTU-Frequently Tested)
- **Latency** — Round-trip time, expressed in **milliseconds (ms)**. Typical cloud SLA: < **100 ms** for real-time APIs.
- **Throughput** — Data processed per unit time, in **MB/s** or **IOPS** (Input/Output Operations Per Second).
- **Uptime / Availability** — Expressed as a percentage. Industry standard: **99.9% ("three nines")** ≈ 8.76 hours downtime/year; **99.99% ("four nines")** ≈ 52.56 minutes/year.
- **Elasticity Ratio** — $E = \dfrac{\text{Peak Capacity}}{\text{Baseline Capacity}}$.
- **Multi-Tenancy Efficiency** — Number of tenants served per physical host.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cloud Computing Elements — The Architectural Stack

The five elements are arranged in a **layered reference architecture**. Each lower layer abstracts the complexity of the layer below it.

### 🔹 Layer 1 — Client (Front-End)
- **Components**: Web browser, mobile app, thin client, terminal, API client.
- **Responsibilities**: Authentication, request formatting (REST/HTTP), rendering responses.
- **Types of Clients**:
  - **Mobile Client** — Smartphones/tablets.
  - **Thin Client** — Minimal local processing; depends on the cloud.
  - **Thick Client** — Performs local computation, syncs with cloud.
  - **Zero Client** — No local OS; pure display device (e.g., Citrix).

### 🔹 Layer 2 — Services (The Cloud Software Layer)
- The actual logical deliverables — **Compute, Storage, Database, Networking, Security, Analytics, AI/ML**.
- This is where **consumption-based pricing** and **SLAs (Service Level Agreements)** are enforced.

### 🔹 Layer 3 — Application
- Delivered as **SaaS** — Software hosted and managed entirely by the provider.
- Accessed via browser, mobile app, or API.
- Examples: **Microsoft 365**, **Google Workspace**, **Salesforce CRM**.

### 🔹 Layer 4 — Platform
- The **runtime ecosystem**: OS, language runtimes (JVM, Node.js, Python), databases, message queues.
- Delivered as **PaaS** — e.g., **AWS Elastic Beanstalk**, **Google App Engine**, **Azure App Service**.
- Eliminates the need to manage underlying OS, patching, capacity planning.

### 🔹 Layer 5 — Infrastructure
- The **physical + virtualized hardware layer**: servers, storage arrays, network switches, hypervisors.
- Delivered as **IaaS** — e.g., **AWS EC2**, **Google Compute Engine (GCE)**, **Azure VMs**.
- The consumer provisions **VMs, volumes, and VPCs** programmatically via API.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"Differentiate between PaaS and IaaS using an example."* → PaaS = You bring code, provider brings OS+runtime (Google App Engine). IaaS = You bring code + OS, provider brings raw VM (AWS EC2 where you install Linux yourself).

---

## 2.2 Understanding Services and Applications by Type — The Service Model Pyramid

The **NIST SP 500-292** reference model classifies cloud services into three primary service models, extended in modern literature to include FaaS, MBaaS, CaaS, etc. Collectively called **"XaaS" (Anything as a Service)**.

### 🏗️ The Service Model Pyramid (Bottom = More Control, Top = Less Control)

```
                ┌──────────────────────┐
                │      SaaS            │  ← Application Layer
                │  (Email, CRM, ERP)   │
                ├──────────────────────┤
                │      PaaS            │  ← Platform Layer
                │ (App Engine, Heroku) │
                ├──────────────────────┤
                │      IaaS            │  ← Infrastructure Layer
                │ (EC2, Azure VM, GCE) │
                └──────────────────────┘
```

### 📊 Service Model Comparison Matrix

| Attribute | **IaaS** | **PaaS** | **SaaS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Consumer manages** | Apps, Data, Runtime, Middleware, OS | Apps, Data | Just usage |
| **Provider manages** | Virtualization, Servers, Storage, Networking | OS, Middleware, Runtime, plus all below | Everything end-to-end |
| **Typical Users** | Sysadmins, DevOps engineers | Application developers | End users / business teams |
| **Example Providers** | AWS EC2, Azure VM, GCE | AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Google App Engine | Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce |
| **Time to Deploy** | Minutes–Hours | Seconds–Minutes | Instant (sign-up) |
| **Flexibility** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Highest | ⭐⭐⭐ Medium | ⭐ Lowest |
| **KTU Mention Frequency** | Very High | Very High | Very High |

### 🌐 Extended "XaaS" Taxonomy (Important for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

| Service | Full Form | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FaaS** | Function as a Service | Run event-driven functions; pay only for execution time. | AWS Lambda, Azure Functions |
| **CaaS** | Container as a Service | Managed container orchestration. | AWS ECS, Google Kubernetes Engine |
| **DBaaS** | Database as a Service | Managed relational/NoSQL databases. | Amazon RDS, Firebase |
| **MBaaS** | Mobile Backend as a Service | Push notifications, auth, storage for mobile. | Firebase, AWS Amplify |
| **STaaS** | Storage as a Service | Object, block, or file storage on demand. | Amazon S3, Azure Blob |
| **NaaS** | Network as a Service | Virtual networks, load balancers, firewalls. | AWS VPC, Azure VNet |
| **SECaaS** | Security as a Service | Identity, DDoS protection, WAF. | Cloudflare, AWS Shield |
| **AIaaS** | AI as a Service | Pre-trained ML models, NLP, vision APIs. | AWS SageMaker, Google Vertex AI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** For Part B 14-mark answers, **always draw the service model pyramid** and **tabulate responsibilities** between consumer and provider. Examiners award dedicated marks for the diagram.

---

## 2.3 Cloud Services — The Core Service Categories

Modern public cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) organize their offerings into well-defined service categories. These are the **Services element** of the cloud architecture.

### ⚙️ 1. Compute Services
Provide processing power as **virtual CPUs (vCPUs)**, **GPU instances**, or **serverless functions**.
- **Virtual Machines** (EC2, Azure VM) — Persistent VMs with chosen OS.
- **Containers** (ECS, EKS, AKS, GKE) — Lightweight OS-level virtualization.
- **Serverless / FaaS** (Lambda, Azure Functions) — Event-driven, auto-scaling, billed per **GB-second**.
- **Batch / HPC** (AWS Batch, Azure Batch) — For tightly-coupled parallel workloads.

### 💾 2. Storage Services
- **Object Storage** (S3, Azure Blob) — For unstructured data (images, logs, backups). Accessed via HTTP.
- **Block Storage** (EBS, Azure Disk) — Low-latency volumes attached to VMs.
- **File Storage** (EFS, Azure Files) — Shared POSIX-compliant file systems.
- **Archival Storage** (Glacier, Azure Archive) — Cheap, slow retrieval.

**Durability Standard**: S3 Standard is designed for **99.999999999% (eleven 9's)** durability over a given year.

### 🗄️ 3. Database Services (DBaaS)
- **Relational (SQL)**: Amazon RDS, Azure SQL, Google Cloud SQL.
- **NoSQL – Key-Value**: Amazon DynamoDB, Azure Cosmos DB.
- **NoSQL – Document**: MongoDB Atlas.
- **NoSQL – Graph**: Amazon Neptune.
- **In-Memory Caching**: Amazon ElastiCache (Redis/Memcached).

### 🌐 4. Networking Services
- **Virtual Private Cloud (VPC)** — Logically isolated private network in the cloud.
- **Load Balancer** (ELB, Azure LB) — Distributes traffic across instances.
- **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — CloudFront, Azure CDN — caches content at edge POPs.
- **DNS** — Route 53, Azure DNS.
- **VPN / Direct Connect** — Hybrid connectivity from on-premise to cloud.

### 🔐 5. Security & Identity Services
- **IAM (Identity and Access Management)** — Role-based access control.
- **KMS (Key Management Service)** — Encryption key lifecycle.
- **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** — Layer-7 protection.
- **DDoS Protection** — AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection.

### 📈 6. Analytics, AI/ML, and IoT Services
- **Big Data**: EMR, Dataproc, HDInsight, BigQuery.
- **AI/ML**: SageMaker, Azure ML, Vertex AI.
- **IoT**: AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google IoT Core (deprecated, succeeded by partner solutions).

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Availability** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime + Downtime}} \times 100\%$ | % | SLA calculation |
| **Annual Downtime** | $D = 8760 \times (1 - A) \text{ hours}$ | hours/year | Translate % to hours |
| **Elasticity Ratio** | $E = \dfrac{C_{peak}}{C_{base}}$ | dimensionless | Auto-scaling questions |
| **Cost Pay-Per-Use** | $Cost = \sum_{i} U_i \times P_i$ where $U_i$ = usage, $P_i$ = unit price | currency | Billing questions |
| **Storage Durability (S3)** | $P_{loss} = 1 - 0.99999999999^{N}$ for $N$ objects | probability | Reliability questions |
| **Latency Budget** | $L_{total} = L_{net} + L_{proc} + L_{queue}$ | ms | Performance engineering |
| **Multi-Tenancy Density** | $M = \dfrac{\text{No. of Tenants}}{\text{No. of Hosts}}$ | tenants/host | Resource efficiency |
| **Serverless Billing (GB-s)** | $Cost = \lambda \cdot \bar{t}_{exec} \cdot M_{RAM} \cdot P_{GB\cdot s}$ | currency | Lambda cost estimation |
| **Throughput (IOPS)** | $IOPS = \dfrac{\text{No. of I/O Ops}}{\text{Time in seconds}}$ | ops/sec | Storage performance |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Annual Downtime from Availability SLA

**Given**: A cloud provider advertises an SLA of **99.95%** monthly availability. Compute the maximum permissible **annual downtime in minutes**.

### Step 1: Convert the availability percentage to a decimal.
$$A = 99.95\% = 0.9995$$

### Step 2: Express the fraction of allowable downtime.
$$1 - A = 1 - 0.9995 = 0.0005$$
This means 0.05% of the time the system may be unavailable.

### Step 3: Compute total hours in a year.
$$H_{year} = 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 4: Multiply downtime fraction by total hours.
$$D_{hours} = (1 - A) \times H_{year}$$
$$D_{hours} = 0.0005 \times 8760 = 4.38 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 5: Convert hours to minutes.
$$D_{min} = D_{hours} \times 60 = 4.38 \times 60 = 262.8 \text{ minutes}$$

> ✅ **Answer**: Maximum annual downtime = **262.8 minutes ≈ 4 hours 22 minutes 48 seconds per year**.

### 🎯 Reference: "Nines" Table for KTU

| Availability | Annual Downtime | KTU Memory Hint |
|---|---|---|
| 99% ("two nines") | 3.65 days | "1 in 100" |
| 99.9% ("three nines") | 8.77 hours | "Industry default SLA" |
| 99.95% | 4.38 hours | "Premium SLA" |
| 99.99% ("four nines") | 52.6 minutes | "Mission-critical" |
| 99.999% ("five nines") | 5.26 minutes | "Telecom-grade" |

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Serverless (Lambda) Cost Estimation

**Problem (KTU-Style)**: An AWS Lambda function is invoked on average **λ = 10,000 times per day**. Each invocation executes for an average of **t̄ = 200 ms**, allocated with **M = 512 MB** RAM. The unit price is **P = $0.00001667 per GB-second**. Compute the **monthly execution cost**. (Assume 30 days/month. AWS Lambda also offers a free tier of 1M requests/month and 400,000 GB-s/month — ignore for now.)

### Step 1: Compute total monthly invocations.
$$N = \lambda \times 30 = 10{,}000 \times 30 = 300{,}000 \text{ invocations}$$

### Step 2: Convert allocated memory to GB.
$$M_{GB} = \dfrac{512 \text{ MB}}{1024 \text{ MB/GB}} = 0.5 \text{ GB}$$

### Step 3: Convert execution time to seconds.
$$t_{s} = \dfrac{200 \text{ ms}}{1000} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

### Step 4: Compute GB-seconds consumed.
$$GB\text{-}s = N \times M_{GB} \times t_{s}$$
$$GB\text{-}s = 300{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 = 30{,}000 \text{ GB-s}$$

### Step 5: Apply the unit price.
$$Cost = GB\text{-}s \times P$$
$$Cost = 30{,}000 \times 0.00001667 = \$0.5001 \approx \$0.50$$

> ✅ **Answer**: The monthly bill is **≈ $0.50** for 300,000 invocations — demonstrating why serverless is preferred for **intermittent, spiky workloads**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Object Storage Durability Probability

**Problem**: Amazon S3 Standard is designed for **99.999999999%** annual durability. If a customer stores **N = 10 million (10⁷) objects**, what is the **expected probability of losing at least one object** in a year?

### Step 1: Express per-object durability.
$$p_{safe} = 0.99999999999$$

### Step 2: Express per-object loss probability.
$$p_{loss} = 1 - p_{safe} = 10^{-11}$$

### Step 3: Assuming independence, the probability that **all** N objects survive is:
$$P_{all\ survive} = p_{safe}^{N} = (0.99999999999)^{10^{7}}$$

### Step 4: Use the approximation $(1 - x)^{N} \approx e^{-Nx}$ for small x.
$$P_{all\ survive} \approx e^{-10^{7} \times 10^{-11}} = e^{-10^{-4}}$$
$$P_{all\ survive} \approx 1 - 10^{-4} = 0.9999$$

### Step 5: Probability of losing at least one object.
$$P_{\ge 1\ loss} = 1 - P_{all\ survive} \approx 10^{-4} = 0.01\%$$

> ✅ **Answer**: Even with 10 million objects, the expected annual loss probability is only **0.01%** — i.e., on average **1 object lost per 10,000 years**. This justifies S3's claim of "eleven 9s" durability.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Service Model Should I Choose?"

| Scenario | Recommended Service | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Hosting a static company website with no backend. | **SaaS** (Wix, Squarespace) | No DevOps overhead; provider manages everything. |
| Running a custom Java Spring Boot microservice. | **PaaS** (Elastic Beanstalk, App Engine) | Focus on code, not servers. |
| Migrating an on-premise Oracle DB to the cloud. | **IaaS** (EC2 + manually installed Oracle) | Fine-grained control over Oracle licensing. |
| Image-processing pipeline triggered by S3 uploads, running < 1 minute. | **FaaS** (Lambda) | Event-driven, cost-optimal for short bursts. |
| Long-running ML training job (8 hours, GPU). | **IaaS** (EC2 P3 instance) | Lambda max timeout = 15 min; persistent VM needed. |
| Globally distributed user authentication for a mobile app. | **MBaaS** (Firebase Auth, AWS Cognito) | Built-in OAuth, push, social login. |
| Storing 500 TB of compliance logs accessed once a quarter. | **STaaS — Archival** (Glacier Deep Archive) | Cheapest storage tier (~ $0.00099/GB/month). |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Service Selection — "PISCES"**:
> **P**ay-as-you-go → **I**nfrastructure control → **S**peed to market → **C**ost of ownership → **E**xpertise required → **S**cale requirement.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the five essential elements of Cloud Computing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The five essential elements of cloud computing are:

1. **Client (Front-End)** — The device and interface (browser, mobile app, thin client) through which the end user accesses cloud services.
2. **Services** — The actual on-demand IT resources delivered by the cloud, such as compute, storage, database, and networking.
3. **Application (Software)** — The SaaS-level software programs accessed over the Internet (e.g., Google Docs, Office 365).
4. **Platform** — The PaaS-level runtime environment, including operating systems, middleware, and development frameworks that support application development and deployment.
5. **Infrastructure** — The IaaS-level physical and virtualized hardware resources, including servers, storage arrays, and network equipment.

*(Mark Distribution: 0.5 mark per correct element + 0.5 mark for each brief explanation = 3 marks total.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Service Model | What Consumer Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** (Infrastructure as a Service) | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Apps, Data | AWS EC2, Azure Virtual Machines |
| **PaaS** (Platform as a Service) | Apps and Data only | Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk |
| **SaaS** (Software as a Service) | Only the user access | Gmail, Microsoft Office 365 |

IaaS offers the **highest level of control and flexibility**, requiring the consumer to manage the OS and runtime. PaaS abstracts the OS and middleware, allowing developers to focus on **code and data**. SaaS delivers a **fully managed application** to the end user with zero local installation. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinguishing line, 1 mark for examples.)*

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read before writing!):**
> - ❌ **Confusing "elements" with "service models"** — Elements are the *components* (Client, Services, App, Platform, Infra); service models are the *delivery abstractions* (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS).
> - ❌ **Missing the diagram** — In any 14-mark answer about service models, the pyramid/diagram carries **2–3 marks**. **Always draw it.**
> - ❌ **Using "cloud = internet" loosely** — Cloud is a **service paradigm with specific characteristics** (on-demand self-service, elasticity, measured service). Be precise.
> - ❌ **Skipping real-world examples** — KTU evaluators award marks for "industry relevance" examples.

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **five essential elements of cloud computing** with a neat block diagram. Describe how these elements interact during a typical user request to retrieve a document from cloud storage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **three primary cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)** with their **responsibility split between consumer and provider**. Use the service model pyramid. Compare with at least **three examples** for each model. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Cloud computing systems are built from five fundamental elements that work together to deliver on-demand IT resources. These elements were formalized in the NIST Reference Architecture and are central to the KTU 2024 OECST722 Module 3 syllabus.

**The Five Elements (3.5 marks):**

1. **Client (Front-End) Layer** — Represents the user-facing interface. It includes web browsers, mobile applications, thin clients, and API SDKs. The client is responsible for sending requests over HTTP/HTTPS and rendering responses. Example: Chrome browser used to access Google Drive.

2. **Services Layer** — The core cloud layer where logical services (compute, storage, database, networking) are exposed via APIs. It is the layer where **metering, billing, and SLA enforcement** occur.

3. **Application Layer** — The SaaS layer that hosts complete software products delivered to end users. Example: Salesforce CRM, Microsoft 365.

4. **Platform Layer** — The PaaS layer providing operating systems, language runtimes (Java, Python, Node.js), databases, and middleware. Developers deploy code onto this layer without worrying about the underlying hardware.

5. **Infrastructure Layer** — The IaaS layer comprising physical servers, storage devices, network switches, and the **hypervisor** that virtualizes them.

**Block Diagram (1.5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph layer1["CLIENT"]
        L1["Web Browser / Mobile App"]
    end
    subgraph layer2["SERVICES"]
        L2["Compute / Storage / DB APIs"]
    end
    subgraph layer3["APPLICATION"]
        L3["SaaS Software e.g. Office 365"]
    end
    subgraph layer4["PLATFORM"]
        L4["OS + Runtime + Middleware"]
    end
    subgraph layer5["INFRASTRUCTURE"]
        L5["Virtualized Servers + Storage + Network"]
    end
    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    classDef lyr fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class layer1,layer2,layer3,layer4,layer5 lyr
```

**Interaction Flow During a Document Download (1 mark):**
1. User clicks a document link in the **Client** browser.
2. The request is routed over HTTPS to the **Services** API gateway, which authenticates via IAM.
3. The **Application** layer (e.g., a webmail service) locates the document.
4. The **Platform** layer's runtime queries the **Database** for metadata.
5. The **Infrastructure** layer's object storage (e.g., S3) returns the bytes.
6. The response travels back up the stack, with **Services** metering the request and logging the event.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** The five elements form a layered stack where each layer abstracts the complexity of the layer below, enabling elastic and metered delivery of IT resources.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The NIST cloud reference model defines three primary service models based on **what the consumer manages versus what the provider manages**. These are IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS.

**The Service Model Pyramid (1.5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S["SaaS - Email CRM Office 365"]:::saasStyle
    P["PaaS - App Engine Beanstalk"]:::paasStyle
    I["IaaS - EC2 Azure VM GCE"]:::iaasStyle

    classDef saasStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef paasStyle fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iaasStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**IaaS (1.5 marks):** Provides raw virtualized hardware — VMs, volumes, virtual networks. The consumer installs the OS, middleware, runtime, and application. Maximum control, maximum responsibility.
- **Examples:** AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure Virtual Machines, Google Compute Engine, DigitalOcean Droplets, IBM Cloud Virtual Servers.

**PaaS (1.5 marks):** Provides a managed platform — OS + runtime + middleware — so the consumer only deploys code and data. Eliminates infrastructure management.
- **Examples:** Google App Engine, AWS Elastic Beanstalk, Heroku, Red Hat OpenShift, Azure App Service.

**SaaS (1 mark):** Provides a complete, ready-to-use application hosted by the provider. The consumer just signs in.
- **Examples:** Gmail, Microsoft 365, Slack, Salesforce, Dropbox, Zoom.

**Comparison Table (0.5 mark):**

| Aspect | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control | High | Medium | Low |
| Management Burden | On Consumer | Shared | On Provider |
| Target User | Sysadmins | Developers | End Users |
| Time to Deploy | Hours | Minutes | Instant |

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Choosing the right service model is a trade-off between **control, speed, and management overhead** — guided by the application's complexity, scale, and the team's expertise.

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Re-Appearing Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain the **classification of cloud services by type** — IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS, DBaaS, STaaS, and SECaaS. Tabulate the **consumer vs. provider responsibility** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup is hosting a mobile app with the following requirements: (i) 10,000 daily active users, (ii) 2 GB of user-generated photos per day, (iii) push notifications, (iv) server-side user authentication, (v) image-resizing triggered on photo upload. **Recommend suitable cloud services** (with justification) for each requirement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Cloud services are classified by the **level of abstraction** they provide to the consumer. Modern literature extends the NIST triad (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) with **"XaaS" (Anything as a Service)** to reflect the proliferation of specialized services.

**Classification with Examples (4 marks):**

| Service Type | Full Form | Provider Manages | Consumer Manages | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | Servers, Storage, Networking, Virtualization | OS, Middleware, Runtime, App, Data | AWS EC2 |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | OS, Middleware, Runtime + all below | App, Data | Google App Engine |
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Everything | Just user access | Office 365 |
| **FaaS** | Function as a Service | Runtime, Container, Scaling | Function code, Trigger | AWS Lambda |
| **DBaaS** | Database as a Service | Patching, Backup, Replication, Scaling | Schema, Queries | Amazon RDS |
| **STaaS** | Storage as a Service | Hardware, Replication, Durability | Objects, Buckets | Amazon S3 |
| **SECaaS** | Security as a Service | Threat detection, WAF, DDoS mitigation | Security policies | AWS Shield |

**Consumer vs. Provider Responsibility Diagram (1.5 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Application"] -->|Consumer| B["Data"]
    B -->|Consumer| C["Runtime"]
    C -->|Mixed| D["Middleware"]
    D -->|Provider in PaaS SaaS| E["OS"]
    E -->|Provider| F["Virtualization"]
    F -->|Provider| G["Hardware"]
```

**Why the "XaaS" Model Matters (1 mark):**
The XaaS taxonomy allows organizations to **cherry-pick** the right level of abstraction for each workload. A typical modern application uses a **mix** — e.g., S3 (STaaS) for storage, Lambda (FaaS) for processing, DynamoDB (DBaaS) for data, and CloudFront (CDN) for delivery.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** Understanding the service-type classification is essential for **cost optimization, scalability planning, and architectural design** in cloud-native development.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (0.5 mark):**
Modern cloud-native applications use a **composition of services** rather than monolithic infrastructure. Below is a service-wise recommendation for the startup's mobile app.

**Requirement-by-Requirement Recommendation (6 marks):**

| # | Requirement | Recommended Cloud Service | Service Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | 10,000 daily active users | **AWS EC2 Auto Scaling Group** behind an **Application Load Balancer** | IaaS + NaaS | Auto-scaling handles traffic spikes; pay only for what you use. |
| (ii) | 2 GB/day of user photos | **Amazon S3 Standard** | STaaS | Object storage with 11 nines durability; cheap ($0.023/GB/month); native integration with Lambda triggers. |
| (iii) | Push notifications | **Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM)** or **Amazon SNS** | MBaaS / PaaS | FCM provides free, cross-platform push notifications for iOS and Android. |
| (iv) | Server-side user authentication | **AWS Cognito** or **Firebase Authentication** | SECaaS / MBaaS | Provides OAuth 2.0, social login (Google/Apple/Facebook), and JWT issuance out-of-the-box. |
| (v) | Image resizing on upload | **AWS Lambda** triggered by S3 ObjectCreated event | FaaS | Event-driven, pay-per-execution; automatically resizes each uploaded photo without a dedicated server. |
| (Extra) | Database for user metadata | **Amazon DynamoDB** | DBaaS | Serverless NoSQL with single-digit-millisecond latency; auto-scales with users. |
| (Extra) | Content delivery to global users | **Amazon CloudFront** | CDN/NaaS | Caches resized images at edge locations, reducing latency. |

**Architecture Diagram (0.5 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Mobile App"] -->|HTTPS| CF["CloudFront CDN"]
    CF -->|Auth| COG["Cognito"]
    COG -->|JWT| API["API Gateway"]
    API -->|CRUD| DDB["DynamoDB"]
    M -->|Upload Photo| S3["S3 Bucket"]
    S3 -->|Trigger| LAM["Lambda Resize Function"]
    LAM -->|Store Thumbnail| S3
    LAM -->|Push Notification| SNS["SNS"]
    SNS --> M
```

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
This **serverless-first architecture** eliminates the need for the startup to manage any servers, reduces operational cost to near-zero at low scale, and provides **elastic scaling** as the user base grows — a textbook example of composing XaaS services for a mobile backend.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Five Cloud Elements** = **C-S-A-P-I** → Client, Services, Application, Platform, Infrastructure (always enumerate top-down).
- ✅ **Service Model Pyramid** → IaaS (bottom, max control) → PaaS (middle) → SaaS (top, zero control).
- ✅ **IaaS examples** = EC2, Azure VM, GCE.
- ✅ **PaaS examples** = App Engine, Beanstalk, Heroku.
- ✅ **SaaS examples** = Gmail, Office 365, Salesforce.
- ✅ **FaaS** = event-driven, billed in **GB-seconds**, max 15-min timeout (Lambda).
- ✅ **S3 Standard durability** = **99.999999999%** (eleven 9's).
- ✅ **Industry SLA** = **99.9%** ≈ 8.76 hours/year downtime.
- ✅ **XaaS** = "Anything as a Service" — modern extension of NIST triad.
- ✅ **DBaaS** = Amazon RDS, DynamoDB, Cosmos DB (managed SQL/NoSQL).
- ✅ **SECaaS** = Shield (DDoS), WAF, IAM, KMS.
- ✅ **STaaS tiers** = S3 Standard (hot) → S3 IA → Glacier (cold) → Glacier Deep Archive (coldest).
- ✅ **Always draw the diagram** in 14-mark answers — it carries 2–3 marks.
- ✅ **Consumer responsibility** = top of stack (App + Data); **Provider responsibility** = bottom (Virtualization + Hardware + Networking).
- ✅ **Multi-tenancy** is a key differentiator of cloud — multiple customers share the same physical infrastructure, isolated logically.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Viva Trick:** If the examiner asks *"Is PaaS always better than IaaS?"* — Answer: **No, it depends on the use case.** PaaS reduces management overhead but may **lock you into a vendor's runtime** (vendor lock-in). IaaS provides portability but requires **skilled DevOps staff**. Always relate your answer to a **real-world scenario**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing and Security - Risks in Cloud Computing, Data Security in Cloud, Cloud Security Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing and Security - Risks in Cloud Computing, Data Security in Cloud, Cloud Security Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Computing Security — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cloud Security (KTU 2024 Definition):** A discipline of cybersecurity dedicated to securing cloud computing systems — including maintaining data privacy, protecting data and applications from unauthorized access, defending infrastructure from internal and external threats, and enforcing regulatory compliance across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS delivery models.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Cloud Security is treated as a **three-pillar unified discipline** that simultaneously addresses:
1. The **Risk Surface** of moving workloads to shared, multi-tenant environments.
2. The **Data Protection Lifecycle** of information stored, processed, and transmitted via the cloud.
3. The **Security Services Portfolio** offered by Cloud Service Providers (CSPs) and consumed by tenants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (OECST722 - Module 3):** This topic carries high weightage in Part B questions (typically 14 marks), often combined with case-study analysis of AWS, Azure, or GCP security breaches and countermeasure design.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Model

Think of cloud security like a **modern bank vault system**:

| Real-World Object | Cloud Computing Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Bank building | Data Center (Region/Availability Zone) |
| Vault door | Encryption (AES-256, TLS 1.3) |
| Safety deposit keys | Cryptographic Keys (KMS, HSM) |
| Security guard | IAM Policies & MFA |
| CCTV cameras | SIEM, IDS/IPS, Audit Logs |
| Alarm system | DDoS Protection, WAF |
| Customer locker | Tenant-isolated Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) |
| Insurance policy | SLA-backed availability (99.99%) |
| Background checks on staff | Insider threat detection, Zero Trust |
| Cash in transit | Data in transit (TLS, VPN) |
| Cash in safe | Data at rest (encrypted storage) |
| Cash being counted | Data in use (confidential computing) |

**Why this analogy works:** Just as a bank does not own your money but is *responsible* for its safety, a CSP does not own your data but is *accountable* for its confidentiality, integrity, and availability — formally known as the **CIA Triad**.

---

## 1.3 The CIA Triad in Cloud Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad** — the foundational security model explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 Cloud Computing syllabus.

- **Confidentiality (C):** Preventing unauthorized disclosure of data. Achieved via encryption, access control lists (ACLs), and data classification.
- **Integrity (I):** Ensuring data is not altered or tampered with in transit or at rest. Achieved via hashing (SHA-256), HMACs, and digital signatures.
- **Availability (A):** Guaranteeing that data and services are accessible when needed. Measured by SLA percentages (e.g., **99.99%** = ~52 minutes downtime/year).

---

## 1.4 Cloud Security Domain Map (KTU 2024 Module 3 Scope)

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              CLOUD COMPUTING SECURITY (Module 3)             │
├──────────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┤
│   Risks in Cloud     │  Data Security   │  Cloud Security    │
│   Computing          │  in Cloud        │  Services          │
├──────────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────┤
│ • Data Breach        │ • Encryption     │ • IAM              │
│ • Data Loss          │ • Key Management │ • SIEM             │
│ • Account Hijack     │ • Tokenization   │ • DDoS Protection  │
│ • Insecure APIs      │ • DLP            │ • WAF              │
│ • DoS / DDoS         │ • Backup/DR      │ • HSM              │
│ • Insider Threat     │ • Data Masking   │ • Compliance       │
│ • Shared Tech Vuln   │ • Homomorphic E. │ • CSPM             │
│ • Compliance Risk    │ • Integrity      │ • CASB             │
└──────────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Shared Responsibility Model:** Always remember — in IaaS the customer secures OS/Middleware/Data/Apps, while CSP secures Physical/Network/Storage. In SaaS, the customer secures only access & data, while the CSP owns the rest. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Risks in Cloud Computing — The "Top 9" Cloud Computing Alliance (CSA) Threat Catalogue

The **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** publishes the industry-standard threat catalogue. For KTU 2024, the following nine risks are explicitly in scope:

### 2.1.1 Risk #1: Data Breaches
- **Definition:** Unauthorized access to sensitive data (PII, PHI, financial records) stored in the cloud.
- **Why it happens:** Misconfigured storage buckets (e.g., AWS S3 left public), weak IAM policies, credential leakage.
- **Impact:** GDPR fines (up to **4% of global turnover**), reputational damage, legal liability.
- **Countermeasure:** AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, least-privilege IAM, data classification.

### 2.1.2 Risk #2: Data Loss
- **Definition:** Permanent destruction of data due to accidental deletion, malicious action, or natural disaster.
- **Why it matters:** In cloud, the customer *may* be the only one with a copy of the encryption key.
- **Countermeasure:** 3-2-1 backup rule (3 copies, 2 media, 1 offsite), geo-replication, KMS-managed key escrow.

### 2.1.3 Risk #3: Account Hijacking
- **Definition:** Attackers steal credentials via phishing, credential stuffing, or session hijacking.
- **Why it matters:** Hijacked accounts can spin up crypto-mining instances, exfiltrate S3 buckets, or launch further attacks.
- **Countermeasure:** MFA, conditional access, session token rotation, anomaly-based UEBA.

### 2.1.4 Risk #4: Insecure APIs
- **Definition:** Cloud services are exposed via REST/GraphQL APIs; weak APIs become attack vectors.
- **Countermeasure:** OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, API gateways, rate limiting, input validation.

### 2.1.5 Risk #5: Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)
- **Definition:** Flooding the cloud service with traffic to make it unavailable.
- **Why cloud is special:** Cloud's elasticity *helps attackers* — they can rent botnet capacity on-demand using stolen credit cards.
- **Countermeasure:** AWS Shield, Azure DDoS Protection, Cloudflare, Anycast routing, scrubbing centers.

### 2.1.6 Risk #6: Malicious Insider
- **Definition:** A privileged employee (CSP staff or tenant admin) abuses access.
- **Why it's hard:** Insider attacks bypass perimeter defenses.
- **Countermeasure:** Background checks, separation of duties, audit logging, just-in-time access (JIT), Zero Trust.

### 2.1.7 Risk #7: Abuse of Cloud Services
- **Definition:** Attackers leverage free-tier cloud resources for crypto-jacking, spam relay, or as C2 servers.
- **Countermeasure:** CSP-side abuse detection, CAPTCHA, account reputation scoring.

### 2.1.8 Risk #8: Insufficient Due Diligence
- **Definition:** Migrating to cloud without understanding the CSP's SLAs, regulatory posture, or exit strategy.
- **Countermeasure:** Vendor risk assessment (SIG, SOC 2 Type II reports), RFI/RFP process.

### 2.1.9 Risk #9: Shared Technology Vulnerabilities
- **Definition:** Vulnerabilities in hypervisors, container runtimes, or shared CPU caches (e.g., Spectre, Meltdown).
- **Countermeasure:** Patch management, confidential computing (Intel SGX, AMD SEV), micro-virtualization.

---

## 2.2 Data Security in Cloud — Layered Protection Model

Data in a cloud environment exists in three states, each requiring distinct security controls:

| State | Description | Primary Controls | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data at Rest** | Stored in databases, object stores, block storage | AES-256 encryption, server-side encryption, transparent data encryption (TDE) | AWS S3 SSE-S3, EBS encryption |
| **Data in Transit** | Moving across networks | TLS 1.3, IPsec VPN, mTLS | TLS termination at ALB |
| **Data in Use** | Being processed in CPU/memory | Confidential computing, homomorphic encryption, secure enclaves | Intel SGX, Azure Confidential VMs |

### 2.2.1 Key Management Hierarchy

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Customer Master Key (CMK) — KMS      │  ← Top-level key, never leaves HSM
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
               │ Wraps (envelope encryption)
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Data Encryption Key (DEK)             │  ← Used to encrypt data
└──────────────┬─────────────────────────┘
               │ Encrypts
               ▼
┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Encrypted Data (Ciphertext)           │  ← Stored in S3 / RDS / EBS
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Envelope Encryption** is the dominant cloud pattern. It solves the key-rotation problem: you rotate the CMK without re-encrypting petabytes of data — you only re-wrap the small DEKs.

### 2.2.2 Tokenization vs Encryption

| Property | Encryption | Tokenization |
|---|---|---|
| Reversibility | Reversible with key | Irreversible (lookup table) |
| Format preservation | No (changes length) | Yes |
| Use case | Files, databases | Credit card PAN, PII |
| Performance | Slower | Faster (random lookup) |
| Cloud example | AWS KMS | AWS CloudHSM tokenization |

---

## 2.3 Cloud Security Services — The Provider's Defense Stack

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern CSPs offer **40+ managed security services**. KTU 2024 Module 3 groups them into **6 functional categories**:

### 2.3.1 Identity & Access Management (IAM)
- **Function:** Authentication + Authorization.
- **Mechanisms:** RBAC, ABAC, MFA, SSO (SAML 2.0), federation.
- **Cloud Examples:** AWS IAM, Azure AD (Entra ID), Google Cloud IAM.

### 2.3.2 Network Security
- **Components:** Security Groups (stateful firewall), NACLs (stateless), WAF, VPC, Transit Gateway.
- **Cloud Examples:** AWS WAF, Azure Firewall, GCP VPC Service Controls.

### 2.3.3 Data Protection Services
- **Components:** KMS, HSM, Secrets Manager, Certificate Manager, Macie (data discovery).
- **Cloud Examples:** AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, Google Cloud KMS.

### 2.3.4 Detection & Response
- **Components:** IDS/IPS, SIEM, EDR, SOAR, threat intelligence feeds.
- **Cloud Examples:** AWS GuardDuty, Azure Sentinel, Google Chronicle.

### 2.3.5 Application Security
- **Components:** WAF, API Gateway, Bot Management, runtime application self-protection (RASP).
- **Cloud Examples:** AWS WAF + Shield, Cloudflare, Azure App Gateway WAF.

### 2.3.6 Compliance & Governance
- **Components:** CSPM (Cloud Security Posture Management), CIEM (Cloud Infrastructure Entitlement Management), audit logs.
- **Cloud Examples:** AWS Config, Azure Policy, GCP Security Command Center.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are repeatedly asked in KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions. **Memorize the boundary values in bold.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Typical Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Service Availability** | $A = \dfrac{U}{U + D} \times 100$ | **99.9% = 8.77 hr/yr**, **99.99% = 52.6 min/yr**, **99.999% = 5.26 min/yr** | % |
| 2 | **Downtime per Year** | $D_{year} = (1 - A) \times 525{,}600$ | 525,600 = total minutes/yr | minutes |
| 3 | **Mean Time Between Failures** | $MTBF = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Industry: 1000–10000 hrs | hours |
| 4 | **Mean Time To Recovery** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum T_{repair}}{N_{failures}}$ | Industry: < 1 hr for tier-1 | hours |
| 5 | **System Reliability** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{MTBF}$ | For $\lambda t \ll 1$: $R \approx 1 - \lambda t$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Risk Score** | $R = P \times I$ | P = Probability (0–1), I = Impact (0–10) | score 0–10 |
| 7 | **Annualized Loss Expectancy** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | SLE = Single Loss Expectancy, ARO = Annual Rate of Occurrence | $ /year |
| 8 | **SLE** | $SLE = Asset\_Value \times Exposure\_Factor$ | EF = % loss if threat realized | $ |
| 9 | **HMAC Verification** | $HMAC = H((K \oplus opad) \parallel H((K \oplus ipad) \parallel M))$ | Used in JWT, AWS SigV4 | bits |
| 10 | **AES Effective Key Strength** | $2^{k} = 2^{k}$ operations for brute force | AES-128 = **3.4×10³⁸**, AES-256 = **1.16×10⁷⁷** keys | operations |
| 11 | **Modular Exponentiation (RSA)** | $C = M^{e} \mod n$, $M = C^{d} \mod n$ | RSA-2048 = 112-bit security | – |
| 12 | **Diffie–Hellman Shared Secret** | $K = (g^{b} \mod p)^{a} \mod p = (g^{a} \mod p)^{b} \mod p$ | p ≥ 2048 bits | – |
| 13 | **Storage Overhead (Replication)** | $Overhead = (n-1) \times 100$% where n = copies | 3-copy RAID-1 = 200% overhead | % |
| 14 | **Erasure Coding Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{k}{k + m}$ where k=data, m=parity | Reed–Solomon (10,4) = 71.4% | ratio |
| 15 | **Bandwidth–Delay Product** | $BDP = Bandwidth \times RTT$ | Determines TCP window size | bits |

---

## 2.5 The Shared Responsibility Model — KTU Favourite

| Layer | On-Premises | IaaS (e.g., EC2) | PaaS (e.g., RDS) | SaaS (e.g., Gmail) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Application & Data** | Customer | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| **Runtime / Middleware** | Customer | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| **Operating System** | Customer | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| **Virtualization** | Customer | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Servers & Storage** | Customer | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Networking** | Customer | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Physical Data Center** | Customer | CSP | CSP | CSP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Candidates frequently confuse *who* is responsible for *what*. Memorize: **"AWS secures the cloud; the customer secures what's in the cloud."** This single phrase is worth 2–3 marks in Part A.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Cloud Security Concepts |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare (HIPAA)** | PHI must be encrypted at rest + in transit; BAA required with CSP |
| **Finance (PCI-DSS)** | Tokenization of card numbers; network segmentation via VPCs |
| **Government (FedRAMP)** | Only GovCloud regions; FIPS 140-2 validated HSMs |
| **E-Commerce** | WAF + bot management during sale events (e.g., Big Billion Day) |
| **IoT** | Per-device X.509 certificates; device-to-cloud mTLS |
| **AI/ML** | Confidential computing for training on encrypted medical data |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation #1: SLA Availability to Annual Downtime

> **Why we need this:** KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"If a CSP offers 99.95% availability SLA, what is the maximum allowable downtime per month?"*

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define total minutes in a year:**

$$
T_{year} = 365 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ hours} \times 60 \text{ min} = 525{,}600 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Express availability as a fraction of uptime:**

$$
A = \frac{U}{T_{year}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = A \times T_{year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute downtime (the complement):**

$$
D_{year} = T_{year} - U = T_{year} - (A \times T_{year}) = T_{year} \times (1 - A)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute A = 0.9995:**

$$
D_{year} = 525{,}600 \times (1 - 0.9995) = 525{,}600 \times 0.0005
$$

$$
D_{year} = 262.8 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per-month allowance (divide by 12):**

$$
D_{month} = \frac{262.8}{12} = 21.9 \text{ minutes/month}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A 99.95% SLA allows only **~22 minutes of downtime per month**. If a tenant's measured downtime exceeds this, they are legally entitled to SLA credits (typically 10–30% of the monthly bill).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation #2: Risk Score Calculation (Quantitative Risk Assessment)

> **Why we need this:** The KTU 2024 Part B (14 marks) frequently asks you to compute risk for a given scenario using the formula **R = P × I** and then recommend controls.

### Worked Example:

A company stores **2 million customer credit card numbers** (asset value = ₹50 crore) on AWS S3. Threat: Misconfigured bucket leads to public exposure.
- **Probability (P)** of misconfiguration = 0.3 (30% of orgs misconfigure at least once)
- **Impact (I)** on a scale 0–10 = 9 (regulatory + reputational damage)
- **Exposure Factor (EF)** if threat realized = 0.6 (60% of records exposed)

**Step 1 — Compute Single Loss Expectancy:**

$$
SLE = Asset\_Value \times EF = 50{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6 = 30{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Estimate Annual Rate of Occurrence:**
Suppose the company has had 1 misconfiguration incident in the last 2 years:

$$
ARO = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ incidents/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Annualized Loss Expectancy:**

$$
ALE = SLE \times ARO = 30{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 15{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Risk Score:**

$$
R = P \times I = 0.3 \times 9 = 2.7 \text{ (Medium-High)}
$$

> **Recommendation (worth 3 marks in KTU):** Implement AWS Config rules, S3 Block Public Access, Macie for PII discovery, and enable KMS with automatic key rotation. This reduces P to < 0.05 and I to < 4.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation #3: HMAC (Hashed Message Authentication Code)

> **Why we need this:** HMAC is the cornerstone of cloud API authentication (AWS SigV4, Azure SAS tokens, Google OAuth JWTs). KTU 2024 often asks: *"Explain how AWS Signature Version 4 ensures integrity and authenticity."*

### Mathematical Definition:

$$
HMAC_{K}(M) = H\left( (K \oplus opad) \parallel H\left( (K \oplus ipad) \parallel M \right) \right)
$$

Where:
- $H$ = cryptographic hash (SHA-256 in AWS SigV4)
- $K$ = secret key (padded to block size if shorter)
- $M$ = message
- $ipad = 0x36$ repeated to block size (inner pad)
- $opad = 0x5C$ repeated to block size (outer pad)
- $\oplus$ = XOR
- $\parallel$ = concatenation

### Step-by-Step Trace (SHA-256, 64-byte block):

**Step 1 — Key normalization:**
If $K$ is shorter than 64 bytes, pad with zeros to 64 bytes.
If longer, hash it first: $K' = H(K)$, then pad.

**Step 2 — Compute inner key:**

$$
K_{inner} = K' \oplus 0x36\ldots36 \text{ (64 bytes)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute inner hash:**

$$
H_{inner} = SHA\text{-}256(K_{inner} \parallel M)
$$

**Step 4 — Compute outer key:**

$$
K_{outer} = K' \oplus 0x5C\ldots5C \text{ (64 bytes)}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute final HMAC:**

$$
HMAC = SHA\text{-}256(K_{outer} \parallel H_{inner})
$$

Output = 256-bit (32-byte) digest.

> **Engineering utility:** Any modification to $M$ produces a different HMAC, ensuring **integrity**. Only a holder of $K$ can produce a valid HMAC, ensuring **authenticity**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation #4: AES-256 Brute-Force Resistance

> **Why we need this:** KTU 2024 occasionally asks: *"Why is AES-256 considered quantum-resistant compared to RSA-2048?"*

**Step 1 — Total number of possible keys:**

$$
N_{keys} = 2^{256} \approx 1.1579 \times 10^{77}
$$

**Step 2 — World's fastest supercomputer (Frontier) speed:**
Assume $10^{18}$ operations/second (1 ExaFLOP/s).

**Step 3 — Time to brute-force:**

$$
T = \frac{2^{256}}{10^{18} \times 3.154 \times 10^{7}} = \frac{1.158 \times 10^{77}}{3.154 \times 10^{25}} \approx 3.67 \times 10^{51} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to years:**

$$
T_{years} = \frac{3.67 \times 10^{51}}{3.154 \times 10^{7}} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{44} \text{ years}
$$

> **Interpretation:** AES-256 brute force would take **~10⁴⁴ years** — vastly longer than the age of the universe (1.38 × 10¹⁰ years). Compare with RSA-2048, which Shor's algorithm could break in polynomial time on a quantum computer with ~4096 logical qubits.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Derivation #5: Erasure Coding Storage Efficiency

> **Why we need this:** KTU 2024 Module 3 case study: *"Compare 3-way replication vs. Reed–Solomon (10, 4) erasure coding for 100 TB of data."*

**Storage overhead for 3-way replication:**

$$
Storage_{replicated} = 3 \times 100 \text{ TB} = 300 \text{ TB} \quad (200\% \text{ overhead})
$$

**Storage for Reed–Solomon (10, 4):**
This means k = 10 data shards + m = 4 parity shards = 14 shards total. Can tolerate any 4 shard losses.

$$
Storage_{EC} = \frac{14}{10} \times 100 \text{ TB} = 140 \text{ TB} \quad (40\% \text{ overhead})
$$

**Efficiency ratio:**

$$
E = \frac{k}{k+m} = \frac{10}{14} = 71.4\%
$$

> **Trade-off:** EC uses 53% less storage, but reconstruction is CPU-intensive. Used in cold storage tiers (AWS Glacier, Azure Archive).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Threat-to-Control Mapping (KTU 2024 High-Yield Table)

| Threat | Likelihood | Impact | Risk Score (P×I) | Primary Control | Secondary Control | CSP Service |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data Breach (S3 misconfig) | High (0.7) | Critical (10) | **7.0** | S3 Block Public Access | KMS + Macie | AWS Macie |
| DDoS Attack | Medium (0.4) | High (8) | **3.2** | AWS Shield Advanced | Cloudflare | Route 53 |
| Account Hijack | Medium (0.3) | Critical (10) | **3.0** | MFA + IAM Identity Center | GuardDuty | AWS IAM |
| Insider Threat | Low (0.2) | High (8) | **1.6** | Separation of Duties | CloudTrail Lake | AWS IAM Access Analyzer |
| Insecure API | Medium (0.5) | High (7) | **3.5** | API Gateway + WAF | OAuth 2.0 + mTLS | AWS WAF |
| Key Compromise | Low (0.1) | Critical (10) | **1.0** | HSM + auto-rotation | KMS grants | AWS CloudHSM |
| VM Escape | Very Low (0.05) | Critical (10) | **0.5** | Patch management | Confidential VMs | Intel SGX |
| Data Loss (no backup) | Medium (0.4) | Critical (9) | **3.6** | 3-2-1 backup | Cross-region replication | AWS Backup |

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Rule:** Risk Score > 5.0 → **Immediate remediation required**. 2.5–5.0 → **Plan & mitigate within 90 days**. < 2.5 → **Accept & monitor**.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example: Availability Cascade for 3-Tier Architecture

> **KTU 2024 Typical 14-mark Question Type**

A web application has 3 tiers with the following SLA targets:
- Web servers: 99.95%
- Application servers: 99.9%
- Database: 99.99%

**Step 1 — Compute the series availability (system fails if any tier fails):**

$$
A_{system} = A_1 \times A_2 \times A_3
$$

$$
A_{system} = 0.9995 \times 0.9990 \times 0.9999
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply stepwise:**

$$
A_{system} = 0.9995 \times 0.9999 = 0.99940\ldots
$$

$$
A_{system} = 0.99940\ldots \times 0.9990 = 0.99840\ldots
$$

$$
A_{system} \approx 0.9984 = 99.84\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute annual downtime:**

$$
D_{year} = (1 - 0.9984) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0016 \times 525{,}600 \approx 841 \text{ minutes/year}
$$

$$
D_{year} \approx 14 \text{ hours/year}
$$

> **Engineering insight:** A 3-tier architecture in **series** has *worse* availability than its weakest component. To improve, you add **redundancy in parallel** (multi-AZ deployment) so that availability is computed as:

$$
A_{parallel} = 1 - (1 - A_1) \times (1 - A_2)
$$

For two redundant web servers each at 99.95%:

$$
A_{parallel} = 1 - (0.0005)^2 = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 99.999975\%
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Cloud Security Reference Architecture (Defense-in-Depth)

```mermaid
graph TB
    user1[End User / Mobile App] -->|HTTPS TLS 1.3| cdn1[CloudFront CDN]
    cdn1 -->|WAF Rules| waf1[AWS WAF]
    waf1 -->|Rate Limit| apigw1[API Gateway]
    apigw1 -->|OAuth 2.0 JWT| auth1[Cognito IAM]
    apigw1 -->|mTLS| alb1[Application Load Balancer]
    alb1 --> sg1[Security Group Layer 7]
    sg1 --> ec2a[EC2 Web Tier AZ1]
    sg1 --> ec2b[EC2 Web Tier AZ2]
    ec2a --> sg2[Security Group Layer 4]
    ec2b --> sg2
    sg2 --> ec2c[EC2 App Tier]
    ec2c --> kms1[KMS Encryption]
    ec2c --> rds1[RDS Encrypted Multi-AZ]
    ec2c --> s3a[S3 Bucket SSE-KMS]
    ec2c --> secret1[Secrets Manager]
    kms1 --> hsm1[CloudHSM FIPS 140-2]
    ec2a --> cw1[CloudWatch Logs]
    ec2b --> cw1
    ec2c --> cw1
    cw1 --> guard1[GuardDuty Threat Detection]
    cw1 --> ct1[CloudTrail Audit]
    cw1 --> siem1[SIEM Sentinel]
    guard1 --> sns1[SNS Alert]
    siem1 --> sns1
    sns1 --> soa1[SOAR Auto-Remediation]
```

> **Reading guide:** Data flows top-to-bottom through 7 defense layers: Edge → WAF → Auth → Network → Compute → Data Encryption → Monitoring. An attacker must defeat *all* layers to reach the data.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Risk Assessment & Treatment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start1[Step A Identify Assets] --> step1b[Step B Identify Threats]
    step1b --> step1c[Step C Estimate P and I]
    step1c --> step1d[Step D Compute Risk R = P times I]
    step1d --> step1e{Risk Score}
    step1e -->|R greater than 5| step1f[Step E Mitigate Immediately]
    step1e -->|R between 2.5 and 5| step1g[Step F Plan Controls 90 days]
    step1e -->|R less than 2.5| step1h[Step G Accept and Monitor]
    step1f --> step1i[Step H Residual Risk Reassess]
    step1g --> step1i
    step1h --> step1i
    step1i --> step1j[Step I Document and Audit]
```

> **KTU mapping:** This is the NIST SP 800-30 risk management framework — the most commonly cited model in KTU case-study questions.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Data Security Lifecycle (3 States × 6 Controls)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph State1[Data at Rest]
        dar1[AES-256 Server Side Encryption]
        dar2[Transparent Data Encryption TDE]
        dar3[Object Lock S3 WORM]
    end
    subgraph State2[Data in Transit]
        dit1[TLS 1.3 Mutual]
        dit2[IPsec VPN Tunnel]
        dit3[Application Layer mTLS]
    end
    subgraph State3[Data in Use]
        diu1[Confidential Computing Enclave]
        diu2[Homomorphic Encryption]
        diu3[Secure Multi Party Computation]
    end
    subgraph Controls1[Identity and Access]
        ctl1[IAM RBAC ABAC]
        ctl2[Multi Factor Authentication]
        ctl3[Just In Time JIT Access]
    end
    subgraph Controls2[Key Management]
        ctl4[Envelope Encryption CMK DEK]
        ctl5[HSM FIPS 140-2 Level 3]
        ctl6[Automatic Key Rotation]
    end
    subgraph Controls3[Monitoring]
        ctl7[Data Access Audit Logs]
        ctl8[Anomaly Detection UEBA]
        ctl9[Data Loss Prevention DLP]
    end
    State1 --> Controls1
    State2 --> Controls1
    State3 --> Controls1
    State1 --> Controls2
    State2 --> Controls2
    State3 --> Controls2
    State1 --> Controls3
    State2 --> Controls3
    State3 --> Controls3
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Shared Responsibility Model (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cust1[CUSTOMER Responsibility]
        c1[Data]
        c2[Applications]
        c3[OS Patching IaaS Only]
        c4[Identity and Access IAM Policies]
        c5[Network Firewall Security Groups]
    end
    subgraph CSP1[CSP Responsibility]
        s1[Physical Data Center]
        s2[Server Hardware]
        s3[Storage Devices]
        s4[Network Infrastructure]
        s5[Hypervisor]
        s6[Region and AZ Uptime]
    end
    subgraph Shared1[SHARED Responsibility]
        sh1[Encryption Configuration]
        sh2[Patch Management Coordination]
        sh3[Identity Federation]
    end
    c1 -.- Shared1
    s4 -.- Shared1
    s5 -.- Shared1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The boundary between customer and CSP shifts depending on the service model (IaaS → PaaS → SaaS). Higher-level services shift *more* responsibility to the CSP, leaving the customer responsible primarily for **data and access**.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5: Cloud Security Services Stack (Layered Menu)

```mermaid
graph TB
    top1[Application Security Layer] --> mid1[Identity and Access IAM]
    top1 --> mid2[Data Protection KMS HSM]
    top1 --> mid3[Network Security WAF NACL]
    top1 --> mid4[Detection and Response SIEM SOAR]
    top1 --> mid5[Compliance and Governance CSPM]
    top1 --> mid6[Threat Intelligence GuardDuty]
    mid1 --> base1[Underlying Compute Storage Network]
    mid2 --> base1
    mid3 --> base1
    mid4 --> base1
    mid5 --> base1
    mid6 --> base1
```

---

## 4.6 Diagram 6: Incident Response Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Detect as Detection
    participant Triage as Triage
    participant Contain as Containment
    participant Eradicate as Eradication
    participant Recover as Recovery
    participant Learn as Post Incident
    Detect->>Triage: GuardDuty alert fired
    Triage->>Contain: Severity High
    Contain->>Eradicate: Isolate EC2 instance
    Eradicate->>Recover: Patch and restore from snapshot
    Recover->>Learn: Generate RCA report
    Learn-->>Detect: Update detection rules
```

> **Engineering utility:** This 6-step cycle is **favourite KTU Part B content** — questions often ask you to map a real breach (e.g., Capital One 2019, SolarWinds 2020) onto this cycle.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: List and briefly explain any three major risks associated with cloud computing as identified by the Cloud Security Alliance (CSA).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per risk):**

1. **Data Breaches (1 mark):** Unauthorized access to sensitive information such as PII or financial records. Example: The 2017 Verizon AWS S3 misconfiguration exposed 14 million customer records.

2. **Account Hijacking (1 mark):** Stolen credentials used to manipulate data, redirect clients, or fabricate invoices. Mitigated by MFA, conditional access, and short-lived session tokens.

3. **Insecure APIs (1 mark):** Cloud services expose RESTful APIs; weak authentication or missing rate limiting makes them attack vectors. Mitigated by OAuth 2.0, API gateways, and WAF rules.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing "hacking" or "viruses" as risks — these are too generic. KTU examiners award marks only for **CSA-categorized risks** (the 9 listed in Section 2.1).

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between the responsibilities of a cloud customer and a cloud service provider in the IaaS model. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Layer | Responsible Party | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Physical security, network, hypervisor (1.5 marks) | **CSP** | Securing data center, host OS hardening |
| OS patching, application, data, IAM policies (1.5 marks) | **Customer** | Patching the guest OS on EC2, encrypting EBS volumes with KMS |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often say *"AWS is responsible for everything"* — this is **wrong**. Even in IaaS, the customer is responsible for the guest OS and above. This is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction if missed.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### `Question A — 14 Marks` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, adapted]`

**Q: (a) Discuss in detail the nine major risks of cloud computing as enumerated by the Cloud Security Alliance. For each risk, suggest at least one mitigation control. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)** identifies nine critical risks:

1. **Data Breaches:** Unauthorized access to confidential data. *Mitigation:* AES-256 encryption at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit, principle of least privilege IAM.

2. **Data Loss:** Accidental deletion, hardware failure, or natural disaster. *Mitigation:* 3-2-1 backup rule, cross-region replication, immutable storage (S3 Object Lock).

3. **Account Hijacking:** Stolen credentials via phishing or credential stuffing. *Mitigation:* Mandatory MFA, conditional access policies, anomaly detection (GuardDuty), session token rotation.

4. **Insecure APIs:** Weak authentication on REST/GraphQL endpoints. *Mitigation:* OAuth 2.0 + OpenID Connect, mTLS, API Gateway with rate limiting and WAF.

5. **Denial of Service:** Volumetric or application-layer attacks. *Mitigation:* AWS Shield Advanced, Cloudflare, Anycast routing, auto-scaling, scrubbing centers.

6. **Malicious Insider:** Privileged user misuses access. *Mitigation:* Background checks, separation of duties, just-in-time access, immutable CloudTrail logs.

7. **Abuse of Cloud Services:** Free-tier resources used for crypto-jacking or C2. *Mitigation:* CSP-side abuse detection, CAPTCHA, account reputation scoring.

8. **Insufficient Due Diligence:** Migration without understanding CSP SLAs, exit strategy, or compliance posture. *Mitigation:* Vendor risk assessment, SOC 2 Type II review, exit planning.

9. **Shared Technology Vulnerabilities:** Hypervisor, container, or CPU-level flaws (Spectre, Meltdown). *Mitigation:* Patch management, confidential computing, micro-virtualization.

> (1 mark for the explanation + 1 mark for the mitigation; balanced scoring: ~0.7 mark per risk + 0.7 mark per mitigation)

---

**Q: (b) Explain the various states in which data exists in a cloud environment. Discuss the security controls applicable to each state with real-world cloud service examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Data in a cloud environment exists in **three states**:

1. **Data at Rest (2.5 marks):**
   - **Definition:** Data stored on persistent media (databases, object stores, block storage).
   - **Controls:** AES-256 server-side encryption, transparent data encryption (TDE), AWS KMS-managed keys.
   - **Example:** AWS S3 with SSE-KMS, EBS volumes with default encryption, Azure Blob Storage with customer-managed keys.

2. **Data in Transit (2.5 marks):**
   - **Definition:** Data moving across networks (public internet, inter-AZ, inter-region).
   - **Controls:** TLS 1.3, IPsec VPN, mTLS for service-to-service communication.
   - **Example:** HTTPS endpoints on ALB, AWS Direct Connect with MACsec, Azure VPN Gateway.

3. **Data in Use (2 marks):**
   - **Definition:** Data being processed in CPU registers, cache, or RAM.
   - **Controls:** Confidential computing (Intel SGX, AMD SEV), homomorphic encryption, secure multi-party computation.
   - **Example:** Azure Confidential VMs, AWS Nitro Enclaves, IBM HElayers for homomorphic encryption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bonus point (extra credit):** Mention that **envelope encryption** is the gold standard — a Customer Master Key (CMK) wraps per-object Data Encryption Keys (DEKs), enabling rotation without re-encrypting data.

---

### `Question B — 14 Marks` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, adapted]`

**Q: (a) What is the Shared Responsibility Model in cloud computing? With a neat diagram, explain how responsibilities are divided between the CSP and the customer across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS service models. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Shared Responsibility Model** is a security framework that delineates which security tasks are handled by the **Cloud Service Provider (CSP)** versus the **customer** (2 marks).

**Division across service models (5 marks):**

| Layer | IaaS (e.g., EC2) | PaaS (e.g., RDS) | SaaS (e.g., Gmail) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Application & Data** | Customer | Customer | Customer |
| **Runtime / Middleware** | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| **Operating System** | Customer | CSP | CSP |
| **Virtualization** | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Servers, Storage, Network** | CSP | CSP | CSP |
| **Physical Data Center** | CSP | CSP | CSP |

> **As you move from IaaS → PaaS → SaaS, the customer is relieved of more operational burden but remains responsible for data and access.**

**Example:** In **IaaS**, the customer must patch the guest OS, but in **SaaS**, the CSP handles the entire stack including patches.

---

**Q: (b) Explain the following cloud security services in detail: (i) Identity and Access Management (IAM), (ii) Key Management Service (KMS), (iii) Security Information and Event Management (SIEM). (7 marks — 2.5 + 2.5 + 2)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Identity and Access Management (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Framework for authenticating users and authorizing their access to cloud resources.
- **Components:** Users, Groups, Roles, Policies (JSON-formatted), MFA, SSO via SAML 2.0 / OIDC.
- **Mechanisms:** RBAC (Role-Based), ABAC (Attribute-Based), PBAC (Policy-Based), least-privilege principle.
- **Examples:** AWS IAM, Azure Entra ID, Google Cloud IAM.
- **Feature:** Identity federation with on-premises AD via SAML or OIDC.

**(ii) Key Management Service (2.5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Managed service for creating, storing, rotating, and auditing cryptographic keys.
- **Key concepts:** CMK (Customer Master Key), DEK (Data Encryption Key), envelope encryption.
- **FIPS 140-2 validation:** Keys stored in HSMs (Hardware Security Modules).
- **Examples:** AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault, Google Cloud KMS, AWS CloudHSM.
- **Feature:** Automatic annual rotation; access logged in CloudTrail.

**(iii) Security Information and Event Management (2 marks):**
- **Definition:** Centralized platform for collecting, correlating, and analyzing security logs and events across cloud and on-premises systems.
- **Pipeline:** Log ingestion → Normalization → Correlation → Alerting → Investigation.
- **Examples:** Azure Sentinel, AWS Detective + GuardDuty + CloudTrail Lake, Google Chronicle, Splunk Cloud.
- **Use case:** Detect lateral movement, privilege escalation, and data exfiltration in real-time.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks in Module 3 (Cloud Security):**
> 1. **Confusing confidentiality with integrity** — they are *different* goals with *different* controls. Confidentiality = encryption; Integrity = hashing/HMAC.
> 2. **Writing "cloud is not secure"** as a generic answer — examiners want *specific, named* risks and *specific* controls.
> 3. **Skipping the Shared Responsibility diagram** — the diagram is worth 1.5 marks on its own.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention "in which state"** when discussing data security — the 3-state model (rest, transit, use) is a *mandatory* framing.
> 5. **Memorizing acronyms without definitions** — RBAC, ABAC, KMS, HSM, SIEM, SOAR, CSPM, CIEM, UEBA, DLP all must be expanded and explained.

---

## 5.4 Numerical Practice Question (Bonus — 7 marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Sample]`

**Q: A SaaS provider advertises 99.95% monthly availability SLA. Calculate:**
**(a)** Maximum allowable downtime per month in minutes.
**(b)** Annual downtime budget.
**(c)** If a customer suffers 35 minutes of downtime in a particular month, what percentage of SLA credit should the CSP offer (typical credit: 10% for breaches < 50%, 30% for breaches ≥ 50%)?

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Total minutes in a month (assume 30 days):

$$
T_{month} = 30 \times 24 \times 60 = 43{,}200 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
D_{month} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 43{,}200 = 0.0005 \times 43{,}200 = 21.6 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(b)** Annual downtime:

$$
D_{year} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours}
$$

**(c)** Breach percentage:

$$
Breach = \frac{35}{21.6} = 1.62 = 162\% \text{ of allowed downtime}
$$

Since 162% ≥ 50%, the customer is entitled to a **30% SLA credit** on that month's bill.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Cloud Security)**

- ✅ The **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ✅ **CSA's 9 Risks:** Data Breach, Data Loss, Account Hijack, Insecure APIs, DoS, Insider, Abuse, Due Diligence, Shared Tech.
- ✅ **3 States of Data:** At Rest, In Transit, In Use — each requires *different* controls.
- ✅ **Shared Responsibility:** CSP secures *the cloud*; customer secures *what's in the cloud*.
- ✅ **Service Models:** IaaS (EC2), PaaS (RDS), SaaS (Gmail) — responsibility shifts leftward toward CSP as you move up.
- ✅ **Envelope Encryption:** CMK (master) wraps DEKs (data keys) — enables cheap key rotation.
- ✅ **AES-256 = 1.16 × 10⁷⁷ keys** — practically unbreakable by brute force.
- ✅ **SLA 99.99% = 52.6 min/yr downtime; 99.95% = 4.38 hr/yr.**
- ✅ **Risk Score = Probability × Impact;** if > 5, immediate mitigation.
- ✅ **ALE = SLE × ARO** — quantitative risk assessment.
- ✅ **Key Security Services:** IAM, KMS, HSM, WAF, SIEM, SOAR, CSPM, DLP, CASB.
- ✅ **Confidential Computing:** protects data in use via enclaves (Intel SGX, AMD SEV, AWS Nitro).
- ✅ **HMAC = H((K ⊕ opad) ∥ H((K ⊕ ipad) ∥ M))** — used in AWS SigV4.
- ✅ **3-2-1 Backup Rule:** 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite (e.g., S3 cross-region replication).
- ✅ **Erasure Coding (10,4) = 71.4% efficiency** vs. 3-way replication (33% efficiency).
- ✅ **Top KTU case studies:** Capital One 2019 S3 breach, SolarWinds 2020 supply chain, Uber 2016 S3 leak.
- ✅ **Always quote the formula and the boundary value** — examiners love seeing "99.99% = 52.6 min/yr" as an anchor.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When answering any cloud security question, structure your answer in **3 layers**:
> 1. **Identify the risk** (name the threat category from CSA's 9).
> 2. **Quantify the impact** (use R = P × I or ALE).
> 3. **Recommend layered controls** (people, process, technology — and map them to a CSP service).
>
> This **Risk → Quantify → Mitigate** framework is the gold standard for full marks in KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Computing Tools - Tools and Technologies for Cloud, Apache Hadoop, Cloud Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Computing Tools - Tools and Technologies for Cloud, Apache Hadoop, Cloud Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Cloud Computing Tools

**Cloud Computing Tools** are a specialized, integrated collection of software frameworks, platforms, utilities, and services that enable developers, system administrators, and end-users to build, deploy, monitor, manage, and scale applications and infrastructure over cloud environments. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST722 – Cloud Computing, Module 4), cloud tools span three primary categories:

1. **Deployment & Infrastructure Tools** – for provisioning virtual machines, containers, storage, and networks (e.g., AWS CloudFormation, Terraform, Azure Resource Manager).
2. **Development & Programming Frameworks** – for building distributed, data-intensive, and scalable applications (e.g., **Apache Hadoop**, Apache Spark, Kubernetes, Docker).
3. **Management & Monitoring Tools** – for orchestration, performance tracking, and observability (e.g., Prometheus, Grafana, Ansible).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (Module 4):** Apache Hadoop is treated as the *flagship reference tool* for understanding how distributed data processing is built on commodity cloud hardware. Every other modern tool is benchmarked against the Hadoop paradigm.

## 1.2 What is Apache Hadoop?

**Apache Hadoop** is an open-source, Java-based, distributed storage and processing framework designed to store and analyze *very large datasets* (terabytes to petabytes) across clusters of commodity computers. It is maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation (ASF)** and follows a "bring the code to the data" philosophy rather than moving data to a central server.

The Hadoop framework consists of **four core modules**:

| Module | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hadoop Common** | Core utilities and libraries supporting other Hadoop modules |
| **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) | Distributed, fault-tolerant storage layer |
| **MapReduce** | Distributed parallel data-processing programming model |
| **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) | Cluster resource management and job scheduling |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Library Workshop"

Imagine a librarian who manages **10 million books** in a single city library. Instead of one giant reading room, the librarian distributes the books across **100 branch libraries** (think of these as *DataNodes* in HDFS). To prevent loss, every book is photocopied and kept in **3 different branches** (the **replication factor = 3**). When a researcher asks for a count of books containing a specific keyword:

- **Mapper** workers (one per branch) scan *their local books* in parallel and produce a partial keyword count.
- The **Shuffle & Sort** phase aggregates all partial counts.
- The **Reducer** workers finalize the global count.

> This is exactly how **HDFS stores data** and how **MapReduce processes it** — locally, in parallel, with redundancy.

## 1.4 High-Level Tools & Technologies for Cloud

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick reference callout for KTU short-answer questions:**

- **IaaS Tools:** OpenStack, AWS EC2, Google Compute Engine
- **PaaS Tools:** Google App Engine, Heroku, AWS Elastic Beanstalk
- **SaaS Delivery Tools:** Salesforce, Office 365, Google Workspace
- **Big Data / Distributed Processing:** Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark, Apache Flink
- **Container & Orchestration:** Docker, Kubernetes
- **Configuration & IaC:** Ansible, Terraform, Puppet, Chef
- **Monitoring:** Prometheus, Grafana, Nagios, CloudWatch
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hadoop Architecture — Layered View

Hadoop's architecture is decomposed into **two master/slave layers** plus a resource layer:

### Layer A — HDFS (Storage Layer)
- **NameNode (Master):** Maintains the **metadata** (file → block mapping), namespace, and regulates file access.
- **DataNode (Slave):** Stores the actual data **blocks** (default block size = **128 MB** in Hadoop 2.x/3.x) and serves read/write requests.
- **Secondary NameNode:** Periodically merges the *fsimage* with the *edit log* to prevent metadata-log corruption. (Note: in Hadoop HA setups, the *Standby NameNode* replaces this role.)

### Layer B — YARN (Resource Management Layer)
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Global scheduler for the entire cluster.
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent managing containers, CPU, RAM.
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application negotiator that requests resources from RM and works with NMs.
- **Container:** An abstracted unit of CPU + RAM + disk allocated to a task.

### Layer C — MapReduce (Processing Layer)
- **JobTracker / ApplicationMaster** dispatches `map` and `reduce` tasks to cluster nodes.
- **TaskTracker / NodeManager** executes assigned tasks.

## 2.2 MapReduce Programming Model — Step-by-Step

The MapReduce paradigm, originally proposed by **Jeffrey Dean and Sanjay Ghemawat at Google (2004)**, decomposes any parallel data job into two user-defined functions:

1. **Map Function:** $\quad (k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list of } (k_2, v_2)$
2. **Reduce Function:** $\quad (k_2, \text{list of } v_2) \rightarrow \text{list of } (k_3, v_3)$

**Execution Pipeline:**

1. **Input Split** — Input file is split into fixed-size chunks (typically equal to HDFS block size, **128 MB**).
2. **Map Phase** — Each split is processed by a Mapper that emits intermediate $\langle key, value \rangle$ pairs.
3. **Combine Phase (Optional)** — Local mini-reducer at the mapper node to pre-aggregate data and reduce shuffle traffic.
4. **Shuffle & Sort Phase** — Framework groups all values belonging to the same key and routes them to a single Reducer.
5. **Reduce Phase** — Final aggregation/transformation per key.
6. **Output** — Final $\langle key, value \rangle$ pairs written back to HDFS.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — these are the most-tested numerical derivations for Hadoop in KTU Module-4 questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
