| 1 | Total HDFS Storage Required | $S_{\text{total}} = D \times R$ | $D$ = raw data size (MB / GB), $R$ = replication factor (default **3**) |
| 2 | Number of Blocks | $B = \left\lceil \dfrac{D}{B_s} \right\rceil \times R$ | $B_s$ = HDFS block size (default **128 MB** or **256 MB** in Hadoop 3.x) |
| 3 | Number of Mappers | $M = \left\lceil \dfrac{D}{B_s} \right\rceil$ | One mapper per input split by default |
| 4 | Number of Reducers | $N_R$ = configured value (often computed as $N_R \approx 0.95 \times N \times R_{\text{containers}}$) | $N$ = number of nodes, $R_{\text{containers}}$ = max containers/node |
| 5 | MapReduce Total Time | $T_{\text{MR}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}$ | All in seconds |
| 6 | Shuffle Volume (Bytes) | $V_{\text{shuffle}} = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \text{size}(\text{intermediate}_i)$ | Compressed if `mapreduce.map.output.compress=true` |
| 7 | Data-Node Capacity (usable) | $C_{\text{usable}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - H_{\text{HDFS}})$ | $H_{\text{HDFS}}$ = Hadoop reserved fraction (default **10%**) |
| 8 | Speculative Execution Trigger | Latency threshold: $T_{\text{task}} > \overline{T_{\text{task}}} \times \alpha$ | $\alpha$ = speculative-slowtask threshold (default **1.0**) |
| 9 | Heartbeat Interval (YARN) | $\Delta t_{\text{HB}} = 3\ \text{s}$ (default) | NM → RM |
| 10 | Rack-Aware Replica Placement | 1 replica on local node, 1 on same rack, 1 on different rack | Optimization for write/read locality |

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Engineering

- **Capacity planning** in enterprise cloud clusters (used by Netflix, Yahoo, Facebook) depends on Equations 1 and 2.
- **Performance tuning** of a Hadoop job uses Equations 3–6 to balance mapper/reducer counts.
- **Speculative execution** (Eq. 8) prevents *straggler* nodes from bottlenecking a multi-hour job.

## 2.5 Hadoop Ecosystem — Adjacent Tools

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2-Mark Favourite:** *List any 5 components of the Hadoop ecosystem.*

- **Apache Pig** – High-level scripting language (Pig Latin) over MapReduce.
- **Apache Hive** – SQL-like queries (HQL) translated to MapReduce / Tez / Spark.
- **Apache HBase** – NoSQL columnar store on top of HDFS.
- **Apache Sqoop** – Bulk transfer between RDBMS and HDFS.
- **Apache Flume** – Streaming log ingestion into HDFS/HBase.
- **Apache Oozie** – Workflow scheduler for Hadoop jobs.
- **Apache ZooKeeper** – Coordination service for distributed applications.
- **Apache Spark** – In-memory, faster successor to MapReduce (often *paired* with Hadoop for storage but using its own engine).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of HDFS Blocks

**Problem Statement (KTU style):**
A 2 TB dataset is loaded into HDFS with a default block size of **128 MB** and a replication factor of **3**. Calculate (a) the number of logical blocks, and (b) the total physical storage consumed.

### Step 1 — Convert units to a common base
We must ensure the data size and block size are in identical units. Let us express both in **MB**.

$$
D = 2\ \text{TB} = 2 \times 1024\ \text{GB} = 2 \times 1024 \times 1024\ \text{MB} = 2{,}097{,}152\ \text{MB}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute logical block count
Apply Equation 2 *without* the replication factor to obtain logical blocks:

$$
B_{\text{logical}} = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B_s} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2{,}097{,}152}{128} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 16{,}384.0 \right\rceil
$$

$$
B_{\text{logical}} = 16{,}384\ \text{blocks}
$$

Since the division is exact, no partial block is required.

### Step 3 — Compute total physical storage (with replication)
Apply Equation 1:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = D \times R = 2{,}097{,}152\ \text{MB} \times 3 = 6{,}291{,}456\ \text{MB}
$$

Converting back to TB:

$$
S_{\text{total}} = \frac{6{,}291{,}456}{1024 \times 1024} = 6\ \text{TB}
$$

### Step 4 — Cross-check with full block-form
Equivalent calculation:

$$
B_{\text{physical}} = B_{\text{logical}} \times R = 16{,}384 \times 3 = 49{,}152\ \text{physical blocks}
$$

$$
S_{\text{verify}} = 49{,}152 \times 128\ \text{MB} = 6{,}291{,}456\ \text{MB} = 6\ \text{TB} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Result:** The 2 TB dataset expands to **6 TB** of physical storage under HDFS default settings, creating **49,152** physical block replicas across the cluster.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Usable Capacity per DataNode

**Problem Statement:**
A cluster has 10 DataNodes, each with a raw disk capacity of **2 TB**. Hadoop reserves **10%** of each disk for non-DFS use. The replication factor is **3**. Compute the *logical usable capacity* (i.e., the amount of original data the cluster can hold) and the *raw physical HDFS capacity*.

### Step 1 — Usable capacity per DataNode

$$
C_{\text{usable}} = C_{\text{raw}} \times (1 - H_{\text{HDFS}}) = 2\ \text{TB} \times (1 - 0.10) = 1.8\ \text{TB}
$$

### Step 2 — Raw HDFS capacity across cluster

$$
C_{\text{cluster,raw}} = N_{\text{DN}} \times C_{\text{usable}} = 10 \times 1.8\ \text{TB} = 18\ \text{TB}
$$

### Step 3 — Logical (original data) capacity

Since every byte of original data is replicated 3 times, the logical capacity is:

$$
C_{\text{logical}} = \frac{C_{\text{cluster,raw}}}{R} = \frac{18\ \text{TB}}{3} = 6\ \text{TB}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MapReduce Time Decomposition

For a job with $M$ mappers and $N_R$ reducers, the total wall-clock time is:

$$
T_{\text{MR}} = T_{\text{map}} + T_{\text{shuffle}} + T_{\text{reduce}}
$$

**Expand each term using the dominant cost:**

$$
T_{\text{map}} \approx \max_{i=1..M} \left( \frac{B_s}{r_{\text{read}}} + T_{\text{CPU,map}} \right)
$$

$$
T_{\text{shuffle}} \approx \frac{V_{\text{shuffle}}}{B_{\text{net}}} \times (1 + \eta_{\text{contention}})
$$

$$
T_{\text{reduce}} \approx \frac{1}{N_R} \sum_{j=1}^{N_R} \left( T_{\text{sort},j} + T_{\text{user-reduce},j} \right)
$$

where:
- $r_{\text{read}}$ = per-node disk read throughput (typically **100–200 MB/s** for spinning disk, **500+ MB/s** for SSD).
- $B_{\text{net}}$ = per-node network bandwidth (typically **1 Gbps or 10 Gbps**).
- $\eta_{\text{contention}}$ = network contention factor (commonly **0.2–0.4** for moderate loads).
- $T_{\text{CPU,map}}$, $T_{\text{user-reduce},j}$ = application-defined processing times.

> **Engineering insight:** The slowest mapper dictates $T_{\text{map}}$, so **speculative execution** (Equation 8) is critical. Network bandwidth (not CPU) typically dominates $T_{\text{shuffle}}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Hadoop vs Apache Spark vs Traditional RDBMS

| Parameter | Hadoop (MapReduce) | Apache Spark | Traditional RDBMS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Scale | **Petabytes** | Petabytes | Gigabytes–Terabytes |
| Processing Paradigm | Disk-based batch | **In-memory** batch + streaming | Row/column-oriented |
| Latency | Minutes–hours | Seconds–minutes | Milliseconds |
| Fault Tolerance | **Replication + Re-execution** | RDD lineage + checkpointing | ACID transactions |
| Cost Model | Commodity hardware | Memory-intensive (RAM) | High-end SAN storage |
| Best Use Case | Log archival, ETL | Iterative ML, streaming analytics | OLTP transactions |
| KTU Module | **Module 4 (core)** | Module 4 (comparative) | Pre-requisite (DBMS) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hadoop Core Architecture — Master/Slave Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    client1[Client Application]
    namenode1[NameNode Master - HDFS Metadata]
    snamenode1[Secondary NameNode - Checkpoint]
    resmgr1[ResourceManager Master - YARN]
    dn1[DataNode 1 - Blocks]
    dn2[DataNode 2 - Blocks]
    dn3[DataNode 3 - Blocks]
    dn4[DataNode 4 - Blocks]
    nm1[NodeManager 1]
    nm2[NodeManager 2]
    nm3[NodeManager 3]
    nm4[NodeManager 4]
    am1[ApplicationMaster per Job]
    c1[Container 1]
    c2[Container 2]
    c3[Container 3]
    c4[Container 4]

    client1 --> namenode1
    namenode1 -.fsimage checkpoint.-> snamenode1
    client1 --> resmgr1
    resmgr1 --> am1
    am1 --> nm1
    am1 --> nm2
    am1 --> nm3
    am1 --> nm4
    nm1 --> c1
    nm1 --> c2
    nm2 --> c3
    nm3 --> c4
    namenode1 --> dn1
    namenode1 --> dn2
    namenode1 --> dn3
    namenode1 --> dn4
    dn1 --- nm1
    dn2 --- nm2
    dn3 --- nm3
    dn4 --- nm4
```

## 4.2 MapReduce Data Flow — Block to Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input File HDFS - 512 MB] --> B[Input Split 1 - 128 MB]
    A --> C[Input Split 2 - 128 MB]
    A --> D[Input Split 3 - 128 MB]
    A --> E[Input Split 4 - 128 MB]

    B --> M1[Mapper 1]
    C --> M2[Mapper 2]
    D --> M3[Mapper 3]
    E --> M4[Mapper 4]

    M1 --> S1[Local Disk Intermediate]
    M2 --> S2[Local Disk Intermediate]
    M3 --> S3[Local Disk Intermediate]
    M4 --> S4[Local Disk Intermediate]

    S1 --> SH[Shuffle and Sort Phase by Key]
    S2 --> SH
    S3 --> SH
    S4 --> SH

    SH --> R1[Reducer 1 - Key Group A]
    SH --> R2[Reducer 2 - Key Group B]

    R1 --> O1[Output Part 1 HDFS]
    R2 --> O2[Output Part 2 HDFS]
```

## 4.3 Cloud Tools — Functional Categorization Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root1[Cloud Computing Tools]

    root1 --> cat1[Deployment and IaC]
    root1 --> cat2[Development Frameworks]
    root1 --> cat3[Container and Orchestration]
    root1 --> cat4[Monitoring and Observability]
    root1 --> cat5[Data and Analytics]

    cat1 --> t1[Terraform]
    cat1 --> t2[AWS CloudFormation]
    cat1 --> t3[Ansible]

    cat2 --> t4[Apache Hadoop]
    cat2 --> t5[Apache Spark]
    cat2 --> t6[MapReduce]

    cat3 --> t7[Docker]
    cat3 --> t8[Kubernetes]

    cat4 --> t9[Prometheus]
    cat4 --> t10[Grafana]
    cat4 --> t11[CloudWatch]

    cat5 --> t12[Apache Hive]
    cat5 --> t13[Apache Pig]
    cat5 --> t14[Apache HBase]
```

## 4.4 HDFS Write Path — Rack-Aware Replica Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    clientA[Client]
    nnA[NameNode]
    dnA1[DataNode Rack 1 - Node A - Local]
    dnA2[DataNode Rack 1 - Node B - Same Rack]
    dnA3[DataNode Rack 2 - Node C - Different Rack]

    clientA -->|1 Request Block Allocation| nnA
    nnA -->|2 Return Pipeline Nodes| clientA
    clientA -->|3 Write Packet| dnA1
    dnA1 -->|4 Forward Packet| dnA2
    dnA2 -->|5 Forward Packet| dnA3
    dnA3 -->|6 ACK| dnA2
    dnA2 -->|7 ACK| dnA1
    dnA1 -->|8 ACK| clientA
```

> **Engineering Note (from the diagram):** The placement follows the rule: 1 replica on the writer's local node, 1 on a different node of the same rack, and 1 on a different rack. This minimizes inter-rack traffic for the common read pattern.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the core components of Apache Hadoop.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Apache Hadoop is a distributed storage and processing framework consisting of **four core modules** (½ mark each for naming + ½ mark each for the function):
1. **Hadoop Common** – Provides the shared utilities and libraries (e.g., Java RPC, file-system abstractions) that support the other modules.
2. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** – A distributed, fault-tolerant file system that splits files into blocks (default 128 MB) and stores them across DataNodes with a default replication factor of 3.
3. **MapReduce** – A parallel programming model based on `map()` and `reduce()` functions used for batch processing of huge datasets.
4. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** – The cluster resource management layer that schedules jobs and allocates CPU/RAM containers across nodes.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between HDFS and traditional file systems in 6 points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — write any 6 differences):**

| # | HDFS | Traditional File System (NFS / ext4) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Designed for **write-once, read-many** workloads | Designed for **read-write** workloads |
| 2 | Splits files into **blocks (128 MB)** | Uses fixed-size sectors/clusters |
| 3 | Provides **replication** for fault tolerance (default $R = 3$) | Relies on RAID at the storage layer |
| 4 | **Streaming** access pattern | Random access pattern |
| 5 | Optimized for **high throughput**, not low latency | Optimized for **low latency** |
| 6 | Built for **commodity hardware** | Built for **high-end hardware** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Module 4 Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse **HDFS** with **MapReduce** — HDFS is *storage*, MapReduce is *processing*. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> - In numerical problems, students forget to multiply by the **replication factor** when computing physical storage. **Always re-read the question** for whether logical or physical storage is asked.
> - In the Write pipeline diagram, students forget the **rack-aware** placement rule (1 same-rack, 1 different-rack). Drawing only 3 DataNodes in one rack is a 1-mark deduction.
> - Do **not** write `YARN = MapReduce` — YARN is a *separate* resource manager. MapReduce runs *on top of* YARN.

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Hadoop architecture**. Discuss the role of the NameNode, DataNode, Secondary NameNode, ResourceManager, NodeManager, and ApplicationMaster. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** with a suitable example (e.g., Word Count). Derive the total number of physical blocks required for a 4 TB dataset when the HDFS block size is 256 MB and the replication factor is 3. Also compute the total physical storage consumed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**Hadoop Architecture (7 marks):**

Hadoop follows a **master–slave architecture** across two principal layers:

**1. HDFS Layer (Storage)**
- **NameNode (Master):** Runs on the master node and stores *metadata* (filename, block IDs, block locations, permissions). It does *not* store actual file data. (1 mark)
- **DataNode (Slave):** Runs on every worker node. It stores the **actual data blocks** and periodically sends *heartbeats* (default every 3 s) and *block reports* to the NameNode. (1 mark)
- **Secondary NameNode:** Does *not* act as a hot standby. It periodically merges the *edit log* (recent changes) with the *fsimage* (snapshot) to prevent log overflow and reduce recovery time. (1 mark)

**2. YARN + MapReduce Layer (Processing)**
- **ResourceManager (RM):** Cluster-level authority that allocates resources (CPU + RAM) to submitted applications. It contains the *Scheduler* and the *ApplicationsManager*. (1 mark)
- **NodeManager (NM):** Per-node agent that supervises *containers*, monitors resource usage, and reports to RM via heartbeats. (1 mark)
- **ApplicationMaster (AM):** Per-application component that negotiates resources with RM and coordinates task execution with the NMs. (1 mark)

**3. Co-location (1 mark):** For data locality, DataNodes and NodeManagers run on the **same physical node**, so compute is sent to the storage.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the master–slave diagram in Section 4.1.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**1. MapReduce Model — Word Count Example (3 marks):**

Consider the input text: `"hadoop cloud hadoop mapreduce cloud hadoop"`.

- **Map Phase:** Each mapper emits $\langle \text{word}, 1 \rangle$ for every word it sees.
  - Mapper 1 → `(hadoop,1), (cloud,1), (hadoop,1)`
  - Mapper 2 → `(mapreduce,1), (cloud,1), (hadoop,1)`
- **Shuffle & Sort:** Framework groups by key:
  - `(cloud, [1,1]), (hadoop, [1,1,1]), (mapreduce, [1])`
- **Reduce Phase:** Sum the values per key:
  - `(cloud, 2), (hadoop, 3), (mapreduce, 1)`

The user supplies only `map()` and `reduce()`; the framework handles splits, distribution, sorting, fault tolerance, and re-execution.

**2. Numerical Derivation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 4\ \text{TB}$, $B_s = 256\ \text{MB}$, $R = 3$.

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$
D = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024\ \text{MB} = 4{,}194{,}304\ \text{MB}
$$

**Step 2 — Logical block count:**

$$
B_{\text{logical}} = \left\lceil \frac{4{,}194{,}304}{256} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 16{,}384 \right\rceil = 16{,}384\ \text{blocks}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical block count (with replication):**

$$
B_{\text{physical}} = 16{,}384 \times 3 = 49{,}152\ \text{physical blocks}
$$

**Step 4 — Total physical storage:**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 4\ \text{TB} \times 3 = 12\ \text{TB}
$$

**Final Answer:** **49,152 physical blocks**, consuming **12 TB** of physical storage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **HDFS file-read** and **file-write** mechanisms with suitable diagrams. How does HDFS achieve **fault tolerance** through replication? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Hadoop ecosystem** by briefly explaining any **five** tools: Apache Pig, Apache Hive, Apache HBase, Apache Sqoop, and Apache Flume. Show how these tools interact with HDFS. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q (a)

**1. HDFS Read Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. Client contacts the **NameNode** to fetch the metadata (list of blocks + DataNode locations) for the requested file.
2. NameNode returns the ordered list of DataNodes holding the blocks.
3. Client then **directly reads the blocks** from the *nearest* DataNode (network-distance aware), bypassing the NameNode for data transfer.
4. If a block is corrupt or a DataNode is down, the client transparently falls back to another replica.

**2. HDFS Write Mechanism (3 marks):**
1. Client calls `create()` on the NameNode, which allocates a new file entry in its namespace (no blocks yet).
2. NameNode returns the DataNode list. The client splits the file into packets and pushes them to the *first* DataNode in the pipeline.
3. DataNodes form a **write pipeline** (rack-aware: 1 local, 1 same-rack, 1 different-rack). Each DataNode forwards the packet to the next and persists the block locally.
4. After all replicas acknowledge, the client sends a final `complete()` to the NameNode, which commits the file.

**3. Fault Tolerance via Replication (2 marks):**
- Default replication factor **R = 3** ensures that the loss of up to **2 DataNodes** holding a block does not cause data loss.
- The NameNode continuously monitors block reports. Under-replicated blocks are re-replicated to healthy nodes by the NameNode scheduler.
- HDFS also stores **checksums** per block; corrupted replicas are detected and replaced.

---

#### Model Solution — Q (b)

**1. Apache Pig (1.5 marks):** A high-level scripting platform that uses **Pig Latin** to express data-flow transformations. Scripts are compiled into MapReduce / Tez jobs and run on HDFS.

**2. Apache Hive (1.5 marks):** A data-warehouse system that provides **HQL (HiveQL)** — an SQL-like query language. Hive translates queries into MapReduce/Tez/Spark jobs and stores data in HDFS in formats like ORC / Parquet.

**3. Apache HBase (1.5 marks):** A distributed, **NoSQL columnar database** built on HDFS. It provides real-time, random read/write access to large tables, modelled after Google BigTable.

**4. Apache Sqoop (1 mark):** A bulk data-transfer tool that imports data from **RDBMS** (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL) into HDFS and vice versa, using MapReduce under the hood.

**5. Apache Flume (1.5 marks):** A distributed, reliable service for **streaming log/event collection**. Sources (e.g., web servers) → Channels → Sinks (HDFS / HBase). Used for ingesting click-streams, sensor data, etc.

> All five tools use HDFS as their **underlying storage layer**, and three of them (Pig, Hive, Sqoop) leverage **MapReduce** as their processing engine.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (must know before exam):**

- ✅ Hadoop = **HDFS (storage) + YARN (resource manager) + MapReduce (processing)**.
- ✅ Default HDFS block size = **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x); replication factor **R = 3**.
- ✅ **NameNode** stores *metadata only*; **DataNodes** store actual data blocks.
- ✅ **Secondary NameNode ≠ standby NameNode** — it only performs checkpointing.
- ✅ MapReduce = `map()` → *shuffle & sort* → `reduce()`. The framework, not the user, handles distribution, sorting, and fault tolerance.
- ✅ **Rack-aware replica placement:** 1 local node, 1 same rack, 1 different rack.
- ✅ **Heartbeat interval** between DataNode ↔ NameNode and NM ↔ RM = **3 seconds**.
- ✅ **Speculative execution** re-launches slow tasks on faster nodes to mitigate *stragglers*.
- ✅ Hadoop ecosystem tools: **Pig** (scripting), **Hive** (SQL), **HBase** (NoSQL), **Sqoop** (RDBMS import), **Flume** (log streaming), **Oozie** (workflow), **ZooKeeper** (coordination), **Spark** (in-memory).
- ✅ Hadoop is best for **batch + large-scale, low-latency-tolerant** workloads; not suitable for real-time OLTP.
- ✅ YARN separates **resource management** from **application logic**, enabling Spark, Tez, etc. to coexist with MapReduce.
- ✅ For numerical problems, always re-check whether the answer expects **logical** or **physical** storage.
- ✅ Cloud tools categories (KTU-favourite 3-mark question): **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS** + supporting tools (monitoring, IaC, orchestration).
- ✅ Apache Hadoop is **open-source** under the Apache License 2.0 and is maintained by the **Apache Software Foundation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** When asked "Explain Hadoop architecture", always draw the **two-layer master–slave model** (HDFS layer + YARN/MR layer), label the master and slave components distinctly, and mention *heartbeats* and *replication*. A clear, well-labeled diagram is worth at least **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cloud Applications - Moving Applications to the Cloud, Microsoft Cloud Services, Google Cloud Applications, Amazon Cloud Services
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST722 - CLOUD COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cloud Applications - Moving Applications to the Cloud, Microsoft Cloud Services, Google Cloud Applications, Amazon Cloud Services

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cloud Applications — Formal Definition

A **Cloud Application** is a software program whose logic, data, and processing components are distributed across networked computing resources (typically public, private, or hybrid cloud infrastructure) and accessed by clients over the internet using standard protocols. Unlike traditional monolithic on-premise software, cloud applications are designed to exploit **elastic provisioning**, **multi-tenancy**, **measured service**, and **service-level abstraction** as defined by the NIST Cloud Computing Reference Architecture.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (OECST722 — Module 4), the term "Cloud Applications" is treated as an *umbrella concept* that encompasses:
1. The **migration methodology** used to transition legacy on-premise applications to a cloud target.
2. The **service portfolios** offered by the three dominant hyperscale cloud providers — **Microsoft Azure**, **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)**, and **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** — that host these applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> Students must be able to: (i) describe the rationale and techniques for moving applications to the cloud, (ii) identify and compare the major service offerings of Azure, GCP, and AWS, and (iii) map business workloads to appropriate cloud service categories (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, FaaS).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Apartment vs. The House"

Imagine you currently **own a house** (your on-premise data center): you must buy the land, pour the foundation, install plumbing, fix the roof when it leaks, and pay property tax even when the house is empty. If guests visit, you have no spare rooms and they must sleep on the floor. To host 500 guests, you need 500 rooms, *built in advance*.

Now imagine you **rent apartments in a managed complex** (the cloud):
- You move in only when you need space.
- The complex maintains the elevator, security, water, and parking.
- You can rent a 1BHK today, a 3BHK tomorrow, and release it the day after — paying only for what you used.
- The complex already has thousands of apartments; your "neighbors" are other companies sharing infrastructure (multi-tenancy), but each apartment is private and metered.

**Cloud Applications** are the *furniture, appliances, and lifestyle* you arrange inside those apartments. Moving them from the old house to the new one is what we call **Application Migration**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning in Cloud Context |
|---|---|
| **Workload** | A discrete set of application code, configuration, and data that performs a business function. |
| **Lift & Shift (Rehost)** | Migrating an application to the cloud *as-is*, without redesign. |
| **Refactor** | Re-architecting the application to natively use cloud features (e.g., managed databases, auto-scaling). |
| **Multi-tenancy** | A single software instance serving multiple customer organizations (tenants) with logical isolation. |
| **Hyperscaler** | A cloud provider operating at global, petabyte-scale (Azure, GCP, AWS). |
| **Hyperscale Region** | A geographic area containing multiple Availability Zones (AZs). |
| **SLAs (Service Level Agreements)** | Contractual uptime/performance guarantees, typically expressed as monthly percentage (e.g., **99.99%**). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Cloud-Native Application (CNCF)**
> A cloud-native application is built (or refactored) using microservices, containers, immutable infrastructure, and declarative APIs, enabling automated scaling, healing, and rapid deployment. Examples: containerized APIs on Kubernetes, serverless functions triggered by events.
> 

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why Move Applications to the Cloud? — The Seven Business Drivers

The decision to migrate is rooted in measurable engineering and economic benefits. The **Seven Rs of Cloud Migration** (formalized by Gartner and widely taught in KTU) are:

1. **Rehost (Lift & Shift)** — Move VMs directly to IaaS (e.g., EC2, Azure VMs). Fastest, lowest risk.
2. **Replatform** — Minor optimizations (e.g., swap SQL Server → Azure SQL Managed Instance).
3. **Refactor / Re-architect** — Re-code to use PaaS/serverless. Highest long-term ROI.
4. **Repurchase** — Drop the custom build, buy a SaaS product (e.g., replace homegrown CRM with Salesforce).
5. **Retire** — Decommission unused applications during the migration wave.
6. **Retain** — Keep some apps on-premise due to compliance, latency, or cost (hybrid model).
7. **Relocate** — Move entire physical/virtual infrastructure to a compatible cloud (e.g., Hyper-V to Azure).

## 2.2 The Cloud Application Migration Lifecycle (High-Yield for KTU)

The migration is not a single event; it follows a structured lifecycle:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Migration Lifecycle Stages (CAF — Cloud Adoption Framework)**
> 1. **Strategy** — Define business justification, ROI, and risk posture.
> 2. **Plan** — Inventory workloads (Discovery), assess dependencies, prioritize.
> 3. **Ready** — Build landing zone (network, identity, security baseline).
> 4. **Adopt (Migrate/Innovate)** — Execute the chosen R per workload.
> 5. **Govern** — Enforce policy, cost controls, compliance.
> 6. **Manage** — Operate, monitor (SLA, observability), and continuously improve.

## 2.5 Mathematical & Engineering Foundation (Migration Cost Math)

Cloud migration decisions are supported by **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** analysis. The governing comparison is:

$$TCO_{cloud} \;=\; \sum_{t=1}^{N} \frac{C_{compute}(t) + C_{storage}(t) + C_{network}(t) + C_{ops}(t)}{(1+r)^{t}}$$

where:
- $t$ = time period (month/year)
- $N$ = analysis horizon
- $r$ = discount rate
- $C_{compute}(t)$ = on-demand or reserved compute cost
- $C_{storage}(t)$ = storage cost (GB-month basis)
- $C_{network}(t)$ = egress + inter-AZ traffic
- $C_{ops}(t)$ = operational labor + managed-service fees

The decision rule is:

$$\text{Migrate} \iff TCO_{cloud} + \text{Risk Cost}_{on\text{-}prem} \;<\; TCO_{on\text{-}prem}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> Hyperscalers offer **Pricing Calculators** (Azure Pricing Calculator, AWS Pricing Calculator, GCP Pricing Calculator) that implement exactly this equation. Engineers parameterize workload, region, and utilization to project 1-, 3-, and 5-year TCO against current on-premise spend.

## 2.6 SLA — The Mathematical Contract

Each cloud service publishes an SLA (e.g., EC2 single-AZ = **99.95%**, Azure Cosmos DB multi-region = **99.999%**). The implied annual downtime is:

$$\text{Max Annual Downtime} \;=\; (1 - SLA) \times 365 \times 24 \times 60 \;\; \text{minutes}$$

**Example (KTU-favorite):** If EC2 SLA = 99.95%, then

$$\text{Downtime} = (1 - 0.9995) \times 525{,}600 = 0.0005 \times 525{,}600 = 262.8 \;\text{minutes/year} \approx 4.38 \text{ hours/year.}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **SLA is NOT a guarantee** — it is a *service credit* if missed. Most SLAs require the customer to deploy across multiple AZs/regions to receive the higher-tier number.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — Migration Strategy & Service Mapping

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough #1: The "Right R" Selection Matrix

**Problem:** A KTU-style scenario: A retail company has (a) a 15-year-old COBOL inventory app with no source code, (b) a Java Spring Boot customer portal, (c) a custom-built HR system, and (d) an experimental Python ML model. Decide the most appropriate R for each and justify.

**Step 1 — Workload A: COBOL inventory app, no source.**

- The binary is unmaintainable; refactor is technically infeasible.
- The hardware is end-of-life. Rehosting preserves the binary.
- *Decision:* **Rehost (Lift & Shift)** to a COBOL-compatible Linux VM on Azure Migrate / AWS Application Migration Service (CloudEndure).
- *Justification:* Lowest risk, preserves the working binary, defers modernization to a future phase.

**Step 2 — Workload B: Java Spring Boot customer portal.**

- Code is maintainable; current Oracle DB license is expensive.
- *Decision:* **Replatform** — containerize with Docker, deploy to AKS / EKS / GKE, swap Oracle → Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL or Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
- *Justification:* Managed DB removes licensing + patching overhead; Kubernetes gives elastic scaling.

**Step 3 — Workload C: Custom-built HR system.**

- Off-the-shelf SaaS products (e.g., Workday, SAP SuccessFactors) cover the same business process.
- *Decision:* **Repurchase** — retire the custom build, adopt a SaaS HR suite.
- *Justification:* Lower TCO over 5 years; vendor handles compliance updates.

**Step 4 — Workload D: Python ML model.**

- Requires GPU burst capacity, not a steady-state workload.
- *Decision:* **Refactor** to a serverless architecture using Azure Functions + Azure ML / AWS Lambda + SageMaker inference endpoints.
- *Justification:* Pay-per-use model aligns with sporadic inference traffic; auto-scales to zero when idle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision rule used above:** Choose the R that minimizes *risk × cost* while maximizing *business agility* for that specific workload.

## 3.2 Analytical Walkthrough #2: Selecting the Right Hyperscaler

**Step 1 — Inventory the workload profile.**

$$\text{Workload Profile} = \{ \text{Compute Intensity}, \text{Data Gravity}, \text{Identity Stack}, \text{Compliance} \}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the bias rule:**

| If the organization has… | Then prefer… | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Heavy investment in **Active Directory, Windows Server, .NET, Office 365** | **Azure** | Native Entra ID federation, hybrid Azure AD Connect, Azure Arc for servers. |
| Heavy investment in **Linux, Python, TensorFlow, Big Data, Kubernetes** | **GCP** | Originating author of K8s, leading AI/ML tooling, Anthos for hybrid. |
| A **greenfield, cloud-native, DevOps-first** culture | **AWS** | Largest service catalog, mature IaC, broadest partner ecosystem. |
| Strict **data-sovereignty in EU/India** requirements | All three, but evaluate **regional coverage** | Check region availability maps before committing. |

**Step 3 — Validate with a Proof of Concept (PoC).** Migrate one non-critical workload, measure latency, cost, and team velocity, then expand.

## 3.3 Analytical Walkthrough #3: TCO Comparison (Worked Numerical)

A KTU numerical-style problem:

> An on-premise data center costs **₹18,00,000/year** to operate (power + cooling + hardware depreciation + 1 FTE). A cloud equivalent (Azure reserved instances, 3-year term) costs **₹4,50,000/year** compute + **₹1,20,000/year** storage + **₹60,000/year** network egress + **₹90,000/year** managed services. Discount rate **r = 8%**. Horizon **N = 3 years**. Is migration financially justified?

**Step 1 — Discount each year's on-prem cost.**

$$\text{PV}_{on-prem} = \sum_{t=1}^{3} \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{(1+0.08)^{t}}$$

$$= \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.08} + \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.1664} + \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{1.2597}$$

$$= 16{,}66{,}666.67 + 15{,}43{,}209.88 + 14{,}29{,}546.18 = 46{,}39{,}422.73 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 2 — Discount each year's cloud cost.**

$$\text{PV}_{cloud} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{1.08} + \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{1.1664} + \frac{7{,}20{,}000}{1.2597}$$

$$= 6{,}66{,}666.67 + 6{,}17{,}283.95 + 5{,}71{,}818.47 = 18{,}55{,}769.09 \text{ INR}$$

**Step 3 — Compute net savings.**

$$\text{Net Savings} = \text{PV}_{on-prem} - \text{PV}_{cloud} = 46{,}39{,}422.73 - 18{,}55{,}769.09 = 27{,}83{,}653.64 \text{ INR}$$

$$\text{Savings Ratio} = \frac{27{,}83{,}653.64}{46{,}39{,}422.73} \times 100\% \approx 60\%$$

**Step 4 — Decision.** Since Net Savings > 0, migration is financially justified under the stated assumptions. Sensitivity analysis should vary $r$ and $N$ to ensure the conclusion is robust.

## 3.4 Analytical Walkthrough #4: Mapping Business Need → Hyperscaler Service

A more conceptual KTU question:

> *"A startup needs: (i) globally distributed NoSQL data with millisecond reads, (ii) a serverless API endpoint that scales to zero, (iii) a managed Kubernetes cluster to run ML training jobs, and (iv) an S3-compatible object store. Recommend a single hyperscaler that satisfies all four requirements with its first-party services, and name the services."*

**Step-by-step mapping:**

1. **Globally distributed NoSQL** → **Amazon DynamoDB Global Tables** (multi-region active-active replication) *or* **Google Cloud Firestore** *or* **Azure Cosmos DB**. Pick **AWS DynamoDB** if S3 is the target.
2. **Serverless API scaling to zero** → **AWS Lambda** + **Amazon API Gateway** (HTTP API tier).
3. **Managed Kubernetes for ML** → **Amazon EKS** (or EKS Managed Node Groups with GPU instances).
4. **S3-compatible object store** → **Amazon S3** (the de-facto industry standard; many 3rd-party tools are S3-API-compatible).

**Final recommendation: AWS**, with services DynamoDB, Lambda + API Gateway, EKS, and S3.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-argument for academic depth:** If the startup is data/ML-first and already on Google Workspace, **GCP** is competitive: Firestore (multi-region), Cloud Functions, GKE (the K8s reference impl), and Cloud Storage (S3-compatible via Interop API).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
*List any three cloud service categories and give one example service for each from Microsoft Azure.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service)** — Azure Virtual Machines (provides on-demand VMs with chosen OS, CPU, RAM).
2. **PaaS (Platform as a Service)** — Azure App Service (hosts web apps and REST APIs without managing servers).
3. **SaaS (Software as a Service)** — Microsoft 365 (delivers Office, Teams, Exchange over the internet).

*(1 mark per correct pair; 3 marks total)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
*What is the difference between Rehost and Refactor in cloud migration? Give one use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Rehost (Lift & Shift):** Moving an application to a cloud VM with **no code changes**. *Use case:* Migrating a legacy COBOL inventory system to an Azure VM to retire aging on-premise hardware quickly. (1.5 marks)
- **Refactor:** Re-architecting the application to **natively use cloud services** (managed DB, serverless, containers). *Use case:* Converting a monolithic Java app into microservices on AKS with Cosmos DB. (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students routinely **lose 2–3 marks** by listing services without categorizing them (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS). Always tag the category.
> - In comparison questions, examiners expect a **table format** with at least 2–3 rows, not prose. Tables score higher.
> - Avoid writing "AWS is best" or "Azure is best" — questions ask for *justified comparison*. Show the deciding criteria (cost, ecosystem, hybrid support, AI strength).
> - When asked to "discuss" migration, **mention all 7 Rs** even if you elaborate on only 2–3. Missing Rs is a common 1-mark deduction.
> - Always state SLAs as **percentages** with the **implied downtime**, not vague phrases like "high availability."

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Migration Methodology + Microsoft Azure*

**(a)** Explain the **Seven Rs of Cloud Migration** with one example scenario for each R. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Seven Rs, formalized by Gartner and adopted by Microsoft/AWS as their standard migration taxonomy, are:

1. **Rehost (Lift & Shift):** Move the application *as-is* to a cloud VM. *Example:* A 10-year-old ASP.NET intranet app is moved from on-premise Windows Server 2012 to an Azure Windows VM to escape hardware refresh cycles. *(1 mark)*
2. **Replatform:** Make **minor cloud-targeted optimizations** without rewriting core code. *Example:* Migrate the same app's database from SQL Server 2016 on-premise to **Azure SQL Managed Instance** to gain automatic patching and backups. *(1 mark)*
3. **Refactor (Re-architect):** Re-code to use cloud-native services. *Example:* Decompose the monolith into microservices on **AKS**, swap the DB for **Cosmos DB** with multi-region writes. *(1 mark)*
4. **Repurchase:** Replace custom code with a SaaS product. *Example:* Retire the homegrown expense tracker and adopt **SAP Concur**. *(1 mark)*
5. **Retire:** Decommission apps that are unused. *Example:* A reporting tool that 3 users accessed once in 6 months is removed during migration assessment. *(1 mark)*
6. **Retain:** Keep some workloads on-premise. *Example:* A low-latency SCADA system controlling factory PLCs is retained on-premise due to millisecond response requirements. *(1 mark)*
7. **Relocate:** Move physical/virtual infrastructure to a compatible cloud with minimal change. *Example:* Hyper-V VMs are moved to **Azure Local** (formerly Azure Stack HCI) using Azure Migrate. *(1 mark)*

> The choice of R depends on business urgency, code availability, and ROI horizon.

---

**(b)** Describe the **Microsoft Azure service portfolio**. Categorize the following services into IaaS / PaaS / SaaS and state the primary use of each: **(7 marks)**
*(i) Azure Virtual Machines, (ii) Azure App Service, (iii) Azure Kubernetes Service, (iv) Azure Functions, (v) Azure Cosmos DB, (vi) Microsoft 365, (vii) Azure Blob Storage.*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Service | Category | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Azure Virtual Machines | **IaaS** | On-demand Windows/Linux VMs for running any workload. |
| (ii) | Azure App Service | **PaaS** | Host web apps, REST APIs, and mobile back-ends without managing servers. |
| (iii) | Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) | **PaaS** | Managed Kubernetes for container orchestration. |
| (iv) | Azure Functions | **FaaS / Serverless PaaS** | Event-driven code execution billed per invocation. |
| (v) | Azure Cosmos DB | **PaaS** | Globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL database with millisecond latency. |
| (vi) | Microsoft 365 | **SaaS** | Cloud-delivered Office apps, Teams, Exchange Online. |
| (vii) | Azure Blob Storage | **IaaS** | Massively scalable object storage for unstructured data. |

*(1 mark for correct category + use across all 7 = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Comparison: Google Cloud vs. Amazon Web Services*

**(a)** Compare **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** and **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** with respect to their **core strengths, flagship compute services, and AI/ML offerings**. Use a comparison table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Dimension | **Google Cloud Platform (GCP)** | **Amazon Web Services (AWS)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Strength** | Data analytics, AI/ML, Kubernetes (GKE is the upstream K8s reference); strong in TensorFlow and big data (BigQuery). | Largest service catalog, IaaS pioneer, mature ecosystem, broadest partner network. |
| **Flagship Compute (IaaS)** | **Compute Engine** — VMs with live migration, custom machine types. | **Amazon EC2** — broadest instance variety (Graviton, GPU, FPGA, Trainium). |
| **Flagship Container Service** | **Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE)** — the original managed K8s. | **Amazon EKS** — managed K8s; plus ECS for AWS-native containers. |
| **Flagship Serverless** | **Cloud Run** (containers to zero) and **Cloud Functions**. | **AWS Lambda** with **Fargate** for serverless containers. |
| **Flagship Object Storage** | **Cloud Storage** (multi-regional, Nearline, Coldline, Archive classes). | **Amazon S3** — the de-facto industry standard; 11 nines of durability. |
| **Flagship Database** | **Cloud Spanner** — globally distributed, strongly consistent SQL. | **Amazon Aurora** (MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible) and **DynamoDB** (NoSQL). |
| **AI/ML Flagship** | **Vertex AI** unified platform; **Gemini API** for generative AI. | **SageMaker** for ML; **Bedrock** for foundation models (Anthropic, Llama, Titan). |
| **Networking Edge** | Premium-tier global network (private fiber). | CloudFront CDN + AWS Global Accelerator. |
| **Typical Customer** | Data-first startups, ML researchers, media analytics. | Cloud-native enterprises, DevOps-first organizations, regulated industries. |

*(1 mark per correct, justified row; 7 rows = 7 marks)*

---

**(b)** A startup wants to build a **serverless data pipeline** that ingests IoT sensor data, stores raw events in object storage, processes them via event-driven functions, and loads aggregates into a managed NoSQL database. **(7 marks)**
*(i) Recommend the **AWS** service stack for this pipeline, naming one service for each stage. (ii) Justify why AWS is suitable over on-premise for this use case.*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) AWS Service Stack (3.5 marks):**

| Pipeline Stage | AWS Service | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **IoT Ingestion** | **AWS IoT Core** | MQTT/HTTPS gateway; millions of devices. |
| **Object Storage (raw events)** | **Amazon S3** | Durable (11 9s), cheap raw lake. |
| **Event Bus** | **Amazon EventBridge** | Decouples producers from consumers; routes IoT events. |
| **Serverless Compute** | **AWS Lambda** | Processes events on demand, auto-scales, scales to zero. |
| **Managed NoSQL** | **Amazon DynamoDB** | Single-digit millisecond reads/writes for aggregates. |
| **Monitoring** | **Amazon CloudWatch** | Logs, metrics, alarms. |

**(ii) Justification — Why AWS over on-premise (3.5 marks):**

1. **Elasticity without over-provisioning:** IoT traffic is bursty. AWS Lambda + DynamoDB on-demand mode scales instantly and charges per request, eliminating idle capacity cost. On-premise would require sizing for peak load 24×7. *(1 mark)*
2. **Global reach and low latency:** AWS has 30+ regions and 400+ edge locations; IoT devices worldwide get a low-latency endpoint. On-premise cannot match this geographically. *(1 mark)*
3. **Managed services reduce operational toil:** DynamoDB, S3, and Lambda patch themselves. A 3-person startup can run a globally distributed pipeline without a large ops team. *(1 mark)*
4. **Pay-per-use aligns with startup cash flow:** Capital expenditure (CapEx) on hardware is replaced with operating expenditure (OpEx) tied to actual usage, preserving runway. *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap — Print & Pin**
> - **Cloud Application** = software hosted on cloud infrastructure, accessed over the internet, exploiting elasticity and multi-tenancy.
> - **Seven Rs of Migration:** Rehost, Replatform, Refactor, Repurchase, Retire, Retain, Relocate.
> - **Migration Lifecycle (CAF):** Strategy → Plan → Ready → Adopt → Govern → Manage.
> - **Azure Identity:** Microsoft **Entra ID** (formerly Azure AD) is the central identity plane.
> - **Azure Flagships:** VMs, App Service, AKS, Functions, Cosmos DB, Blob Storage, Microsoft 365.
> - **GCP Origin Strengths:** Data analytics (BigQuery), AI/ML (Vertex AI, Gemini), Kubernetes (GKE is upstream).
> - **GCP Flagships:** Compute Engine, GKE, Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, Cloud Storage, Cloud Spanner, Vertex AI.
> - **AWS Origin Strengths:** Service breadth, IaaS pioneer, mature DevOps tooling.
> - **AWS Flagships:** EC2, S3, Lambda, EKS, DynamoDB, Aurora, SageMaker, Bedrock.
> - **Serverless = scale to zero, pay per invocation** (Lambda / Functions / Cloud Run).
> - **SLAs are contractual credits, not guarantees**; multi-AZ deployment unlocks higher numbers.
> - **TCO equation** is the formal basis for migration justification: $\text{TCO} = \sum \frac{C_{total}}{(1+r)^t}$.
> - **Comparison answers in KTU:** Always use a **table**, always state the **decision criterion** (cost, hybrid, AI, ecosystem).
> - **Common pitfall:** Forgetting to *tag services as IaaS / PaaS / SaaS* in descriptive answers — direct 1–2 mark penalty.
> - **Hybrid hallmark:** Azure Arc / Azure Stack; AWS Outposts; Google Anthos — these extend cloud to on-premise.
> - **Data sovereignty:** Always check hyperscaler **region availability** before committing; some compliance frameworks (e.g., GDPR, DPDP Act) require data to remain in-country.
> - **For KTU numericals:** Annual downtime $= (1 - SLA) \times 525{,}600$ minutes; remember the factor for leap years if the question is over a long horizon.
> - **For diagrams:** Always show a **client → edge → identity → compute → data** layered architecture for full marks on 7–10 mark questions.
